guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: A reasonable A1C goal for many nonpregnant adults is <7% (53 mmol/mol). Providers might reasonably suggest more stringent A1C goals (such as <6.5%) for selected individual patients if this can be achieved without significant hypoglycemia or other adverse effects. Less stringent A1C goals (such as <8%) may be appropriate for patients with a history of severe hypoglycemia, limited life expectancy, advanced complications, or extensive comorbidities.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for type 2 diabetes. If A1C is above target after approximately 3 months of metformin monotherapy, consider combination therapy with an additional glucose-lowering agent based on patient factors including atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and need to minimize hypoglycemia or weight gain. GLP-1 receptor agonists or SGLT2 inhibitors with proven cardiovascular benefit are recommended for patients with established ASCVD, heart failure, or CKD.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients with diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, high-intensity statin therapy should be added to lifestyle therapy. For patients aged 40-75 with diabetes without ASCVD, moderate-intensity statin therapy is recommended. Aspirin therapy (75-162 mg/day) is reasonable for adults with diabetes at increased cardiovascular risk. Blood pressure should be measured at every routine visit and treated to a target of <130/80 mmHg if it can be safely attained.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: At least once a year assess urinary albumin (e.g., spot UACR) and eGFR in patients with type 1 diabetes with duration >=5 years and in all patients with type 2 diabetes. An ACE inhibitor or ARB is recommended for patients with diabetes and albuminuria (UACR >=30 mg/g). For patients with type 2 diabetes, CKD, and eGFR >=20, an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended to reduce CKD progression and cardiovascular events. A nonsteroidal MRA (finerenone) is recommended for patients with CKD at risk of progression.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Glucose (approximately 15-20 g) is the preferred treatment for conscious individuals with blood glucose <70 mg/dL, although any form of carbohydrate that contains glucose may be used. Hypoglycemia unawareness or one or more episodes of level 3 hypoglycemia should trigger re-evaluation of the treatment regimen. Clinically significant hypoglycemia is defined as glucose <54 mg/dL. Insulin or sulfonylurea doses should be reduced in patients with recurrent hypoglycemia.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Perform the A1C test at least two times a year in patients meeting treatment goals. Perform the A1C test quarterly in patients whose therapy has changed or who are not meeting glycemic goals. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) should be offered to adults with diabetes on multiple daily injections or insulin pump therapy. Self-monitoring of blood glucose is recommended for patients on insulin therapy to guide treatment decisions.

Normal blood pressure is defined as <120/<80 mmHg. Elevated blood pressure is defined as systolic 120-129 and diastolic <80 mmHg. Stage 1 hypertension is systolic 130-139 or diastolic 80-89 mmHg. Stage 2 hypertension is systolic >=140 or diastolic >=90 mmHg. Hypertensive crisis is systolic >180 and/or diastolic >120 mmHg requiring immediate medical attention.

For adults with confirmed hypertension and known CVD or 10-year ASCVD risk >=10%, a BP target of <130/80 mmHg is recommended. For adults with confirmed hypertension without additional markers of increased CVD risk, a BP target of <130/80 mmHg may be reasonable. Use of BP-lowering medications is recommended for stage 1 hypertension (BP 130-139/80-89) with clinical CVD or ASCVD risk >=10%. Nonpharmacologic interventions are recommended for elevated BP and stage 1 hypertension with ASCVD risk <10%.

First-line agents for the treatment of hypertension include thiazide diuretics, calcium channel blockers (CCBs), and ACE inhibitors or ARBs. Initial first-line therapy with two antihypertensive agents of different classes is recommended for adults with stage 2 hypertension and an average BP >20/10 mmHg above their target. ACE inhibitors and ARBs should not be used in combination. For Black adults without heart failure or CKD, initial therapy should include a thiazide diuretic or CCB.

In adults with CKD, treatment with an ACE inhibitor or ARB is reasonable to slow kidney disease progression. In adults with diabetes and hypertension, all first-line classes of antihypertensives are useful. In adults with stable ischemic heart disease, beta-blockers and/or ACE inhibitors or ARBs are recommended. In adults with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, ACE inhibitors or ARBs (or ARNI) along with evidence-based beta-blockers and aldosterone antagonists are recommended.

Adults initiating a new or adjusted drug regimen for hypertension should have a follow-up evaluation of adherence and response within 1 month. For adults with hypertension who have achieved BP target, follow-up at 3-6 month intervals is reasonable. Out-of-office BP monitoring with ambulatory or home BP devices is recommended for confirmation of hypertension diagnosis and titration of medications. Potassium and creatinine should be monitored within 2-4 weeks of initiating or adjusting an ACE inhibitor, ARB, or diuretic.

Heart failure is classified by left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF): HFrEF (LVEF <=40%), HFmrEF (LVEF 41-49%), and HFpEF (LVEF >=50%). Stages of HF: Stage A (at risk), Stage B (pre-HF with structural changes but no symptoms), Stage C (symptomatic HF), Stage D (advanced HF). NYHA Functional Classification should be used to describe symptom severity: Class I (no limitation), Class II (slight limitation), Class III (marked limitation), Class IV (unable to carry on any physical activity).

For patients with HFrEF (LVEF <=40%), the following medications are recommended: (1) ACE inhibitor, ARB, or ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan preferred) to reduce morbidity and mortality; (2) Evidence-based beta-blocker (carvedilol, bisoprolol, or metoprolol succinate) to reduce morbidity and mortality; (3) Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) such as spironolactone or eplerenone for patients with NYHA class II-IV; (4) SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) to reduce HF hospitalizations and cardiovascular mortality. All four pillars should be initiated and titrated to target doses.

Serial assessment of volume status including body weight, orthostatic BP, and assessment of fluid retention is recommended at every visit. BNP or NT-proBNP levels can be useful for establishing prognosis and disease severity and may guide management. Serum electrolytes and renal function should be monitored during initiation and titration of GDMT, especially with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, MRAs, and diuretics. Follow-up within 7-14 days after hospital discharge for HF is recommended.

An ICD is recommended for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death in patients with symptomatic HF (NYHA class II-III) and LVEF <=35% despite >=3 months of optimal medical therapy, provided survival with good functional status is expected for >1 year. CRT is recommended for patients with HFrEF (LVEF <=35%), sinus rhythm, LBBB with QRS duration >=150 ms, and NYHA class II-IV symptoms despite GDMT. CRT may be considered in patients with LVEF <=35%, sinus rhythm, non-LBBB with QRS >=150 ms.

Diuretics are recommended in patients with HF who have signs and symptoms of fluid overload to improve symptoms. Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide) are generally preferred over thiazide diuretics for managing volume overload in HF. Thiazide diuretics may be added for diuretic resistance. Electrolytes and renal function should be monitored closely during diuretic therapy. The lowest effective dose should be used to maintain euvolemia.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For overweight or obese patients with type 2 diabetes, lifestyle modifications to achieve and maintain a minimum of 5% weight loss are recommended. Greater weight loss (>10%) generally confers greater benefits including disease-modifying effects. Medications with weight loss potential (GLP-1 RAs, dual GIP/GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2 inhibitors) should be preferentially considered in overweight/obese patients. Metabolic surgery should be recommended for adults with type 2 diabetes and BMI >=40 (>=37.5 in Asian Americans) or BMI 35.0-39.9 (32.5-37.4 in Asian Americans) who do not achieve durable weight loss with nonsurgical methods.

guidelines.section_title: Management of Comorbidities in Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: Atrial fibrillation should be managed with rate or rhythm control in patients with HF; anticoagulation is recommended based on CHA2DS2-VASc score. In patients with HF and iron deficiency (ferritin <100 or ferritin 100-299 with transferrin saturation <20%), IV iron replacement is recommended to improve functional status and quality of life. Diabetes management in HF patients should include SGLT2 inhibitors as a preferred glucose-lowering agent. Sleep-disordered breathing should be assessed and treated in patients with HF.

High-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40-80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg) is recommended for patients aged 20-75 with clinical ASCVD. Moderate-intensity statin therapy is recommended for adults aged 40-75 with LDL-C >=70 mg/dL and a 10-year ASCVD risk >=7.5%. For primary prevention in adults aged 40-75 with LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL and 10-year ASCVD risk 5-<7.5%, statin therapy may be considered with risk-enhancing factors. Maximally tolerated statin therapy should be used before adding nonstatin agents.

For patients with clinical ASCVD at very high risk, an LDL-C threshold of 70 mg/dL is used to consider adding nonstatin therapy. In very high-risk ASCVD patients on maximally tolerated statin with LDL-C >=70 mg/dL, adding ezetimibe is reasonable. If LDL-C remains >=70 mg/dL on statin plus ezetimibe, adding a PCSK9 inhibitor is reasonable. Risk-enhancing factors include family history of premature ASCVD, persistently elevated LDL-C >=160 mg/dL, metabolic syndrome, CKD, chronic inflammatory conditions, history of preeclampsia, South Asian ancestry, and elevated lipoprotein(a) >=50 mg/dL.

In adults at borderline risk (5-<7.5% 10-year ASCVD risk), the presence of risk-enhancing factors favors initiation of moderate-intensity statin therapy. Coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring may be considered to refine risk when the decision about statin therapy is uncertain. CAC score of 0 favors deferring statin therapy except in cigarette smokers, those with diabetes, or strong family history. CAC >=100 or >=75th percentile favors statin therapy. A clinician-patient risk discussion incorporating risk-enhancing factors and CAC score when available is recommended before starting statin therapy for primary prevention.

Obtain a fasting lipid panel at baseline before starting statin therapy. Obtain a fasting lipid panel 4-12 weeks after initiating statin therapy or dose adjustment to assess adherence and therapeutic response, then every 3-12 months. If anticipated therapeutic response is not achieved, assess adherence and secondary causes before intensifying therapy. Monitor for statin-associated side effects including myalgias. Hepatic transaminases should be measured at baseline and as clinically indicated.

guidelines.section_title: Anticoagulation for Stroke Prevention in AF
guidelines.recommendation_text: Oral anticoagulation is recommended for patients with AF and a CHA2DS2-VASc score >=2 in males or >=3 in females. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) are recommended over warfarin in eligible patients with AF, except those with moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves. For patients not candidates for anticoagulation, left atrial appendage occlusion may be considered. CHA2DS2-VASc scoring: congestive HF (1), hypertension (1), age >=75 (2), diabetes (1), stroke/TIA (2), vascular disease (1), age 65-74 (1), sex category female (1).

guidelines.section_title: Rate vs Rhythm Control in AF
guidelines.recommendation_text: Rate control is a reasonable initial strategy for most patients with AF. A resting heart rate target of <110 bpm using a lenient rate control strategy is reasonable for asymptomatic patients with preserved LVEF. Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil) are first-line agents for rate control. Rhythm control with antiarrhythmic drugs or catheter ablation is recommended for patients with symptomatic AF despite adequate rate control. Early rhythm control (within 1 year of diagnosis) improves outcomes in patients with AF and cardiovascular risk factors.

Catheter ablation of AF is recommended for symptomatic paroxysmal AF refractory or intolerant to at least one class I or III antiarrhythmic drug when a rhythm control strategy is desired. Catheter ablation may be considered as first-line rhythm control therapy in selected patients with symptomatic paroxysmal AF. In patients with HFrEF and AF, catheter ablation is reasonable to improve symptoms, quality of life, and potentially reduce mortality and HF hospitalizations. Pulmonary vein isolation is the cornerstone of AF ablation procedures.

In patients with AF and CKD (CrCl 15-29 mL/min), apixaban or reduced-dose rivaroxaban may be considered; DOACs should be avoided when CrCl <15 mL/min without dialysis. In patients with AF and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, anticoagulation is recommended regardless of CHA2DS2-VASc score. Perioperative management of anticoagulation should balance bleeding risk against stroke risk; bridging with heparin is generally not recommended for DOACs. In patients with AF and acute coronary syndromes, the duration of triple therapy (DOAC + dual antiplatelet) should be minimized.

Asthma diagnosis is based on a history of variable respiratory symptoms (wheeze, shortness of breath, chest tightness, cough) AND confirmed variable expiratory airflow limitation (bronchodilator reversibility >=12% and >200 mL, positive bronchial challenge, or excessive variability in peak flow). Assess symptom control using validated tools (ACT score or GINA symptom assessment). Well-controlled asthma: daytime symptoms <=2/week, no night symptoms, rescue inhaler <=2/week, no activity limitation. Assess risk factors for exacerbations, fixed airflow limitation, and medication side effects.

Step 1: As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol (preferred) for mild asthma. Step 2: Daily low-dose ICS plus as-needed SABA, or as-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol. Step 3: Low-dose ICS-LABA maintenance (e.g., budesonide-formoterol, fluticasone-salmeterol). Step 4: Medium-dose ICS-LABA; consider add-on tiotropium or LTRA. Step 5: High-dose ICS-LABA; refer for phenotypic assessment and consider add-on biologic therapy (anti-IgE, anti-IL5/5R, anti-IL4R, anti-TSLP). SABA-only treatment without ICS is no longer recommended due to increased risk of severe exacerbations.

Mild-moderate exacerbation: Increase reliever use (ICS-formoterol as needed up to 72 mcg/day formoterol), or increase controller dose. Severe exacerbation: Add systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40-50mg/day for 5-7 days in adults). In emergency settings: Administer oxygen to target SpO2 93-95%, repeated doses of SABA via spacer or nebulizer, early administration of systemic corticosteroids. Add ipratropium bromide for severe exacerbations not responding to initial treatment. Assess response at 1-2 hours to determine disposition.

Biologic therapy should be considered for patients with severe asthma uncontrolled despite adherence to optimized Step 4-5 therapy with correct inhaler technique. Phenotype assessment is essential: Type 2 inflammation is suggested by blood eosinophils >=150 cells/mcL, FeNO >=20 ppb, or allergen-driven asthma. Anti-IgE (omalizumab) for allergic asthma with elevated IgE; anti-IL5/5R (mepolizumab, benralizumab) for eosinophilic asthma; anti-IL4R (dupilumab) for eosinophilic or Type 2 asthma; anti-TSLP (tezepelumab) can be considered regardless of phenotype. Reassess response at 4 months.

COPD should be considered in any patient with dyspnea, chronic cough, or sputum production and/or a history of exposure to risk factors (smoking, biomass fuel). Diagnosis requires spirometry showing post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70. Severity classification by airflow limitation: GOLD 1 (mild, FEV1 >=80% predicted), GOLD 2 (moderate, 50-79%), GOLD 3 (severe, 30-49%), GOLD 4 (very severe, <30%). ABE assessment groups guide treatment: Group A (few symptoms, low exacerbation risk), Group B (more symptoms, low exacerbation risk), Group E (exacerbation risk, >=2 moderate or >=1 hospitalized exacerbation/year).

Group A: A short- or long-acting bronchodilator (SABA, SAMA, LABA, or LAMA). Group B: LABA + LAMA combination is preferred; a single long-acting bronchodilator (LABA or LAMA) may be appropriate for less symptomatic patients. Group E: LABA + LAMA is recommended; if blood eosinophils >=300 cells/mcL, consider LABA + LAMA + ICS (triple therapy) initially. Escalation to triple therapy (ICS + LABA + LAMA) is recommended for patients with persistent exacerbations on dual bronchodilator therapy, particularly with eosinophils >=100 cells/mcL. ICS should be avoided when eosinophils <100 due to increased pneumonia risk.

Mild exacerbations can be managed with increased short-acting bronchodilator use. Moderate exacerbations: Add short course of systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40mg/day for 5 days) and/or antibiotics if signs of bacterial infection (increased sputum purulence). Severe exacerbations requiring hospitalization: Administer supplemental oxygen to target SpO2 88-92%, systemic corticosteroids, short-acting bronchodilators, and antibiotics if indicated. Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) is recommended for acute respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35). Prevention strategies include smoking cessation, vaccination, pulmonary rehabilitation, and optimized maintenance therapy.

guidelines.section_title: Oxygen Therapy and Pulmonary Rehabilitation in COPD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) is indicated for stable patients with PaO2 <=55 mmHg or SpO2 <=88% at rest, or PaO2 56-59 mmHg with evidence of pulmonary hypertension, polycythemia (hematocrit >55%), or peripheral edema. Target SpO2 during supplemental oxygen is >=90%. Pulmonary rehabilitation is recommended for all symptomatic patients with COPD, particularly those with GOLD grade >=2 or high symptom burden. Post-exacerbation pulmonary rehabilitation started within 4 weeks of hospital discharge reduces readmissions and improves quality of life.

CKD is defined as abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for >3 months with health implications. CKD staging uses eGFR and albuminuria: G1 (>=90), G2 (60-89), G3a (45-59), G3b (30-44), G4 (15-29), G5 (<15 mL/min/1.73m2). Albuminuria categories: A1 (<30 mg/g normal to mildly increased), A2 (30-300 mg/g moderately increased), A3 (>300 mg/g severely increased). The combination of GFR and albuminuria categories creates a risk matrix for prognosis and management intensity. eGFR should be calculated using the CKD-EPI equation without race adjustment.

For adults with CKD, a target systolic BP of <120 mmHg is suggested based on standardized office measurement. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are recommended as first-line therapy for CKD patients with albuminuria (UACR >=30 mg/g), with or without diabetes. The combination of ACE inhibitors and ARBs should not be used. Monitor serum potassium and creatinine within 2-4 weeks of initiating or increasing RAAS blockade. Tolerate up to 30% rise in serum creatinine after RAAS inhibitor initiation if not accompanied by hyperkalemia.

Evaluate for anemia in CKD when hemoglobin <13 g/dL in males or <12 g/dL in females. Iron deficiency should be corrected before or alongside ESA therapy: target TSAT >20% and ferritin >100 ng/mL (>200 ng/mL in dialysis patients). IV iron is preferred over oral iron in hemodialysis patients. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) should not be initiated when hemoglobin >=10 g/dL. ESA therapy should generally target hemoglobin of 10-11.5 g/dL; do not intentionally exceed 13 g/dL. HIF-PHI agents (roxadustat) are an alternative to ESAs.

Monitor serum calcium, phosphorus, PTH, and alkaline phosphatase beginning in CKD G3a. In CKD G3a-G5D, phosphate levels should be lowered toward the normal range. Phosphate binders are indicated when phosphate levels are persistently elevated despite dietary restriction: non-calcium-based binders (sevelamer, lanthanum) are preferred. In CKD G5D, maintain PTH in approximately 2-9 times the upper normal limit. Active vitamin D (calcitriol) or vitamin D analogs should not be routinely used in CKD G3a-G5 without dialysis unless PTH is progressively rising.

Refer to nephrology for: eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m2 (GFR category G4-G5), persistent UACR >=300 mg/g, progressive decline in eGFR (>5 mL/min/1.73m2 per year sustained), CKD with refractory hypertension, or unexplained decline in eGFR. Dialysis planning should begin when eGFR <15-20 and dialysis initiation is typically considered when eGFR approaches 5-10 mL/min/1.73m2 or when uremic symptoms, fluid overload, or hyperkalemia are refractory. Peritoneal dialysis and hemodialysis have comparable outcomes and choice should be based on patient preference and clinical factors.

For patients with acute DVT of the leg, anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) is recommended over vitamin K antagonists (VKA). Preferred DOACs: apixaban or rivaroxaban as single-drug approaches (no need for initial parenteral anticoagulation), or dabigatran or edoxaban after 5-10 days of initial parenteral anticoagulation. Treatment duration: At least 3 months for provoked DVT (surgery, immobilization). Consider indefinite anticoagulation for unprovoked DVT with low bleeding risk. Compression stockings are not routinely recommended to prevent post-thrombotic syndrome.

For acute PE, initiate anticoagulation immediately when clinical suspicion is high, even before diagnostic confirmation. Low-risk PE (hemodynamically stable, no RV dysfunction): Outpatient treatment with DOACs is preferred. Submassive PE (hemodynamically stable with RV dysfunction): Anticoagulation with close monitoring; systemic thrombolysis is not routinely recommended but may be considered for clinical deterioration. Massive PE (hemodynamic instability): Systemic thrombolysis with alteplase is recommended. Duration of anticoagulation follows same principles as DVT. DOAC selection: apixaban or rivaroxaban preferred for initial management.

Pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis is recommended for acutely ill medical patients at increased VTE risk (Padua score >=4 or IMPROVE VTE score >=2). Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH, e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) is preferred over unfractionated heparin for medical and surgical VTE prophylaxis. Duration of prophylaxis: Standard in-hospital duration for medical patients; extended prophylaxis (up to 35 days with LMWH) is recommended after major orthopedic surgery (hip/knee replacement, hip fracture). Mechanical prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression) should be used when pharmacologic prophylaxis is contraindicated.

For life-threatening bleeding on warfarin: Administer 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) plus IV vitamin K 10mg. For life-threatening bleeding on dabigatran: Administer idarucizumab (Praxbind) 5g IV. For life-threatening bleeding on factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban): Administer andexanet alfa if available; otherwise 4F-PCC 50 units/kg. Hold anticoagulation and apply local hemostatic measures. Activated charcoal may be considered if DOAC was ingested within 2 hours. Resumption of anticoagulation after major bleeding should be individualized, typically reconsidered at 1-2 weeks.

Hydroxyurea is recommended for all adults with HbSS or HbS-beta0 thalassemia regardless of clinical severity to reduce frequency of pain crises, acute chest syndrome, and need for transfusions. Starting dose: 15-20 mg/kg/day, with dose escalation every 8 weeks to maximum tolerated dose (targeting mild myelosuppression, typically ANC 2000-4000/mcL). Monitor CBC every 4-8 weeks during dose escalation, then every 2-3 months on stable dose. Hydroxyurea may also be offered to patients with HbSC or HbS-beta+ thalassemia with recurrent vaso-occlusive crises. Counsel on teratogenicity and contraception.

Acute simple transfusion is recommended for: severe symptomatic anemia (Hgb drop >2 g/dL below baseline or Hgb <5-6 g/dL), acute chest syndrome (with exchange transfusion for severe cases), and preoperative management. Chronic transfusion therapy (targeting HbS <30%) is recommended for primary and secondary stroke prevention in children with abnormal TCD velocities and for adults with history of stroke. Monitor for iron overload (ferritin every 3 months, liver iron annually) and initiate iron chelation therapy when ferritin >1000 ng/mL or liver iron >5 mg/g. Ensure extended red cell antigen matching (C, E, K at minimum) to reduce alloimmunization.

guidelines.section_title: Acute and Chronic Pain Management in SCD
guidelines.recommendation_text: For acute vaso-occlusive crisis (VOC): Administer IV opioids within 30 minutes of triage (morphine or hydromorphone preferred). Use individualized dosing based on prior effective doses. Reassess pain every 15-30 minutes and redose as needed. Adjunctive NSAIDs (ketorolac) are recommended if no contraindications. Avoid meperidine due to seizure risk. For chronic pain: Develop a comprehensive multimodal plan including non-opioid analgesics, physical therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, and hydroxyurea optimization. Long-acting opioids may be appropriate for persistent pain with regular reassessment. Screen for depression and anxiety which frequently accompany chronic pain in SCD.

Acute chest syndrome (ACS): Defined by new pulmonary infiltrate plus respiratory symptoms and/or fever. Treat with antibiotics (cephalosporin plus macrolide), supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 >95%, bronchodilators, simple or exchange transfusion (exchange for severe ACS with SpO2 <90% or rapid deterioration), and incentive spirometry. Splenic sequestration: Urgent simple transfusion for hemodynamic instability (target Hgb rise 1-2 g/dL, avoid over-correction). Priapism lasting >4 hours: Urgent urologic consultation for aspiration and irrigation. Aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19): Transfuse for symptomatic anemia; usually self-limited.

guidelines.section_title: Health Maintenance and Screening in SCD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Annual screening recommendations for adults with SCD: Ophthalmology exam for retinopathy, echocardiogram and screening for pulmonary hypertension (TRV >=2.5 m/s warrants further evaluation with right heart catheterization), renal function (eGFR, UACR — SCD nephropathy is common), liver function tests and iron studies (especially if transfusion history), hip MRI or X-ray if avascular necrosis symptoms. Vaccinations: Pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23), influenza annually, meningococcal (MenACWY and MenB), hepatitis B, and COVID-19. Penicillin prophylaxis is no longer routinely recommended after age 5 but should continue in functionally asplenic patients.

For outpatient adults with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) and no comorbidities or risk factors for MRSA or Pseudomonas: amoxicillin 1g TID, or doxycycline 100mg BID, or a macrolide (azithromycin 500mg day 1 then 250mg days 2-5 or clarithromycin) ONLY in areas with pneumococcal resistance <25%. For patients with comorbidities (chronic heart, lung, liver, or renal disease; diabetes; alcoholism; malignancy; asplenia): combination therapy with amoxicillin/clavulanate or a cephalosporin PLUS a macrolide or doxycycline, OR monotherapy with a respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin).

For inpatient adults with non-severe CAP (no ICU admission needed), recommended regimens: combination therapy with a beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam 1.5-3g IV q6h, ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily, or cefotaxime 1-2g IV q8h) PLUS a macrolide (azithromycin 500mg IV daily) or doxycycline 100mg BID. Alternative: respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy (levofloxacin 750mg IV daily or moxifloxacin 400mg IV daily). A beta-lactam plus macrolide is preferred over fluoroquinolone monotherapy based on observational data suggesting lower mortality. Empiric treatment for MRSA or Pseudomonas is NOT recommended unless specific risk factors are present (prior respiratory isolation of these organisms, recent hospitalization with parenteral antibiotics in last 90 days).

Severe CAP criteria (1 major or 3+ minor): Major — septic shock requiring vasopressors, respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Minor — respiratory rate >=30, PaO2/FiO2 <=250, multilobar infiltrates, confusion, uremia (BUN >=20), leukopenia (WBC <4000), thrombocytopenia (platelets <100,000), hypothermia (<36C), hypotension requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation. For severe CAP: beta-lactam PLUS a macrolide (preferred) or a beta-lactam PLUS a respiratory fluoroquinolone. If risk factors for MRSA, add vancomycin or linezolid. If risk factors for Pseudomonas, use an anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem) plus a fluoroquinolone. De-escalate based on cultures and clinical improvement.

guidelines.section_title: Duration of Antibiotic Therapy for CAP
guidelines.recommendation_text: Antibiotic therapy for CAP should be continued for a minimum of 5 days. The patient should be afebrile for 48-72 hours and clinically stable before discontinuation (heart rate <=100, systolic BP >=90, respiratory rate <=24, oxygen saturation >=90% on room air, able to take oral intake, normal mental status). Longer duration may be needed for complications (empyema, lung abscess) or specific pathogens (Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas). Procalcitonin can guide antibiotic discontinuation — consider stopping when procalcitonin <0.25 ng/mL or has decreased by >=80% from peak. Routine chest X-ray follow-up is not necessary at discharge for patients who improve clinically.

guidelines.section_title: Empiric Antibiotic Selection for Complicated UTI
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients with complicated UTI (cUTI) including acute pyelonephritis who do NOT have sepsis: empiric therapy should be guided by local resistance patterns. Suggested empiric agents: fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin), third-generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone), or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if local resistance <20%). Carbapenems are NOT first-line due to stewardship considerations — initial drug-bug mismatch in cUTI without sepsis is unlikely to substantially contribute to mortality. For patients with sepsis due to cUTI: select among third- or fourth-generation cephalosporins, carbapenems, piperacillin-tazobactam, or fluoroquinolones rather than newer agents as initial empiric therapy.

guidelines.section_title: Duration of Therapy and Stewardship for cUTI
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with confirmed complicated UTI who are improving clinically on effective therapy: treat with a shorter course — 5-7 days of a fluoroquinolone or 7 days of a non-fluoroquinolone antibiotic, rather than a longer course (10-14 days). Select a definitive antibiotic with targeted spectrum based on urine culture results as soon as available, rather than continuing empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for the full duration. Step-down from IV to oral therapy should occur when the patient is clinically improving, afebrile, and tolerating oral intake. Asymptomatic bacteriuria should NOT be treated (except in pregnancy or prior to urologic procedures).

For patients with sepsis due to complicated UTI: initiate broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics within 1 hour. Suggested agents: third- or fourth-generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone, cefepime), carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem), piperacillin-tazobactam, or fluoroquinolones. Newer agents (ceftazidime-avibactam, ceftolozane-tazobactam, meropenem-vaborbactam) should be reserved for confirmed resistant organisms, not used empirically. Obtain blood cultures and urine culture BEFORE starting antibiotics. Source control: assess for obstructive uropathy requiring urgent decompression (nephrostomy tube or ureteral stent). Monitor lactate clearance as a marker of treatment response. De-escalate to narrowest-spectrum effective agent based on culture results.

Risk factors for resistant organisms in cUTI: prior fluoroquinolone use in last 90 days, prior UTI with resistant organism, recent hospitalization, indwelling urinary catheter, travel to high-resistance regions. For suspected ESBL-producing Enterobacterales: carbapenems are the treatment of choice for serious infections. Nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin may be considered for uncomplicated lower UTI due to ESBL producers. Avoid empiric fluoroquinolones if local E. coli resistance >10% for uncomplicated UTI or if patient has prior fluoroquinolone exposure. For carbapenem-resistant organisms: consult infectious diseases; options include ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, or cefiderocol based on susceptibility testing.

guidelines.section_title: Diagnosis and Initial Management of GERD
guidelines.recommendation_text: GERD should be suspected in patients with typical symptoms of heartburn and regurgitation. An empiric trial of PPI therapy (omeprazole 20mg, lansoprazole 30mg, or equivalent once daily for 8 weeks) is recommended as the initial diagnostic and therapeutic strategy for patients with typical symptoms without alarm features. Alarm features warranting upper endoscopy: dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, unintentional weight loss, anemia, or persistent vomiting. Ambulatory reflux monitoring (pH or impedance-pH) should be performed OFF PPI therapy to establish a GERD diagnosis in patients without prior endoscopic evidence of reflux esophagitis. Upper endoscopy is NOT required for all GERD patients but should be considered in those with >5-10 years of symptoms for Barrett esophagus screening.

guidelines.section_title: Step-Up and Maintenance Therapy for GERD
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients with incomplete PPI response: optimize PPI therapy by ensuring the medication is taken 30-60 minutes before a meal, consider switching to a different PPI, or increasing to twice-daily dosing. H2-receptor antagonists (famotidine 20-40mg) may be added at bedtime for nocturnal symptoms. Lifestyle modifications: weight loss if overweight/obese, avoid meals 2-3 hours before bedtime, elevate head of bed, avoid trigger foods (caffeine, alcohol, chocolate, fatty/spicy foods, citrus, tomato). For long-term maintenance: use the lowest effective PPI dose. Patients with erosive esophagitis (LA grade C or D) or Barrett esophagus should remain on continuous PPI therapy. On-demand PPI therapy may be appropriate for non-erosive reflux disease.

Refractory GERD is defined as persistent symptoms despite 8 weeks of optimized twice-daily PPI therapy. Evaluation should include: 1) Confirm medication adherence and proper timing (30-60 min before meals). 2) Upper endoscopy ON PPI to evaluate for eosinophilic esophagitis, pill esophagitis, or other diagnoses. 3) Ambulatory reflux monitoring (impedance-pH preferred) on PPI to distinguish true refractory reflux from functional heartburn. 4) Esophageal manometry to exclude achalasia or major motility disorder before considering anti-reflux surgery. Functional heartburn (normal acid exposure, negative symptom-reflux correlation) should be treated with neuromodulators (tricyclic antidepressants, SSRIs) rather than escalating acid suppression. Anti-reflux surgery (fundoplication or magnetic sphincter augmentation) should only be considered in patients with objective evidence of pathologic reflux.

Long-term PPI use is appropriate when clinically indicated (erosive esophagitis LA grade C/D, Barrett esophagus, severe symptom relapse off PPI). Clinicians should periodically reassess the need for continued PPI therapy and step down to the lowest effective dose. Potential risks of long-term PPI use (based on observational data with uncertain causality): C. difficile infection, bone fractures, chronic kidney disease, hypomagnesemia. These should NOT preclude PPI use when clearly indicated, but should prompt monitoring: check magnesium if on long-term PPI with concurrent diuretics, ensure adequate calcium/vitamin D intake, and assess bone density per standard osteoporosis screening guidelines. PPI discontinuation should be gradual (step-down over 2-4 weeks) to avoid rebound acid hypersecretion.

For mild-to-moderate Crohn's disease: budesonide 9mg daily for 8 weeks is preferred for ileal or right colonic disease. Sulfasalazine may be used for Crohn's colitis. 5-ASA agents (mesalamine) have limited efficacy in Crohn's disease and are NOT recommended. For moderate-to-severe disease: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40-60mg daily with taper) for short-term induction. For steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory disease: early introduction of biologic therapy (anti-TNF: infliximab, adalimumab; anti-integrin: vedolizumab; anti-IL-12/23: ustekinumab) with or without immunomodulator (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine, methotrexate). Top-down approach with early biologic therapy should be considered in patients with high-risk features (young age, perianal disease, extensive disease, deep ulcers, prior surgery).

Corticosteroids should NOT be used for maintenance therapy in Crohn's disease. Maintenance options: immunomodulators (azathioprine 2-2.5 mg/kg, 6-MP 1-1.5 mg/kg, or methotrexate 15-25mg weekly) for patients with steroid-dependent disease. Biologic therapy is preferred for maintenance in moderate-to-severe disease: anti-TNF agents (infliximab 5mg/kg q8wk, adalimumab 40mg q2wk), vedolizumab 300mg q8wk, or ustekinumab 90mg q8wk. Combination therapy (biologic + immunomodulator) is superior to either alone for anti-TNF agents, particularly infliximab. Therapeutic drug monitoring (trough levels and anti-drug antibodies) should guide dose optimization before switching biologic class. Target: clinical remission (CDAI <150) plus endoscopic remission (mucosal healing).

For mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis: oral 5-ASA (mesalamine >=2.4g/day) is first-line therapy. Combined oral and topical (rectal) mesalamine is more effective than either alone and is recommended for left-sided or extensive colitis. Topical mesalamine (enema or suppository) is preferred for proctitis or left-sided disease. Sulfasalazine 4-6g/day is an alternative, particularly cost-effective but with more side effects. If inadequate response to optimized 5-ASA: oral budesonide MMX 9mg daily (for mild-moderate UC with left-sided/extensive disease) or oral corticosteroids for moderate disease. Patients failing 5-ASA should be assessed for escalation to thiopurines or biologic therapy. 5-ASA therapy should be continued long-term for maintenance in patients who achieve remission.

guidelines.section_title: Moderate-to-Severe Ulcerative Colitis and Biologic Therapy
guidelines.recommendation_text: For moderate-to-severe UC failing conventional therapy: biologic agents are recommended. Options include anti-TNF (infliximab 5mg/kg induction at weeks 0, 2, 6 then q8wk; adalimumab; golimumab), anti-integrin (vedolizumab 300mg at weeks 0, 2, 6 then q8wk), anti-IL-12/23 (ustekinumab), or JAK inhibitor (tofacitinib 10mg BID induction then 5mg BID maintenance). For acute severe UC (hospitalized): IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 60mg/day or hydrocortisone 100mg TID). If no response to IV steroids in 3-5 days: rescue therapy with infliximab or cyclosporine, or colectomy. Patients with acute severe UC should be co-managed by gastroenterologist and colorectal surgeon. VTE prophylaxis should be given to all hospitalized UC patients.

For treatment-naive patients with moderate-to-high disease activity: methotrexate monotherapy is conditionally recommended as first-line therapy over other conventional DMARDs, targeted synthetic DMARDs, or biologics. Recommended starting dose: 15mg/week orally with folic acid 1mg daily. Escalate to 25mg/week by 4-8 weeks if tolerated. Short-term bridging with low-dose glucocorticoids (prednisone <=10mg/day for <3 months) is conditionally recommended during DMARD initiation. Treat-to-target approach: aim for low disease activity or remission within 6 months. Reassess at 3 months — if not at target, escalate therapy. For low disease activity: hydroxychloroquine or sulfasalazine may be used as initial DMARD.

guidelines.section_title: Biologic and Targeted Therapy for RA
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients with moderate-to-high disease activity despite methotrexate optimization (>=15mg/week for >=3 months): adding a biologic DMARD or targeted synthetic DMARD (tsDMARD) is conditionally recommended over triple conventional therapy (MTX + sulfasalazine + hydroxychloroquine). Options: anti-TNF (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab, golimumab), anti-IL-6 (tocilizumab, sarilumab), T-cell co-stimulation blocker (abatacept), anti-CD20 (rituximab — especially if anti-CCP positive), or JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib). Continue methotrexate background when adding biologic (combination > monotherapy for anti-TNF agents). JAK inhibitors carry FDA boxed warnings for serious infections, cardiovascular events, and malignancy in patients >=50 years with cardiovascular risk factors.

Monitor disease activity every 1-3 months during active disease using validated measures (DAS28, CDAI, or SDAI). Labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs every 4-12 weeks on methotrexate; lipid panel on JAK inhibitors. Once sustained remission (>=6 months): consider tapering strategy. Taper glucocorticoids first, then reduce DMARD dose gradually. Biologic dose reduction or interval extension is conditionally recommended over abrupt discontinuation. Do NOT discontinue all DMARDs — maintain at least one conventional DMARD. Flare management: return to previously effective regimen. Screen for comorbidities: cardiovascular disease, infections (hepatitis B/C, tuberculosis before biologic therapy), osteoporosis, and malignancy.

Subcutaneous nodules: methotrexate may worsen nodulosis; consider switching to hydroxychloroquine or a biologic. Interstitial lung disease (ILD): avoid methotrexate if significant ILD; rituximab or abatacept preferred. Hepatitis B: entecavir or tenofovir prophylaxis required before rituximab or other B-cell depleting therapy. Hepatitis C: treat with direct-acting antivirals; methotrexate is relatively contraindicated with significant liver disease. Pregnancy planning: stop methotrexate and leflunomide (washout with cholestyramine) >=3 months before conception. Hydroxychloroquine and sulfasalazine are compatible with pregnancy. Certolizumab has lowest placental transfer among anti-TNF agents. Heart failure: avoid anti-TNF agents in NYHA class III-IV HF.

Urate-lowering therapy (ULT) is conditionally recommended for patients with: >=2 flares/year, tophi, radiographic damage from gout, or uric acid nephrolithiasis. Allopurinol is strongly recommended as first-line ULT over febuxostat. Starting dose: allopurinol 100mg/day (50mg/day if CKD stage >=3). Titrate by 100mg every 2-4 weeks to target serum urate <6 mg/dL (or <5 mg/dL if tophi present). Maximum dose 800mg/day. HLA-B*5801 testing is conditionally recommended before starting allopurinol in Southeast Asian, African American, and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander patients (risk of severe hypersensitivity reaction). Alternative: febuxostat 40-80mg/day (avoid in patients with cardiovascular disease per FDA warning).

Acute flare treatment: colchicine (1.2mg then 0.6mg 1 hour later, then 0.6mg BID), NSAIDs (naproxen 500mg BID or indomethacin 50mg TID), or glucocorticoids (prednisone 30-40mg/day taper over 7-10 days) as first-line options. For patients who cannot take oral medications: intra-articular corticosteroid injection or IM/IV methylprednisolone. IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra) for refractory flares. Flare prophylaxis when starting ULT: colchicine 0.6mg once or twice daily for 3-6 months is strongly recommended over no prophylaxis. Continue ULT during acute flares — do NOT stop or start ULT during a flare (conditionally recommended). NSAIDs or low-dose prednisone may be used as alternative prophylaxis if colchicine is contraindicated.

CKD: Use allopurinol at reduced starting dose (50mg/day for CKD stage >=3) but can still titrate to target; do NOT limit maximum dose based on creatinine clearance alone (old practice). Febuxostat does not require renal dose adjustment. Cardiovascular disease: febuxostat has FDA boxed warning for increased cardiovascular mortality vs allopurinol in CARES trial — use allopurinol preferentially. For patients on thiazide or loop diuretics: continue diuretic if needed for other conditions; do NOT switch solely because of gout. Losartan and SGLT2 inhibitors have modest uricosuric effects and may be preferred for hypertension/diabetes in gout patients. Avoid aspirin and cyclosporine which raise urate levels.

For adults with new-onset focal epilepsy: lamotrigine should be considered (Level B recommendation) as first-line. Levetiracetam and zonisamide may be considered (Level C). For patients >=60 years with focal epilepsy: lamotrigine should be considered (Level B); gabapentin may be considered (Level C). For childhood absence epilepsy: ethosuximide or valproic acid should be considered before lamotrigine (Level B). Important considerations: valproic acid is teratogenic — avoid in women of childbearing age unless no alternative. Levetiracetam has fewest drug interactions and no hepatic metabolism. Carbamazepine and phenytoin are effective but have more drug interactions and side effects. Start at low dose and titrate gradually to minimize side effects. Seizure freedom (no seizures for 12 months) is the primary treatment goal.

Drug-resistant epilepsy: defined as failure of two appropriate antiseizure medications at adequate doses. Evaluation: confirm diagnosis (video-EEG monitoring to rule out non-epileptic events), ensure medication adherence, optimize current medication. Consider combination therapy with drugs of different mechanisms. Surgical evaluation is recommended for all patients with drug-resistant focal epilepsy — resective surgery yields seizure freedom in 60-70% of temporal lobe epilepsy cases. Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS) is an option for patients who are not surgical candidates. Dietary therapy (ketogenic diet, modified Atkins) may be considered. Responsive neurostimulation (RNS) is FDA-approved for focal epilepsy with 1-2 seizure foci.

guidelines.section_title: Epilepsy in Women of Childbearing Age
guidelines.recommendation_text: Valproic acid should be avoided in women of childbearing potential due to teratogenicity (neural tube defects, cognitive impairment in offspring). If valproate must be used, prescribe at lowest effective dose and supplement with high-dose folic acid (4-5mg/day). Lamotrigine and levetiracetam are preferred antiseizure medications in pregnancy (lowest risk of major congenital malformations). Drug levels decline during pregnancy (especially lamotrigine — may need 50-100% dose increase); monitor monthly levels throughout pregnancy. Contraception: enzyme-inducing ASMs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital) reduce efficacy of hormonal contraceptives — recommend IUD or depot medroxyprogesterone. Breastfeeding is generally safe with most ASMs. Preconception counseling should include discussion of seizure risks, medication optimization, and folic acid supplementation.

For noncardioembolic ischemic stroke or TIA: antiplatelet therapy is recommended. Aspirin 81-325mg/day is first-line (Level A). Clopidogrel 75mg/day is a reasonable alternative (Level B). Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT — aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21-90 days after minor stroke (NIHSS <=3) or high-risk TIA (ABCD2 >=4), followed by single antiplatelet (Level A). For cardioembolic stroke (atrial fibrillation): anticoagulation with DOAC is recommended over antiplatelet therapy (Level A). Do NOT combine antiplatelet + anticoagulant for stroke prevention unless separate indication exists. For patients with intracranial atherosclerosis: DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 90 days followed by single antiplatelet. Ticagrelor + aspirin is an alternative for 30 days per THALES trial.

Hypertension: target BP <130/80 mmHg for secondary stroke prevention (Level A). Restart antihypertensives after the acute phase (typically 24-48 hours after stroke onset). Diabetes: optimize glycemic control (A1C target per ADA guidelines). Lipids: high-intensity statin therapy is recommended for all patients with atherosclerotic ischemic stroke/TIA (Level A). Target LDL <70 mg/dL for patients with atherosclerotic cause; add ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitor if needed. Smoking cessation: strongly recommended with pharmacotherapy (varenicline, bupropion, NRT) and counseling. Obesity: weight management, Mediterranean or DASH diet. Physical activity: >=150 min/week of moderate aerobic exercise. Obstructive sleep apnea: CPAP may reduce recurrent stroke risk.

Symptomatic carotid stenosis >=70%: carotid endarterectomy (CEA) is recommended if perioperative morbidity/mortality risk is <6% (Level A). CEA should be performed within 2 weeks of the index event when possible. Carotid artery stenting (CAS) is an alternative when CEA carries high surgical risk or stenosis is not surgically accessible. Symptomatic stenosis 50-69%: CEA may be considered depending on patient-specific factors (age, sex, comorbidities). Asymptomatic stenosis >=70%: CEA may be considered in select patients with life expectancy >5 years and perioperative risk <3%. Medical management (statin, antiplatelet, BP control) is reasonable for all patients with carotid stenosis regardless of intervention.

For initial treatment of moderate-to-severe major depressive disorder (MDD), second-generation antidepressants are recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy. SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine) or SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) are preferred based on efficacy, safety, and tolerability. Selection should consider: side effect profile (sexual dysfunction with SSRIs, nausea/hypertension with SNRIs, weight gain with mirtazapine), comorbidities (duloxetine for comorbid pain, bupropion for fatigue/smoking cessation, mirtazapine for insomnia/appetite loss), prior response, family member response, cost/access, and patient preference. Start at recommended initial dose and titrate as needed. Allow 4-6 weeks at adequate dose before concluding a medication trial has failed. Psychotherapy (CBT, interpersonal therapy) is recommended in combination with pharmacotherapy for moderate-severe MDD.

Monitor for treatment response using validated scales (PHQ-9 recommended) at baseline and every 2-4 weeks during initial treatment. Response is defined as >=50% reduction in PHQ-9 score; remission as PHQ-9 <5. If no response after 4-6 weeks at adequate dose: reassess diagnosis, confirm adherence, and consider dose optimization or switching. Measure-based care (systematic tracking with PHQ-9) improves outcomes vs clinical judgment alone. Assess for suicidality at each visit, especially in first 1-2 months and after dose changes (FDA black box warning for patients <25 years). Monitor for activation symptoms (agitation, insomnia, anxiety) in early treatment. Side effects: if sexual dysfunction on SSRI, consider switching to bupropion or adding bupropion; if weight gain on mirtazapine/paroxetine, consider switching.

Treatment-resistant depression (TRD) is defined as failure to achieve remission after 2 adequate antidepressant trials. Strategies: 1) Switching to a different antidepressant class (SSRI → SNRI, or to bupropion, mirtazapine). 2) Augmentation: lithium augmentation (Level A evidence — target level 0.6-0.8 mEq/L), atypical antipsychotic augmentation (aripiprazole 2-15mg, quetiapine 50-300mg, brexpiprazole 1-3mg — all FDA-approved for adjunctive MDD). 3) Esketamine nasal spray (Spravato): FDA-approved for TRD, administered under medical supervision with REMS program. 4) rTMS (repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation): FDA-cleared, non-invasive option. 5) ECT (electroconvulsive therapy): most effective treatment for severe/refractory depression, especially with psychotic features or acute suicidality. Consider MAOIs for TRD (requires dietary restrictions and washout period).

guidelines.section_title: MDD Maintenance Therapy and Relapse Prevention
guidelines.recommendation_text: Continue antidepressant at the dose that achieved remission for maintenance therapy. Duration: minimum 4-9 months after first episode achieving remission. For patients with recurrent episodes (>=3), chronic depression, or high-risk features (severe episodes, comorbid anxiety, residual symptoms, family history): indefinite maintenance therapy is recommended. Maintain the same dose — do NOT reduce dose for maintenance. Discontinuation: taper gradually over 2-4 weeks minimum (longer for paroxetine and venlafaxine) to avoid discontinuation syndrome. Monitor for relapse during and after taper. Psychotherapy (particularly CBT and MBCT — mindfulness-based cognitive therapy) reduces relapse risk and may allow eventual medication discontinuation in some patients.

guidelines.guideline: ACOG Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Guidelines (2020)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Preeclampsia is diagnosed by new-onset hypertension (systolic >=140 or diastolic >=90 mmHg) after 20 weeks' gestation with proteinuria (>=300 mg/24h or protein/creatinine ratio >=0.3) or other end-organ dysfunction. Preeclampsia with severe features includes systolic >=160 or diastolic >=110, thrombocytopenia (<100,000), elevated liver enzymes (twice normal), renal insufficiency (creatinine >1.1 or doubling), pulmonary edema, or new-onset cerebral/visual disturbances. HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets) is a severe variant requiring urgent evaluation.

guidelines.guideline: ACOG Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Guidelines (2020)
guidelines.section_title: Management and Delivery Timing in Preeclampsia
guidelines.recommendation_text: For preeclampsia without severe features, expectant management is reasonable up to 37 weeks with close monitoring including twice-weekly BP checks, weekly labs (CBC, CMP, LDH), and fetal surveillance. Delivery is recommended at 37 weeks. For preeclampsia with severe features at >=34 weeks, delivery is recommended after maternal stabilization. At <34 weeks with severe features, expectant management may be considered in a facility with maternal-fetal medicine expertise, with corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity. Magnesium sulfate is recommended for seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia. Antihypertensive therapy (IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, or oral nifedipine) for acute severe hypertension (>=160/110).

guidelines.guideline: ACOG Hypertensive Disorders of Pregnancy Guidelines (2020)
guidelines.section_title: Postpartum Monitoring and Long-Term Cardiovascular Risk
guidelines.recommendation_text: Postpartum BP monitoring is recommended for 72 hours after delivery and at 7-10 days postpartum. Persistent postpartum hypertension should be treated with labetalol, nifedipine, or enalapril (safe with breastfeeding). Women with history of preeclampsia have 2-4x increased lifetime risk of chronic hypertension, ischemic heart disease, and stroke. Annual cardiovascular risk assessment is recommended including BP, lipids, and glucose monitoring. Low-dose aspirin (81 mg/day) starting at 12-28 weeks is recommended in subsequent pregnancies for women with prior preeclampsia.

guidelines.section_title: Screening and Diagnosis of Gestational Diabetes
guidelines.recommendation_text: Universal screening for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is recommended at 24-28 weeks' gestation using either a one-step (75g OGTT with fasting, 1h, and 2h glucose) or two-step approach (50g glucose challenge test followed by 100g OGTT if abnormal). Two-step approach: 50g GCT threshold >=130-140 mg/dL; 100g OGTT diagnostic if 2+ values exceed thresholds (fasting >=95, 1h >=180, 2h >=155, 3h >=140 using Carpenter-Coustan criteria). Early screening in first trimester is recommended for women with BMI >=25 plus risk factors (prior GDM, family history of diabetes, high-risk ethnicity, prior macrosomia).

Initial management includes nutritional counseling (carbohydrate-controlled diet), moderate exercise, and glucose self-monitoring (fasting and 1-2h postprandial). Glycemic targets: fasting <95 mg/dL, 1h postprandial <140 mg/dL, 2h postprandial <120 mg/dL. If dietary management fails to achieve targets after 1-2 weeks, pharmacologic therapy is indicated. Insulin is the preferred first-line pharmacotherapy. Metformin and glyburide are alternatives when insulin is refused or impractical, though both cross the placenta. Fetal surveillance (NST, BPP) beginning at 32-36 weeks depending on severity and glycemic control.

guidelines.section_title: Delivery Timing and Postpartum Follow-Up for GDM
guidelines.recommendation_text: For well-controlled GDM on diet alone, delivery at 39-40+6 weeks is reasonable. For GDM on medications with good control, delivery at 39-39+6 weeks is recommended. For poorly controlled GDM, individualize timing from 37-39 weeks. Postpartum: screen for persistent diabetes at 4-12 weeks with 75g OGTT (not HbA1c alone). Women with GDM have 50% lifetime risk of developing type 2 diabetes. Screen every 1-3 years thereafter with fasting glucose, OGTT, or HbA1c. Counsel on lifestyle modifications, weight management, and benefits of breastfeeding for metabolic risk reduction.

Primary hypothyroidism is diagnosed by elevated TSH with low free T4. Subclinical hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, normal free T4) should be treated when TSH >10 mIU/L; treatment is individualized for TSH 4.5-10 based on symptoms, age, cardiovascular risk, and anti-TPO antibody status. Levothyroxine is the standard treatment, dosed at 1.6 mcg/kg/day for full replacement. Take on empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast or at bedtime 3+ hours after last meal. Recheck TSH 4-6 weeks after dose changes. Target TSH 0.5-2.5 mIU/L for most adults; higher targets (4-6) acceptable in elderly. Avoid overtreatment — suppressed TSH increases atrial fibrillation and osteoporosis risk.

Hyperthyroidism is diagnosed by suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 and/or free T3. Graves' disease is the most common cause; confirm with TSI (thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin) or radioactive iodine uptake scan. Three treatment options: antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred; propylthiouracil only in first trimester of pregnancy), radioactive iodine ablation, or thyroidectomy. Methimazole starting dose 10-30 mg/day depending on severity. Monitor CBC (agranulocytosis risk) and LFTs. Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) for symptom control. For Graves' ophthalmopathy, avoid radioactive iodine if moderate-severe; refer to ophthalmology.

Thyroid nodules are common (50-67% prevalence on ultrasound) but only 5-15% are malignant. Initial evaluation: TSH, neck ultrasound. If TSH is low, obtain radioactive iodine uptake/scan — hyperfunctioning nodules are rarely malignant. FNA biopsy is recommended based on ultrasound pattern and size: highly suspicious (>1 cm), intermediate (>1 cm), low suspicion (>1.5 cm), very low (>2 cm or observe). Use Bethesda classification for cytology results. Molecular testing (Afirma, ThyroSeq) for indeterminate cytology (Bethesda III-IV) to guide surgical decision-making. Observation with serial ultrasound is appropriate for benign cytology nodules.

Screen with DXA (dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry) in: all women >=65, men >=70, postmenopausal women <65 with risk factors (low body weight, prior fracture, high-risk medication use, family history), and adults with conditions causing bone loss (glucocorticoid use, hyperparathyroidism, malabsorption). Osteoporosis: T-score <=-2.5 at hip, femoral neck, or lumbar spine. Osteopenia: T-score -1.0 to -2.5. Use FRAX calculator (fracture risk assessment) incorporating clinical risk factors for treatment decisions. Vertebral fracture assessment by DXA or lateral spine imaging recommended for patients with height loss >1.5 inches or unexplained back pain.

Treat when: T-score <=-2.5, hip or vertebral fracture, or osteopenia with FRAX 10-year hip fracture probability >=3% or major osteoporotic fracture >=20%. First-line: bisphosphonates (alendronate 70 mg/week or risedronate 35 mg/week PO, or zoledronic acid 5 mg IV annually). For very high fracture risk, consider anabolic agents first (teriparatide, abaloparatide, or romosozumab) followed by antiresorptive. Denosumab (60 mg SQ every 6 months) for patients intolerant of bisphosphonates or with renal impairment. Ensure adequate calcium (1000-1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-1000 IU/day). Reassess treatment after 3-5 years of bisphosphonate therapy (drug holiday consideration).

Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIOP): Initiate bone-protective therapy in adults starting prednisone >=2.5 mg/day for >=3 months with moderate-high fracture risk. Oral bisphosphonates are first-line; teriparatide is preferred for very high-risk GIOP. Obtain baseline DXA and FRAX at initiation of chronic glucocorticoids. Male osteoporosis: evaluate for secondary causes (hypogonadism, excessive alcohol, glucocorticoid use, malabsorption). Treat with bisphosphonates (same agents/doses as postmenopausal osteoporosis). Testosterone replacement alone is insufficient for men with osteoporosis and hypogonadism — add bisphosphonate. Monitor with DXA every 1-2 years on treatment.

guidelines.guideline: AAOS Osteoarthritis of the Knee Guidelines (2021)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Strong recommendation for low-impact aerobic exercise, neuromuscular education, and strengthening programs. Self-management and weight loss programs recommended for BMI >=25. Physical therapy referral for individualized exercise prescription. Moderate recommendation for lateral wedge insoles (medial compartment OA), hinged knee braces (unicompartmental OA). Limited evidence supports acupuncture, TENS, or manual therapy as adjuncts. Strongly recommend against arthroscopic lavage/debridement for isolated OA without mechanical symptoms.

guidelines.guideline: AAOS Osteoarthritis of the Knee Guidelines (2021)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Strong recommendation for oral NSAIDs and topical NSAIDs as first-line pharmacotherapy. Moderate recommendation for intra-articular corticosteroid injections for short-term relief. Inconclusive evidence for intra-articular hyaluronic acid (viscosupplementation). Strong recommendation against glucosamine and chondroitin supplementation. Tramadol may be considered when NSAIDs are contraindicated. Acetaminophen has limited efficacy but acceptable for patients who cannot take NSAIDs. Duloxetine may benefit patients with centralized pain features. Opioids are not recommended for chronic OA management.

guidelines.guideline: AAOS Osteoarthritis of the Knee Guidelines (2021)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is recommended for patients with symptomatic OA who have failed adequate non-operative management. Moderate recommendation for unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA) in appropriate candidates with isolated unicompartmental disease. High tibial osteotomy may be considered in younger active patients with varus malalignment and unicompartmental OA. Preoperative optimization includes BMI management (goal <40 for best outcomes), glycemic control (HbA1c <8%), smoking cessation, and nutritional assessment. Multimodal pain management perioperatively reduces opioid use.

guidelines.section_title: Evaluation of Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms Due to BPH
guidelines.recommendation_text: Initial evaluation includes medical history, IPSS/AUA Symptom Index (AUA-SI), physical exam including DRE, urinalysis, and PSA (if management would change). Uroflowmetry and post-void residual (PVR) measurement are recommended when invasive treatment is considered. Transrectal or transabdominal ultrasound for prostate size assessment guides treatment selection. Pressure-flow urodynamic studies recommended when diagnosis is uncertain or before invasive therapy. Cystoscopy is optional and recommended mainly when hematuria is present or prior to surgical intervention.

Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin, silodosin, doxazosin, terazosin) are recommended as first-line monotherapy for moderate-severe LUTS. All alpha-blockers have similar efficacy; selection based on side-effect profile. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) recommended for prostate >30-40 mL, can reduce prostate volume 20-25% and risk of acute urinary retention/surgery. Combination alpha-blocker + 5-ARI superior to monotherapy for prostates >30 mL. Tadalafil 5 mg daily is an option particularly with concurrent erectile dysfunction. Anticholinergics or beta-3 agonists (mirabegron) may be added for persistent storage symptoms with PVR <250 mL.

guidelines.section_title: Surgical and Minimally Invasive Therapy for BPH
guidelines.recommendation_text: Surgery is recommended for refractory urinary retention, recurrent UTI, bladder stones, renal insufficiency from BPH, or failure of medical therapy. Transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) remains the gold standard for prostates 30-80 mL. Simple prostatectomy (open, robotic, or laparoscopic) for prostates >80-100 mL. Minimally invasive options: UroLift (prostatic urethral lift) for prostates 30-80 mL preserving ejaculatory function, Rezum (water vapor thermal therapy), and laser enucleation (HoLEP) for any prostate size. GreenLight laser vaporization is an alternative to TURP with lower bleeding risk, suitable for anticoagulated patients.

guidelines.section_title: Diagnosis and Risk Stratification of Bronchiolitis
guidelines.recommendation_text: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in children <2 years based on viral upper respiratory prodrome followed by increased respiratory effort (tachypnea, nasal flaring, retractions, wheezing, crackles). Routine laboratory studies, radiography, or viral testing are NOT recommended for diagnosis. Risk factors for severe disease: age <12 weeks, prematurity (<35 weeks), hemodynamically significant congenital heart disease, immunodeficiency, chronic lung disease, neuromuscular disorders. RSV is the most common cause. Assessment should focus on hydration status, work of breathing, and oxygen saturation.

guidelines.section_title: Management and Supportive Care for Bronchiolitis
guidelines.recommendation_text: Management is primarily supportive: nasal suctioning, adequate hydration (oral or nasogastric), and supplemental oxygen for persistent SpO2 <90% (some centers use <92%). Clinicians should NOT administer albuterol, epinephrine, systemic corticosteroids, or hypertonic saline to infants with bronchiolitis in the emergency department. Antibiotics should not be used unless bacterial coinfection is documented. Chest physiotherapy is not recommended. Continuous pulse oximetry is not needed for stable patients. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) may be used for moderate-severe disease with escalating respiratory distress. Palivizumab (Synagis) prophylaxis for high-risk infants per AAP guidance.

guidelines.section_title: Admission Criteria and Discharge Planning for Bronchiolitis
guidelines.recommendation_text: Admit for: persistent SpO2 <90%, severe respiratory distress (marked retractions, grunting, RR >70), inability to maintain hydration, apnea, or high-risk comorbidities. ICU admission criteria: impending respiratory failure, apnea requiring stimulation/intervention, need for CPAP/HFNC >2 L/kg/min. Discharge criteria: SpO2 >=90% for >=4 hours including sleep, adequate oral intake (>=75% of baseline), improving respiratory status, caregivers educated on nasal suctioning and signs of worsening. Most infants recover in 7-10 days with days 3-5 typically the worst. Follow-up within 1-2 days of discharge.

guidelines.section_title: Risk Stratification for Acute Chest Pain in the ED
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use validated risk stratification tools: HEART score (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin) is recommended for identifying low-risk chest pain patients. HEART score 0-3: low risk (<2% MACE at 30 days), consider discharge with outpatient follow-up. HEART score 4-6: moderate risk, observation and further testing. HEART score 7-10: high risk, admit for invasive strategy. Serial high-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTnI or hs-cTnT) at 0 and 1-3 hours can safely identify low-risk patients for early discharge. TIMI and GRACE scores are alternatives. ECG should be obtained within 10 minutes of ED arrival for all chest pain patients.

guidelines.section_title: Diagnostic Testing for Acute Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: For intermediate-risk patients, non-invasive functional testing or CCTA (coronary CT angiography) is recommended. CCTA has high sensitivity (95-99%) and negative predictive value for excluding obstructive CAD. Stress testing (exercise treadmill, stress echo, nuclear perfusion) is appropriate for patients who can exercise. Pharmacologic stress testing for patients unable to exercise. CT pulmonary angiography for suspected pulmonary embolism; use Wells score or PERC criteria first. D-dimer can rule out PE in low-probability patients. Chest X-ray for all chest pain presentations to evaluate for pneumothorax, pneumonia, aortic pathology, or other non-cardiac causes.

guidelines.section_title: Disposition and Follow-Up for Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: Low-risk patients (HEART 0-3 with negative serial troponins and non-ischemic ECG) may be safely discharged with outpatient follow-up within 72 hours. Shared decision-making is recommended for borderline-risk patients. Moderate-risk patients should undergo observation with serial troponins and provocative testing or CCTA before discharge. All discharged patients should receive: aspirin prescription, nitroglycerin SL for acute symptoms, instructions to return for recurrent/worsening symptoms, and cardiology follow-up referral. Documentation of risk stratification tool used, clinical reasoning, and patient understanding is essential for medical-legal purposes.

Conduct airway evaluation before all anesthetic or procedural sedation encounters. Assess: Mallampati classification (I-IV), thyromental distance (<6 cm concerning), mouth opening (<3 cm concerning), neck mobility, obesity/BMI, obstructive sleep apnea, prior difficult intubation history, and facial/cervical pathology. The Difficult Airway Algorithm should be reviewed and equipment prepared before induction. Awake intubation should be considered when difficulty is anticipated and patient cooperation is feasible. Always have a supraglottic airway device (SGA) available as rescue. Develop a primary plan (Plan A), alternative plan (Plan B), and emergency surgical airway plan (Plan C).

If initial intubation attempt fails after <=3 optimized attempts: insert SGA (laryngeal mask airway) as first rescue. If SGA ventilation succeeds, options include: intubation through SGA, wake patient, or proceed with SGA for surgery. If SGA fails and cannot ventilate: declare 'Cannot Intubate, Cannot Oxygenate' (CICO) emergency. Perform emergency front-of-neck access (cricothyroidotomy or tracheostomy). Call for help early — do not wait until CICO. Video laryngoscopy recommended as first approach (not as rescue only). Limit total intubation attempts to 3+1 (3 by primary operator, 1 by most experienced available). Maintain oxygenation between attempts.

Extubation of the difficult airway is a planned, high-risk event. Assess readiness: adequate consciousness, intact protective reflexes, adequate neuromuscular recovery (train-of-four ratio >=0.9), stable hemodynamics, acceptable oxygenation/ventilation. For known/suspected difficult reintubation: consider use of airway exchange catheter (AEC) placed through ETT before extubation, allowing emergency re-intubation over catheter. Perform in a controlled environment with full re-intubation equipment immediately available. Consider 'deep extubation' or 'awake extubation' based on individual risk assessment. Post-extubation monitoring in PACU/ICU for at-risk patients.

guidelines.section_title: Low-Dose CT Screening for Lung Cancer
guidelines.recommendation_text: Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) screening is recommended for adults aged 50-80 who have a >=20 pack-year smoking history and currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years. Screening should be performed at centers with experienced multidisciplinary teams. Use Lung-RADS classification to standardize reporting and management of findings. Lung-RADS 1-2: continue annual screening. Lung-RADS 3: 6-month follow-up LDCT. Lung-RADS 4: further evaluation (PET, tissue sampling). Shared decision-making discussion should include benefits (20% lung cancer mortality reduction), harms (false positives, incidental findings, radiation exposure), and smoking cessation counseling.

Begin average-risk screening at age 45 (updated from 50). Recommended modalities: colonoscopy every 10 years (preferred), FIT (fecal immunochemical test) annually, stool DNA-FIT (Cologuard) every 1-3 years, CT colonography every 5 years, or flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 years (with FIT every year). High-risk patients (family history of CRC in first-degree relative <60, personal history of adenomatous polyps, IBD, Lynch syndrome) should begin earlier and screen more frequently per specific high-risk protocols. Positive non-invasive tests require follow-up colonoscopy. Screening may stop at age 75-85 based on life expectancy and prior screening history.

USPSTF (2024): Biennial screening mammography for all women aged 40-74 (B recommendation). ACS: Annual mammography starting at 45, transition to biennial at 55 if desired; option to start at 40. Women with first-degree relative with breast cancer or known genetic predisposition (BRCA1/2, other high-penetrance variants) should have enhanced screening with breast MRI + mammography starting at age 30 or 10 years before youngest affected relative. Dense breasts (heterogeneously or extremely dense on mammography) may benefit from supplemental screening with breast MRI or ultrasound. Risk assessment with validated tools (Tyrer-Cuzick, Gail) to identify high-risk women.

Statin therapy for primary prevention: adults 40-75 with >=1 CVD risk factor (dyslipidemia, diabetes, hypertension, smoking) and 10-year CVD risk >=10% (B recommendation). Aspirin for primary prevention: individualized for adults 40-59 with >=10% 10-year CVD risk (C recommendation); not recommended for >=60. Blood pressure screening: adults >=18 years, confirm with ambulatory or home monitoring. Lipid screening: men >=35, women >=45 (or earlier with risk factors). Diabetes screening: adults 35-70 who are overweight or obese. Abdominal aortic aneurysm: one-time ultrasound for men 65-75 who have ever smoked.

guidelines.section_title: Infectious Disease and Other Preventive Screening
guidelines.recommendation_text: Hepatitis C: one-time screening for all adults 18-79 (B recommendation). Hepatitis B: screening for adolescents and adults at increased risk. HIV: screening all adults 15-65 and all pregnant persons (A recommendation). STI screening: chlamydia/gonorrhea for sexually active women <=24 and older women at increased risk. Tuberculosis: screening in populations at increased risk (recent immigrants, HIV+, close contacts). Depression screening: all adults (PHQ-2/PHQ-9) including pregnant and postpartum persons (B recommendation). Anxiety: screening adults <65 (B recommendation). Unhealthy alcohol use: screening and brief counseling for adults >=18.

Cervical cancer: Pap smear every 3 years (ages 21-29), Pap + HPV co-testing every 5 years or HPV alone every 5 years (ages 30-65). Lung cancer: annual LDCT for adults 50-80 with >=20 pack-year history who currently smoke or quit within 15 years. Colorectal cancer: begin screening at age 45 with colonoscopy, FIT, or stool DNA. Breast cancer: biennial mammography ages 40-74. Prostate cancer: informed decision-making for men 55-69 about PSA-based screening (C recommendation); not recommended >=70. Skin cancer: insufficient evidence for general population screening, but clinician awareness recommended.

Topical therapy is first-line for mild-to-moderate psoriasis (<10% BSA). High-potency topical corticosteroids (betamethasone dipropionate, clobetasol) for trunk/extremities; low-to-mid potency for face/intertriginous areas. Limit high-potency steroids to 2-4 weeks continuously, then pulse dosing. Vitamin D analogs (calcipotriene) are effective monotherapy or in combination with corticosteroids. Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for sensitive areas (face, genitals). Tazarotene (topical retinoid) effective for plaque psoriasis. Phototherapy (narrowband UVB) recommended for moderate disease not responding to topicals or widespread involvement. Excimer laser for localized plaques.

Biologics are preferred first-line systemic therapy for moderate-to-severe psoriasis (>=10% BSA or significant impact on quality of life). IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, brodalumab) and IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab, tildrakizumab) have high efficacy with PASI 90 responses >60%. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab) remain effective options. IL-12/23 inhibitor (ustekinumab) is effective with convenient dosing. Oral systemic options: methotrexate (weekly dosing with folic acid), apremilast (PDE4 inhibitor), cyclosporine (short-term use only), acitretin. Screen for tuberculosis and hepatitis B/C before biologic initiation. Monitor labs per agent-specific protocols.

Psoriasis is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, metabolic syndrome, psoriatic arthritis (30% of patients), depression, and liver disease. Screen for psoriatic arthritis at every visit using validated tools (PEST, CASPAR criteria). Cardiovascular risk assessment is recommended — psoriasis is an independent risk factor for MI and stroke. Monitor metabolic parameters (lipids, glucose, BMI, blood pressure) regularly. Depression screening (PHQ-9) should be integrated into routine care. Liver assessment (hepatic fibrosis screening with FIB-4) recommended for patients on methotrexate. Biologic therapy may reduce cardiovascular risk through reduction of systemic inflammation.

Type 1 diabetes: initial dilated eye exam within 5 years of diagnosis, then annually. Type 2 diabetes: initial exam at diagnosis, then annually (may extend to every 2 years if no retinopathy and well-controlled). Gestational diabetes: exam in first trimester with follow-up based on findings. Classification: no apparent retinopathy, mild/moderate/severe nonproliferative DR (NPDR), and proliferative DR (PDR).
