Enteral nutrition (EN) preferred over parenteral (PN). Start EN within 24-48 hours for hemodynamically stable patients. Trophic feeding (10-20 mL/h) initially for severe shock. Advance to goal (25-30 kcal/kg/day) over 48-72h. Protein: 1.2-2.0 g/kg/day (higher for burns, trauma, wounds). PN: start by day 7 if EN not feasible. Monitor: residual volumes (hold at >500 mL), electrolytes, glucose. Avoid overfeeding (refeeding syndrome risk with malnutrition). Indirect calorimetry for personalized targets if available.

Skin self-examination monthly. Full skin exam for high-risk: personal/family history of melanoma, >50 nevi, atypical mole syndrome, prior non-melanoma skin cancer, immunosuppression. Dermoscopy improves diagnostic accuracy. ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter >6mm, Evolution. Ugly duckling sign: lesion that looks different from others. Sun protection: broad-spectrum SPF ≥30, protective clothing, avoid tanning beds. Biopsy: excisional preferred for suspicious lesion.

Confirm diagnosis: KOH prep, fungal culture, or PAS staining of nail clipping (histopathology most sensitive). Terbinafine 250 mg daily (fingernails 6 weeks, toenails 12 weeks) — first-line, highest cure rate. Itraconazole pulse therapy (200 mg BID x 1 week/month for 2-3 months) alternative. Topical: efinaconazole 10% or tavaborole 5% (for mild, <50% nail involvement without matrix involvement). Ciclopirox 8% lacquer less effective. Check LFTs at baseline and 6 weeks for oral azoles. Recurrence common.

Screening: dilated eye exam at T2DM diagnosis, within 5 years for T1DM, then annually (biennial if no retinopathy). AI-based retinal screening validated for primary care settings. Mild NPDR: optimize glycemia and BP, annual exam. Moderate-severe NPDR: referral to retina specialist, exam Q3-6 months. Proliferative DR: panretinal photocoagulation (PRP) or anti-VEGF intravitreal injections (aflibercept per DRCR Protocol S). DME: anti-VEGF first-line (aflibercept, ranibizumab, faricimab). Focal laser for non-center-involving DME.

Diagnosis: polysomnography (PSG) gold standard, or home sleep apnea test (HSAT) for moderate-high pretest probability without significant comorbidities. AHI ≥5 with symptoms or ≥15 regardless = OSA. Severity: mild (5-14), moderate (15-29), severe (≥30). CPAP is first-line for moderate-severe OSA. Autotitrating PAP (APAP) as alternative to fixed pressure. MAD (mandibular advancement device) for mild-moderate or CPAP intolerance. Weight loss (bariatric surgery if appropriate). Hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire) for CPAP-intolerant, BMI <32, AHI 15-65.

Risk assessment: AUDIT-C for use, PAWSS for withdrawal risk. CIWA-Ar protocol for symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing (give chlordiazepoxide or lorazepam when CIWA ≥8-10). Alternative: fixed-dose taper. Severe withdrawal/DTs: ICU level care, IV diazepam or phenobarbital. Refractory: propofol or dexmedetomidine infusion. Seizure prophylaxis: benzodiazepines (not phenytoin). Thiamine 500 mg IV x 3 days BEFORE glucose (prevent Wernicke). Folate, multivitamin, magnesium supplementation. Gabapentin for mild withdrawal without seizure/DT history.

Asymptomatic ≥70% stenosis: carotid endarterectomy (CEA) if perioperative risk <3% and life expectancy >3-5 years. Medical management (aspirin + statin + BP control + smoking cessation) improved outcomes; may be adequate for many patients. Symptomatic ≥50%: CEA (within 2 weeks of event) reduces stroke risk. CAS (carotid artery stenting) alternative for: high surgical risk, prior neck radiation, surgically inaccessible lesion. TCAR (transcarotid artery revascularization) emerging option. Surveillance: carotid duplex annually.

MOH definition: headache ≥15 days/month with regular overuse of acute medication (≥10 days for triptans/opioids/combinations, ≥15 days for simple analgesics). Prevention: limit acute medication to <10-15 days/month. Treatment: withdraw offending medication (may require bridge with steroids or nerve block), start preventive medication simultaneously. NSAIDs and triptans: abrupt withdrawal. Opioids/barbiturates: gradual taper. Preventive options: topiramate, anti-CGRP mAb (may work without withdrawal per studies), onabotulinumtoxinA.

Age 21-24: cytology (Pap) alone Q3 years. Age 25-65: primary HPV testing Q5 years (preferred), cotesting (HPV + Pap) Q5 years, or cytology Q3 years. >65: discontinue if adequate prior screening and no CIN2+ in prior 25 years. Post-hysterectomy (without cervix, no CIN2+ history): stop screening. Immunocompromised: start at age 21, cytology annually until 3 consecutive normals, then Q3 years. Abnormal results: follow ASCCP risk-based management guidelines. HPV vaccination does not change screening intervals.

Level 1 (alert): glucose <70 mg/dL. Level 2 (clinically significant): <54 mg/dL. Level 3 (severe): altered mental status requiring assistance. Treatment: conscious patient — 15g fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, juice), recheck in 15 minutes (rule of 15). Severe hypoglycemia: glucagon 1 mg IM/SC or nasal glucagon 3 mg or dasiglucagon 0.6 mg SC. Reduce/adjust insulin or sulfonylurea. CGM to reduce hypoglycemia. Educate on prevention, driving safety. Hypoglycemia unawareness: raise glucose targets, CGM essential.

Uncomplicated P. falciparum: artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT): artemether-lumefantrine or artesunate-mefloquine. Duration: 3 days. P. vivax/ovale: chloroquine + primaquine 14 days (check G6PD first; tafenoquine single dose alternative). Severe malaria: IV artesunate (first-line); follow with full ACT course. Supportive: manage hypoglycemia, anemia, renal failure, cerebral malaria. Chemoprophylaxis for travelers: atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine based on destination resistance.

Universal screening at 24-28 weeks: 1-step (75g OGTT, IADPSG criteria) or 2-step (50g GCT then 100g OGTT if positive). Diet and exercise first-line. Glucose targets: fasting <95, 1h post-meal <140, 2h post-meal <120 mg/dL. If targets not met in 1-2 weeks: insulin (preferred) or metformin. Fetal surveillance: NST/BPP starting 32-36 weeks depending on severity. Delivery timing: diet-controlled at 39-40 weeks; medication-requiring at 37-39 weeks. Postpartum: screen for T2DM at 4-12 weeks (75g OGTT), then every 1-3 years.

guidelines.section_title: MDD Initial Treatment in Primary Care
guidelines.recommendation_text: Mild depression: watchful waiting 2 weeks or psychotherapy (CBT, IPT). Moderate-severe: pharmacotherapy ± psychotherapy. First-line: SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram) — best balance of efficacy/tolerability. Alternative: SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine), bupropion, mirtazapine. Start low, monitor at 2 and 4 weeks. Response assessment with PHQ-9 (≥50% reduction = response, <5 = remission). If inadequate response at 4-6 weeks at adequate dose: optimize dose, switch, or augment. Duration: continue ≥6-9 months after remission (12+ months for recurrent).

guidelines.section_title: Thoracic and Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Management
guidelines.recommendation_text: AAA surveillance: 3.0-3.9 cm Q3 years, 4.0-4.9 cm Q12 months, 5.0-5.4 cm Q6 months. Repair threshold: AAA ≥5.5 cm (men), ≥5.0 cm (women), or rapid expansion >0.5 cm/6 months. EVAR or open repair based on anatomy and patient factors. TAA: repair at ≥5.5 cm (6.0 for descending), lower threshold for connective tissue disease (Marfan ≥5.0 cm). Medical: BP control (beta-blocker preferred), statin, smoking cessation. Screen first-degree relatives of AAA patients.

First-line: gabapentinoids (pregabalin 150-600 mg/day, gabapentin 1200-3600 mg/day), TCAs (amitriptyline, nortriptyline 25-150 mg/day), SNRIs (duloxetine 60-120 mg/day, venlafaxine 150-225 mg/day). Topical: lidocaine 5% patch/cream, capsaicin 8% patch (applied by provider). Second-line: tramadol, combination therapy. Avoid: first-line opioids for chronic neuropathic pain. Diabetic neuropathy: duloxetine has FDA approval. PHN: pregabalin, gabapentin, lidocaine patch. Trigeminal neuralgia: carbamazepine first-line.

Short sleep (<7 hours) and long sleep (>9 hours) associated with increased CV risk. AHA Life's Essential 8 includes sleep health. OSA is independent CV risk factor: associated with resistant HTN, AF, HF, stroke. Screen cardiac patients for OSA (STOP-BANG ≥3). CPAP improves BP in resistant hypertension with OSA. Central sleep apnea in HF: adaptive servo-ventilation contraindicated in HFrEF (SERVE-HF). Circadian disruption (shift work): associated with metabolic syndrome, CVD.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Diabetes and Chronic Kidney Disease (2025)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Target HbA1c <7% (individualize for CKD stage). First-line glucose-lowering: metformin (adjust for eGFR: reduce at <45, stop at <30). SGLT2 inhibitor for eGFR ≥20 with albuminuria (regardless of diabetes control). GLP-1 RA: cardiovascular and renal benefit. Finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) for T2DM with albuminuria on max RAS blockade. BP target <130/80 with ACEi or ARB. Monitor: eGFR, UACR, potassium Q3-6 months. Avoid NSAIDs. Referral to nephrology at eGFR <30.

Post-PCI with DES: DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12i) minimum 6 months for stable CAD, 12 months for ACS. Short DAPT (1-3 months) then P2Y12 monotherapy for high bleeding risk. Extended DAPT (>12 months) for high ischemic risk without high bleeding risk. Ticagrelor preferred over clopidogrel for ACS. DAPT score guides extension decision. De-escalation from ticagrelor to clopidogrel at 1-3 months per POPular-AGE and HOST-REDUCE trials.

guidelines.section_title: Diuretic Strategy in Acute Decompensated HF
guidelines.recommendation_text: IV loop diuretic (furosemide 40 mg IV if diuretic-naive, or ≥home dose IV if on oral). Assess response: UO ≥100 mL/h in first 2 hours. Insufficient response: double dose or add thiazide (metolazone 2.5-5 mg or HCTZ 25 mg PO). Acetazolamide 500 mg IV daily improves decongestion (ADVOR trial). Monitor daily weights, I&Os, electrolytes (K, Mg, Na), creatinine. Transition to oral when euvolemic.

Conservative: graduated compression stockings (20-30 mmHg), leg elevation, exercise, skin care. Pharmacotherapy: micronized purified flavonoid fraction (MPFF) for symptom relief. Superficial venous reflux: thermal ablation (laser or radiofrequency) or foam sclerotherapy preferred over stripping. Deep venous obstruction: iliac vein stenting. Venous leg ulcers: compression therapy (multilayer bandage system), pentoxifylline adjunct. Wound care: moist environment, debridement as needed.

Intrahepatic: surgical resection if feasible. Adjuvant: capecitabine x 6 months (BILCAP). Unresectable/metastatic: gemcitabine + cisplatin + durvalumab (TOPAZ-1). IDH1-mutated: ivosidenib. FGFR2 fusion: pemigatinib, futibatinib, or erdafitinib. MSI-H: pembrolizumab. HER2-amplified: trastuzumab deruxtecan. Biliary drainage (ERCP/PTBD) for obstructive jaundice. Perihilar (Klatskin): assess resectability by Bismuth-Corlette classification. Locoregional: TARE (Y-90) or EBRT.

Low-grade (G1-G2) well-differentiated: surgical resection if feasible. Somatostatin analogs (octreotide LAR, lanreotide) for tumor control and symptom management (carcinoid syndrome). Targeted: everolimus, sunitinib (pancreatic NET). PRRT: lutetium-177 dotatate (Lutathera) for somatostatin receptor-positive progressive NET after SSA. High-grade (G3) neuroendocrine carcinoma: platinum-based chemo (cisplatin + etoposide). Chromogranin A and 5-HIAA for monitoring.

Epithelioid histology better prognosis. Surgery (EPP or P/D) for selected early-stage patients + multimodal therapy. Non-surgical: nivolumab + ipilimumab (CheckMate 743, first-line for unresectable). Alternative: pemetrexed + cisplatin/carboplatin ± bevacizumab. Radiation for pain palliation or post-surgical. BAP1 loss: may predict better IO response. Pleurodesis for recurrent effusion. Occupational exposure history: asbestos, erionite.

Uncomplicated: outpatient treatment for immunocompetent with oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or ciprofloxacin + metronidazole) x 7-10 days. Antibiotics may be omitted for mild uncomplicated (DIABOLO, AVOD trials). CT abdomen for diagnosis. Complicated (abscess >3 cm): CT-guided percutaneous drainage + antibiotics. Complicated (perforation, peritonitis): emergent surgery (Hartmann procedure or primary anastomosis ± diversion). Elective sigmoid colectomy for recurrent complicated or immunosuppressed.

Serologic screening: tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) + total IgA. If IgA deficient: deamidated gliadin peptide IgG. Confirm with duodenal biopsy (Marsh classification ≥2). Must be on gluten-containing diet during testing. Treatment: strict lifelong gluten-free diet (GFD). Dietitian referral essential. Monitor: tTG-IgA at 6-12 months (should normalize). Screen for: iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium, thyroid, DEXA. First-degree relatives: screen serologically. Refractory: confirm adherence, then consider immunosuppression.

First-line: budesonide 9 mg/day x 6-8 weeks, then taper over 2-3 months. High response rate (>80%). Relapse common on taper — low-dose maintenance (3-6 mg/day) for most patients. Refractory: cholestyramine, bismuth subsalicylate. Second-line immunosuppression: azathioprine, methotrexate. Biologics (TNF inhibitors, vedolizumab) for severe refractory. Remove offending medications (NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs). Diagnosis: random biopsies from grossly normal colon showing collagenous or lymphocytic pattern.

RAS blockade: ACEi or ARB to max tolerated dose. SGLT2 inhibitor: dapagliflozin or empagliflozin if eGFR ≥20. Finerenone: for persistent albuminuria (UACR ≥30) on max RAS blockade. GLP-1 RA: semaglutide or liraglutide for added cardio-renal benefit. BP target: <130/80. HbA1c target: <7% (individualize). Avoid NSAIDs. Refer to nephrology at eGFR <30 or persistent A3 albuminuria. Monitor K+ closely with finerenone and RAS blockade.

Induction: basiliximab (low immunologic risk) or thymoglobulin (high risk). Maintenance triple therapy: tacrolimus (trough 5-8 at 3 months) + mycophenolate 1g BID + prednisone taper. Steroid withdrawal protocol for low-risk. Belatacept-based for CNI avoidance (Nulojix). Rejection surveillance: protocol biopsies or donor-derived cell-free DNA (dd-cfDNA). Treat acute rejection: methylprednisolone pulse, thymoglobulin for steroid-resistant. BK virus screening Q1-3 months year 1. CMV prophylaxis.

Strongly recommended: exercise (aerobic + strengthening), weight loss if overweight, self-management education. Conditionally recommended: topical NSAIDs (knee), oral NSAIDs (lowest effective dose, shortest duration), intra-articular corticosteroid (short-term), duloxetine. Conditionally against: acetaminophen (limited efficacy), hyaluronic acid injections, glucosamine/chondroitin. Strongly against: opioids for OA. Total joint replacement for failed conservative management with severe functional limitation.

First-line: NSAIDs (continuous if active, on-demand if controlled). PT and exercise (spine mobility, posture, swimming). Second-line (NSAID failure): bDMARD — TNF inhibitor (adalimumab, certolizumab, golimumab, etanercept) or IL-17i (secukinumab, ixekizumab). JAK inhibitor (tofacitinib, upadacitinib) as alternative. No role for conventional DMARDs (MTX, SSZ) for axial disease. Sacroiliitis on MRI for non-radiographic axSpA. Biologics equally effective for radiographic and non-radiographic axSpA.

CBT adapted for older adults (simplified homework, larger print materials, slower pace). SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram) first-line — start at half adult dose. Buspirone for generalized anxiety without depression. AVOID benzodiazepines in elderly (falls, cognitive impairment, paradoxical agitation, Beers criteria). SNRIs (duloxetine, venlafaxine) for comorbid pain. Pregabalin for anxiety with neuropathic pain. Screen for comorbid depression and cognitive impairment.

Screen with EPDS or PHQ-9 at first prenatal visit, third trimester, and postpartum visits (6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months). Mild: psychotherapy (CBT, IPT). Moderate-severe: SSRI (sertraline preferred — lowest infant exposure in breast milk). Bruxanolone (IV allopregnanolone) for severe postpartum depression. Zuranolone (oral neuroactive steroid) newly approved for PPD (14-day course). Avoid paroxetine in first trimester (cardiac malformation risk). Lithium: avoid breastfeeding. Refer to perinatal psychiatrist for severe.

Epinephrine IM (anterolateral thigh) 0.3-0.5 mg (1:1000) — FIRST-LINE, give immediately. Repeat Q5-15 minutes if needed. Position: supine with legs elevated (unless respiratory distress). Adjuncts: IV fluids 1-2 L bolus, albuterol for bronchospasm, H1 (diphenhydramine) + H2 blocker (famotidine). Glucocorticoids (methylprednisolone) do NOT prevent biphasic reactions (limited evidence). Observe 4-6 hours (longer for severe). Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector + allergist referral. Tryptase level within 1-2 hours to confirm.

Acute single ingestion: 4-hour APAP level, plot on Rumack-Matthew nomogram. Above treatment line: NAC (N-acetylcysteine). IV protocol: 150 mg/kg over 1h, then 50 mg/kg over 4h, then 100 mg/kg over 16h (modified 2-bag protocol: 200 mg/kg over 4h then 100 mg/kg over 16h). Oral: 140 mg/kg loading then 70 mg/kg Q4h x 17 additional doses. Massive ingestion (>500 mg/kg): prolonged NAC, recheck level. Fulminant hepatic failure: transplant evaluation. King's College Criteria for prognosis.

Definition: INR ≥1.5 + hepatic encephalopathy + no preexisting liver disease + illness <26 weeks. Transfer to transplant center. NAC for all ALF (beneficial even for non-APAP). Intracranial hypertension: elevate HOB 30°, avoid stimulation, mannitol or hypertonic saline for ICP >20. Coagulopathy: do NOT correct unless actively bleeding or pre-procedure. Infection prophylaxis is controversial. King's College Criteria or MELD for transplant listing. Consider auxiliary transplant. Continuous RRT for AKI.

ICP monitoring: EVD (gold standard, allows CSF drainage) or intraparenchymal bolt. Target: ICP <22 mmHg, CPP 60-70 mmHg. Tier 0: HOB 30°, head midline, avoid tight C-collars, treat pain/fever/seizures. Tier 1: CSF drainage, osmotherapy (mannitol 20% 0.25-1 g/kg or 23.4% NaCl 30 mL). Tier 2: moderate hypothermia (35-36°C), high-dose barbiturate coma (pentobarbital with EEG burst suppression). Tier 3: decompressive craniectomy. Avoid hypotension, hypoxia, hyperglycemia, fever.

Severe AA (any 2: ANC <500, plt <20k, retic <1%): matched sibling HSCT if age <40 (curative in 85-90%). No matched sibling: ATG (horse, preferred over rabbit) + cyclosporine + eltrombopag. Eltrombopag improves response rate (RACE trial). Haploidentical HSCT increasingly successful for young patients without matched donor. Monitor for clonal evolution (MDS/AML, PNH). Supportive: transfusions (CMV-negative, irradiated), growth factors.

Early favorable (stage I-II, no bulk): ABVD x 2-4 cycles ± ISRT (20-30 Gy). Early unfavorable: ABVD x 4-6 cycles + ISRT. Advanced (stage III-IV): BV-AVD (brentuximab vedotin + AVD) x 6 cycles per ECHELON-1 (superior to ABVD). Interim PET after 2 cycles guides response-adapted therapy. Relapsed/refractory: salvage chemo (ICE, DHAP) → ASCT → maintenance brentuximab vedotin. Post-ASCT relapse: pembrolizumab, nivolumab. CAR-T (developmental).

First-line: R-CHOP x 6 cycles (rituximab + cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone). Pola-R-CHP (polatuzumab vedotin replacing vincristine) non-inferior with trend to improved PFS (POLARIX). Young high-risk (IPI ≥3): consider dose-adjusted R-EPOCH. CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal MTX or high-dose IV MTX for high CNS IPI or testicular/renal/adrenal involvement. Relapsed/refractory: salvage chemo → ASCT, or CAR-T (axi-cel, liso-cel, tisa-cel) for R/R after 2 lines. Bispecific antibodies: glofitamab, epcoritamab.

Diagnosis: insulin tolerance test (gold standard) or glucagon stimulation test or macimorelin test. Peak GH <3-5 ng/mL confirms GHD. Treatment: recombinant GH (somatropin) starting 0.2-0.3 mg/day SC, titrate by IGF-1 level (target mid-normal for age). Benefits: improved body composition, bone density, QoL, lipid profile. Monitor: IGF-1 Q1-2 months during titration, then Q6 months. Screen for diabetes (GH increases insulin resistance). Contraindicated: active malignancy, diabetic retinopathy, benign intracranial hypertension.

Burch-Wartofsky score ≥45 = thyroid storm. ICU admission. PTU 500-1000 mg loading then 250 mg Q4h (preferred over methimazole: also blocks T4→T3 conversion). Iodine (SSKI or Lugol solution) 1 hour AFTER PTU (to prevent iodine utilization for hormone synthesis). Beta-blocker: propranolol (also blocks T4→T3) or esmolol infusion. Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV Q8h (relative adrenal insufficiency and blocks T4→T3). Cooling for hyperthermia (avoid aspirin — displaces T4 from TBG). Cholestyramine to reduce enterohepatic recirculation.

In situ: 5-10 mm margin (consider Mohs for head/neck). ≤1.0 mm: 1 cm margin. 1.01-2.0 mm: 1-2 cm margin. >2.0 mm: 2 cm margin. SLN biopsy: recommended ≥0.8 mm Breslow depth or <0.8 mm with ulceration/high mitotic rate. Mohs micrographic surgery or staged excision for lentigo maligna (face). Complete circumferential peripheral and deep margin assessment (CCPDMA) for melanoma in situ of special sites.

Low-risk BCC (trunk/extremities, well-defined, nodular, <2 cm): standard excision with 4 mm margins, or curettage and electrodesiccation. High-risk (face, recurrent, morpheaform, perineural): Mohs micrographic surgery (tissue-sparing, highest cure rate 99%). Superficial BCC: topical imiquimod, 5-FU, or PDT. Locally advanced unresectable: hedgehog pathway inhibitor (vismodegib, sonidegib). Metastatic (rare): cemiplimab (anti-PD-1). Gorlin syndrome patients: avoid radiation.

Diagnosis: Pediatric Appendicitis Score (PAS), ultrasound first (avoid CT in children). Uncomplicated: laparoscopic appendectomy (standard) OR non-operative management with antibiotics (APPY trial — ~75% success at 1 year). Perforated with abscess: antibiotics + percutaneous drainage → interval appendectomy at 6-8 weeks (or no interval surgery per emerging evidence). Limit CT use: ultrasound sensitivity improves with experienced sonographers. MRI without contrast as alternative.

Acute: ibuprofen (10 mg/kg, most evidence in children) or acetaminophen. Triptans: almotriptan approved ≥12, rizatriptan ≥6, zolmitriptan nasal ≥12. Prevent MOH: limit acute meds to <10 days/month. Preventive (≥4 headache days/month): cognitive behavioral therapy (CHAMP trial: CBT + amitriptyline superior to amitriptyline alone). Pharmacologic: topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol. CGRP antibodies: limited pediatric data but emerging. Lifestyle: regular sleep, meals, hydration, exercise, screen time limits.

Genetic testing for all CRC <50, endometrial cancer <60, or meeting revised Bethesda criteria. MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM testing. Surveillance if mutation confirmed: colonoscopy Q1-2 years starting age 20-25. Consider EGD Q2-3 years for upper GI cancers. Urine cytology annually. Prophylactic hysterectomy and BSO after childbearing for women (reduces endometrial and ovarian cancer risk). Aspirin 100 mg daily may reduce CRC risk (CAPP2 trial).

Breast surveillance: annual breast MRI + mammogram starting age 25-30. Risk-reducing mastectomy reduces breast cancer risk by ~95%. Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) at 35-40 (BRCA1) or 40-45 (BRCA2) reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80% and breast cancer risk by ~50%. PARP inhibitors for BRCA-mutated breast (olaparib adjuvant per OlympiA) and ovarian cancer. Screen for prostate cancer (BRCA2 men) and pancreatic cancer (annual MRI/EUS if family history).

6-step graduated return-to-sport: 1) Symptom-limited activity (daily activities that don't provoke symptoms). 2) Light aerobic exercise (walking, stationary cycling). 3) Sport-specific exercise (running drills, no contact). 4) Non-contact training drills (passing, complex drills). 5) Full-contact practice (cleared by physician). 6) Return to competition. Minimum 24 hours per step. If symptoms recur, return to previous asymptomatic step. No same-day return to play. Neuropsych testing for persistent symptoms. No pharmacologic intervention for acute concussion.

Prevention: risk assessment (Braden Scale ≤18 = at risk). Interventions: repositioning Q2h, support surface (pressure redistribution mattress), nutrition optimization (protein 1.25-1.5 g/kg/day, calories 30-35 kcal/kg/day), moisture management, friction reduction. Treatment by stage: Stage 1/2: moist wound healing (hydrocolloid, foam). Stage 3/4: debridement (sharp, enzymatic, autolytic), negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT), address infection. Unstageable: debride to determine depth. Offloading is essential.

Detailed history: timing, morphology, severity of reaction. Penicillin allergy: >90% of labeled patients are NOT truly allergic. Skin testing + oral challenge (10% dose → full dose) safely delabels. Cross-reactivity: penicillin → cephalosporin ~2% (similar R1 side chain). Anaphylaxis to drug: absolute contraindication unless desensitization performed. Desensitization: gradual dose escalation for immediate-type allergy when no alternative exists (e.g., aspirin for CAD, carboplatin for ovarian cancer). Document: allergy delabeling in medical record.

Acute attacks: C1-INH concentrate (Berinert IV, Ruconest IV, Haegarda SC), icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist SC), or ecallantide (kallikrein inhibitor SC). On-demand treatment at earliest symptom onset. Do NOT use epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids (bradykinin-mediated, not histamine). Long-term prophylaxis: lanadelumab (anti-kallikrein mAb SC Q2-4 weeks), berotralstat (oral kallikrein inhibitor daily), SC C1-INH. Short-term prophylaxis before procedures: IV C1-INH. Diagnosis: low C4, low C1-INH level and function.

Most common cause of vertigo. Posterior canal (most common): positive Dix-Hallpike test. Treatment: Epley maneuver (canalith repositioning procedure) — 80% cure rate in single session. Horizontal canal: positive supine roll test; treatment: Lempert (BBQ roll) or Gufoni maneuver. No role for routine imaging, audiometry, or vestibular testing unless atypical features. Medication (meclizine) NOT recommended for BPPV (delays central compensation). Refer for persistent symptoms: consider vestibular rehabilitation.

FNA indications guided by ACR TI-RADS: TR5 (highly suspicious) ≥1 cm, TR4 (moderately suspicious) ≥1.5 cm, TR3 (mildly suspicious) ≥2.5 cm. No FNA for TR1-TR2. Bethesda classification: I nondiagnostic (repeat FNA), II benign (follow-up US), III atypia/FLUS (molecular testing or repeat FNA), IV follicular neoplasm (lobectomy or molecular), V suspicious (lobectomy or total thyroidectomy), VI malignant (surgery). Molecular testing (ThyroSeq, Afirma) for Bethesda III-IV to avoid unnecessary surgery.

Taper plan: reduce by 10-25% every 1-2 weeks initially, then slower (5-10% reductions) for final 25%. Switch short-acting (alprazolam, lorazepam) to equivalent long-acting (diazepam, clonazepam) before taper for smoother pharmacokinetics. Adjunctive: gabapentin or carbamazepine for withdrawal symptoms. CBT for insomnia (CBT-I) and anxiety concurrently. Monitor: CIWA-B (for benzodiazepine withdrawal). Avoid abrupt discontinuation (seizure risk after >1 month of daily use). Total taper duration: typically 4-16 weeks depending on duration of use.

Partial-thickness or small full-thickness (<1 cm) in low-demand patient: conservative (PT focusing on ROM and strengthening x 6-12 weeks, subacromial injection). Large/massive or failed conservative in active patients: arthroscopic rotator cuff repair. Irreparable tears: superior capsular reconstruction, tendon transfer (latissimus dorsi, lower trapezius), or reverse total shoulder arthroplasty for cuff tear arthropathy. Postoperative: sling x 6 weeks, progressive rehab x 4-6 months. MRI for diagnosis.

Non-pharmacologic (most important): aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, cycling 30 min 3-5x/week), CBT, sleep hygiene, stress management, patient education. Pharmacologic: duloxetine 60 mg (FDA-approved), milnacipran (FDA-approved), pregabalin 150-450 mg (FDA-approved). Amitriptyline 10-50 mg at bedtime. Cyclobenzaprine for sleep. Avoid: opioids (not effective, risk of dependence), NSAIDs (limited benefit). Address comorbidities: depression, anxiety, sleep disorders. Multidisciplinary approach.

Chlamydia/Gonorrhea: screen all sexually active women <25 annually, men per risk. Chlamydia: doxycycline 100 mg BID x 7 days (preferred over azithromycin). Gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (1g if ≥150 kg). Syphilis primary/secondary: benzathine penicillin G 2.4 million units IM single dose. Latent (early <1 year): same. Late latent or unknown: 2.4 million units IM weekly x 3. Neurosyphilis: IV penicillin G. Trichomonas: metronidazole 500 mg BID x 7 days (women) or 2g single dose (men).

Adults: ≥150 min/week moderate-intensity aerobic (brisk walking, cycling) or ≥75 min/week vigorous-intensity. Resistance training: ≥2 days/week, all major muscle groups. Flexibility: ≥2 days/week. Neuromotor (balance): for fall-prone older adults. Move more, sit less — any physical activity provides health benefits. Dose-response: more activity provides greater benefit up to ~300 min/week moderate. Exercise is medicine: prescribe like a medication (type, dose, frequency, duration). Screen for contraindications per ACSM algorithm.

Beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are first-line for symptomatic obstructive HCM. Mavacamten may be considered for patients with refractory symptoms despite standard therapy.

ICD implantation is recommended for HCM patients with prior cardiac arrest or sustained ventricular tachycardia. Risk stratification should incorporate family history, unexplained syncope, LV wall thickness ≥30 mm, nonsustained VT, and abnormal BP response to exercise.

Colchicine combined with NSAIDs or aspirin is recommended as first-line therapy for acute pericarditis. Duration of colchicine is typically 3 months. Corticosteroids should be avoided as first-line therapy due to risk of recurrence.

Antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures is recommended only for patients at highest risk: prosthetic heart valves, prior endocarditis, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, or cardiac transplant with valvulopathy. Amoxicillin 2 g PO 30-60 minutes before procedure is standard.

Vagal maneuvers are first-line for acute termination of SVT. If ineffective, IV adenosine (6 mg rapid push, may repeat 12 mg) is recommended. For hemodynamically unstable SVT, synchronized cardioversion is indicated.

Beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers are first-line anti-ischemic therapy for stable angina. Sublingual nitroglycerin for acute symptom relief. Ranolazine may be added as second-line for persistent symptoms.

PFO closure may be considered for patients aged 18-60 with cryptogenic stroke and PFO with high-risk features (atrial septal aneurysm, large shunt). Antiplatelet therapy alone is an alternative. Shared decision-making is essential.

Systemic corticosteroids are first-line for symptomatic pulmonary sarcoidosis with progressive disease. Methotrexate or azathioprine may be used as steroid-sparing agents. Treatment duration is typically 12+ months with gradual taper.

Airway clearance techniques are the cornerstone of bronchiectasis management. Long-term macrolide therapy (azithromycin) reduces exacerbation frequency. Inhaled antibiotics may be considered for chronic Pseudomonas colonization.

Workers with silica exposure should undergo periodic screening with chest radiography and spirometry. Removal from exposure is the primary intervention. No pharmacotherapy reverses established silicosis; supportive care and pulmonary rehabilitation are mainstays.

Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 > 90% is recommended for Group 3 PH. PAH-specific vasodilators are generally not recommended for Group 3 PH except in select cases with severe hemodynamic impairment confirmed by right heart catheterization.

MAC lung disease treatment includes macrolide-based regimen (azithromycin + ethambutol + rifampin) for at least 12 months after sputum culture conversion. M. abscessus requires IV amikacin + imipenem or cefoxitin induction followed by oral macrolide-based maintenance.

Prednisone (or budesonide for non-cirrhotic patients) combined with azathioprine is standard induction therapy for autoimmune hepatitis. Biochemical remission (normalization of ALT and IgG) should be achieved before considering maintenance therapy.

Diagnosis requires a combination of low ceruloplasmin, elevated 24-hour urine copper, and hepatic copper content on biopsy. D-penicillamine or trientine are first-line chelation therapy. Zinc maintenance may be used after initial decoppering.

Pneumatic dilation and laparoscopic Heller myotomy are the primary treatment options for type I and II achalasia. Per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM) is an effective alternative. Botulinum toxin injection is reserved for patients unfit for definitive therapy.

Non-dysplastic Barrett esophagus requires surveillance EGD every 3-5 years. Low-grade dysplasia warrants endoscopic eradication therapy or surveillance every 6-12 months. High-grade dysplasia should undergo endoscopic eradication (radiofrequency ablation or endoscopic mucosal resection).

Dietary modification (small, frequent, low-fat, low-fiber meals) is first-line. Metoclopramide is the only FDA-approved prokinetic; use should be limited to lowest effective dose for shortest duration. Gastric electrical stimulation may be considered for refractory symptoms.

Lifestyle modification with weight loss of ≥7-10% is the primary treatment for MASLD/NASH. Resmetirom is approved for non-cirrhotic NASH with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis. Pioglitazone and vitamin E may be considered for biopsy-proven NASH in select patients.

H. pylori test-and-treat is first-line. If negative, PPI trial for 4-8 weeks. Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10-25 mg) are second-line for PPI non-responders. Prokinetics may benefit patients with postprandial distress syndrome.

At least two first-line screening tests should be abnormal: 24h urine free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, or 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Once confirmed, ACTH levels and imaging differentiate ACTH-dependent from ACTH-independent causes.

Alpha-adrenergic blockade (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) for minimum 10-14 days before surgery. Beta-blockers added only after adequate alpha-blockade to prevent hypertensive crisis. Liberal salt and fluid intake to expand intravascular volume.

Transsphenoidal surgery is first-line for most GH-secreting pituitary adenomas. Somatostatin receptor ligands (octreotide LAR, lanreotide) are first-line medical therapy if surgery is not curative. Pegvisomant is an alternative for persistently elevated IGF-1.

Level 1 hypoglycemia (54-70 mg/dL) requires fast-acting carbohydrate (15-20 g glucose). Level 2 (<54 mg/dL) requires glucagon availability and caregiver education. Continuous glucose monitoring reduces hypoglycemia risk in insulin-treated patients.

Diagnosis requires 2 of 3 Rotterdam criteria: oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology. Combined OCPs are first-line for menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism. Metformin improves metabolic parameters; lifestyle modification is fundamental.

Active moderate-to-severe TED is treated with IV glucocorticoids (methylprednisolone pulse therapy). Teprotumumab (IGF-1R inhibitor) is effective for proptosis and diplopia. Selenium supplementation may benefit mild active disease. Urgent orbital decompression for sight-threatening disease.

IV isotonic saline hydration before and after contrast administration for patients at risk (eGFR <30, or eGFR 30-44 with additional risk factors). Use lowest possible contrast volume. Iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast agents preferred. Withhold metformin if eGFR <30.

Medical therapy (ACE inhibitors/ARBs, statins, antiplatelet agents) is first-line for atherosclerotic RAS. Revascularization with stenting is considered for refractory hypertension, declining renal function, or flash pulmonary edema not responsive to medical therapy.

Oral sodium bicarbonate supplementation is recommended for CKD patients with serum bicarbonate <22 mmol/L to slow GFR decline and improve nutritional status. Veverimer is a novel HCl binder under investigation. Dietary acid load reduction (fruits and vegetables) is an adjunct.

Riluzole is recommended for all ALS patients to modestly extend survival. Edaravone may be considered though evidence of meaningful clinical benefit is limited. Tofersen is approved for SOD1-ALS. Multidisciplinary clinic care improves outcomes and quality of life.

Clinical triad of gait disturbance, cognitive impairment, and urinary incontinence combined with ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal enlargement. Large-volume LP (30-50 mL) with pre/post gait assessment aids diagnosis. VP shunting is the definitive treatment.

Scheduled naps and good sleep hygiene are non-pharmacologic cornerstones. Modafinil or sodium oxybate are first-line pharmacotherapy. Solriamfetol and pitolisant are newer options. Sodium oxybate is the only therapy addressing both excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy.

Initial workup for distal symmetric polyneuropathy includes fasting glucose/HbA1c, B12, TSH, SPEP with immunofixation. Nerve conduction studies/EMG for atypical presentations. Treat underlying cause. Symptomatic treatment with duloxetine, pregabalin, or gabapentin.

IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days) or plasma exchange (5 sessions over 2 weeks) are equally effective. Do not combine IVIG and PE. Corticosteroids are not beneficial. Monitor respiratory function closely; intubate for FVC <20 mL/kg or declining by >30%.

guidelines.section_title: Systemic Sclerosis Skin and Lung Involvement
guidelines.recommendation_text: Mycophenolate is first-line for SSc-ILD. Nintedanib may be added for progressive fibrosis. Raynaud phenomenon managed with CCBs (nifedipine); PDE5 inhibitors for digital ulcers. Autologous HSCT considered for rapidly progressive diffuse disease.

Low-dose prednisone (12.5-25 mg/day) is first-line with gradual taper over 12-18 months. Methotrexate is recommended as steroid-sparing agent for patients with relapse, poor response, or steroid-related adverse effects. Screen for GCA symptoms at every visit.

High-dose prednisone (40-60 mg/day) initiated immediately upon clinical suspicion; do not delay for biopsy results. Tocilizumab is recommended as first-line steroid-sparing agent. Temporal artery biopsy or ultrasound for definitive diagnosis.

Artificial tears and saliva substitutes for sicca symptoms. Pilocarpine or cevimeline for moderate-severe oral dryness. Hydroxychloroquine for systemic manifestations (arthralgia, fatigue). Rituximab for severe extraglandular disease. Screen for lymphoma at regular intervals.

High-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) is first-line for PM/DM. Methotrexate or azathioprine as steroid-sparing agents. IVIG for refractory disease or dysphagia. Screen for malignancy (especially within first 3 years of DM diagnosis) and ILD with HRCT and PFTs.

Hydroxyurea is recommended for all patients ≥9 months with SCA (HbSS/HbSβ0) regardless of clinical severity. Voxelotor and crizanlizumab are add-on therapies. L-glutamine may reduce pain crises. Curative options include matched sibling donor HSCT and gene therapy.

Rapid pain assessment and IV opioid administration within 30 minutes of presentation. NSAIDs as adjunct. IV fluids at maintenance rate (avoid overhydration). Incentive spirometry to prevent acute chest syndrome. Blood transfusion for severe anemia or acute chest syndrome.

Observation for asymptomatic ITP with platelets >30,000. First-line: corticosteroids (dexamethasone 40 mg x4 days or prednisone 1 mg/kg). Second-line: TPO-RAs (eltrombopag, romiplostim), rituximab, or splenectomy. IVIG for severe bleeding or urgent platelet increase.

Immediate plasma exchange (PEX) is the cornerstone of TTP treatment. Caplacizumab (anti-vWF nanobody) reduces time to platelet normalization and recurrence. Corticosteroids and rituximab added for immune-mediated TTP. ADAMTS13 activity <10% confirms diagnosis.

Observation for asymptomatic lower-risk MDS. ESAs (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin) for symptomatic anemia with low endogenous EPO. Luspatercept for ESA-refractory anemia. Lenalidomide for del(5q) MDS. Hypomethylating agents for higher-risk MDS.

Prophylaxis with factor VIII replacement is standard of care for severe hemophilia A. Extended half-life products reduce infusion frequency. Emicizumab (bispecific antibody) is an alternative for prophylaxis regardless of inhibitor status. Gene therapy (valoctocogene roxaparvovec) is available for select adults.

Oral antivirals (valacyclovir 1g TID or acyclovir 800mg 5x/day) initiated within 72 hours of rash onset for 7 days. IV acyclovir for disseminated disease or immunocompromised patients. Postherpetic neuralgia managed with gabapentin, pregabalin, or topical lidocaine. Shingrix vaccine for prevention in adults ≥50.

Oseltamivir within 48 hours of symptom onset for high-risk patients, hospitalized patients, and those with severe illness. Baloxavir marboxil is a single-dose alternative. Peramivir IV for patients unable to take oral/inhaled antivirals. Do not delay treatment awaiting confirmatory testing.

Acute PJI (<30 days postoperative or <3 weeks symptom duration) may be treated with DAIR (debridement, antibiotics, implant retention). Chronic PJI requires two-stage exchange arthroplasty. Pathogen-specific antibiotic therapy for 6-12 weeks including rifampin for staphylococcal PJI with retained implant.

Confirm GAS pharyngitis with rapid antigen detection test or throat culture before antibiotic treatment. Amoxicillin or penicillin V for 10 days is first-line. Single IM benzathine penicillin G for non-adherent patients. Centor/McIsaac score guides testing need.

Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (1 g for patients ≥150 kg) is recommended for uncomplicated gonococcal infections (urogenital, rectal, pharyngeal). Test of cure at 7-14 days for pharyngeal gonorrhea. Test for chlamydia co-infection and treat if present.

Doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7 days is preferred for chlamydial urogenital, rectal, and oropharyngeal infections (superior to azithromycin for rectal chlamydia). Azithromycin 1 g single dose is an alternative. Test of cure at 4 weeks for pregnant patients.

Primary, secondary, and early latent syphilis: benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM single dose. Late latent or unknown duration: benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM weekly x3 weeks. Neurosyphilis: IV aqueous penicillin G 18-24 MU/day for 10-14 days.

Second-generation H1-antihistamines at standard dose are first-line. Up-titration to 4x standard dose if inadequate response. Omalizumab is recommended as add-on for antihistamine-refractory CSU. Cyclosporine is third-line. Avoid systemic corticosteroids for chronic use.

Immediate discontinuation of suspected causative agent for SJS/TEN. Transfer to burn unit for TEN (>30% BSA). IVIG or cyclosporine may be considered for TEN though evidence is limited. DRESS syndrome requires systemic corticosteroids. Document allergy and provide drug avoidance counseling.

Systemic antifungal therapy is required for tinea capitis (topical therapy alone is insufficient). Griseofulvin or terbinafine for 4-8 weeks depending on causative organism. Terbinafine preferred for Trichophyton; griseofulvin for Microsporum. Adjunctive antifungal shampoo reduces transmission.

Topical corticosteroids (class III-IV) are first-line for localized vitiligo. Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus) for facial/intertriginous areas. Ruxolitinib cream (JAK inhibitor) is FDA-approved for non-segmental vitiligo. Narrowband UVB phototherapy for widespread disease.

Surgery is indicated when visual impairment from cataract affects quality of life or interferes with essential activities. Phacoemulsification with posterior chamber IOL implantation is standard technique. Preoperative biometry for IOL power calculation. Topical NSAID + antibiotic postoperatively.

Step 1: Education, environmental modification, artificial tears, lid hygiene. Step 2: Cyclosporine or lifitegrast drops, punctal plugs. Step 3: Autologous serum tears, therapeutic contact lenses. Step 4: Systemic anti-inflammatory therapy, amniotic membrane. Assess for Meibomian gland dysfunction.

Risk-based approach using sepsis calculator or categorical risk factors for EOS evaluation. Empiric ampicillin + gentamicin for suspected EOS. Blood culture before antibiotics when possible. Discontinue antibiotics at 36-48 hours if cultures negative and infant is clinically well.

For infants at high risk (severe eczema or egg allergy), introduce peanut-containing foods around 4-6 months after evaluation (including possible skin prick testing). For moderate-risk infants, introduce around 6 months. Early introduction significantly reduces peanut allergy development.

PHQ-A screening in primary care for adolescents ≥12. Mild-moderate depression: active support, psychoeducation, and CBT. Fluoxetine is first-line pharmacotherapy; escitalopram approved for ≥12 years. Black box monitoring: weekly visits first month, biweekly for second month. Combination therapy (CBT + SSRI) is most effective for moderate-severe depression.

Universal screening for substance use at annual well-visits using validated tools (CRAFFT 2.1). Brief intervention for at-risk use. Referral to treatment for substance use disorder. Confidentiality protections for adolescents. Avoid urine drug screens without informed consent.

Intensive health behavior and lifestyle treatment (≥26 contact hours over 3-12 months) is recommended for children ≥6 with BMI ≥95th percentile. Pharmacotherapy (GLP-1 RAs) may be offered for ≥12 years with obesity. Metabolic/bariatric surgery for adolescents ≥13 with severe obesity and comorbidities.

Activate MTP for patients with hemorrhagic shock or anticipated need for >10 units pRBC in 24 hours. Target 1:1:1 ratio (pRBC:FFP:platelets). Tranexamic acid within 3 hours of injury. Limit crystalloid resuscitation. Viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM) for goal-directed therapy.

Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, early mobilization, early enteral nutrition (POD 0), and goal-directed fluid therapy. Alvimopan for accelerating GI recovery after bowel resection with primary anastomosis. Mechanical bowel prep with oral antibiotics reduces SSI for elective colorectal surgery.

Non-operative management (NGT decompression, IV fluids, NPO) for adhesive SBO without signs of strangulation. Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge at 24-48 hours: contrast in colon on follow-up X-ray predicts resolution. Surgical intervention for complete obstruction, signs of strangulation, or failure to resolve in 3-5 days.

Preoperative chlorhexidine bathing, appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 minutes of incision, intraoperative normothermia, glycemic control (<180 mg/dL), and supplemental oxygen. Weight-based antibiotic dosing and redosing for prolonged procedures. Avoid routine postoperative antibiotic extension.

Propofol and ketamine are first-line agents for ED procedural sedation. Continuous monitoring (pulse oximetry, capnography, cardiac monitor) is required. Fasting status does not alter management in emergency settings. Personnel skilled in airway management must be present. Pre-procedural risk assessment using ASA classification.

IV alteplase within 4.5 hours of symptom onset for eligible patients. Target door-to-needle time <60 minutes. Mechanical thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion up to 24 hours with favorable perfusion imaging. Tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg) is emerging as alternative to alteplase.

Universal screening with validated tools (Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale). Safety planning intervention before discharge for patients with suicidal ideation but low acute risk. Lethal means counseling (firearms, medications). Psychiatric consultation for moderate-high risk. No evidence supports mandatory prolonged observation for all presentations.

guidelines.section_title: ED Sepsis Recognition and Hour-1 Bundle
guidelines.recommendation_text: Measure lactate. Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics. Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour. Begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4. Reassess volume status and tissue perfusion. Vasopressors if MAP <65 after fluid resuscitation.

Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective monotherapy for moderate-severe allergic rhinitis. Second-generation antihistamines for mild symptoms. Allergen immunotherapy (subcutaneous or sublingual) for patients with inadequate response to pharmacotherapy. Avoid first-generation antihistamines due to sedation.

Nasal saline irrigation and intranasal corticosteroids are first-line. For CRSwNP, biologic therapy (dupilumab, omalizumab, mepolizumab) is recommended for refractory cases. Short-course oral corticosteroids for acute exacerbations. Endoscopic sinus surgery for persistent symptoms despite medical therapy.

Phenotype-guided biologic selection: omalizumab for allergic asthma (elevated IgE), mepolizumab/benralizumab for eosinophilic asthma (blood eos ≥150), dupilumab for Type 2 inflammation (eos ≥150 or FeNO ≥25), tezepelumab for broad severe asthma population regardless of phenotype.

Dupilumab is first-line systemic therapy for moderate-severe AD in adults and children ≥6 months. JAK inhibitors (abrocitinib, upadacitinib) are alternatives. Tralokinumab for adults. Traditional immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, methotrexate) are less preferred due to safety profile.

Regular medication review to identify potentially inappropriate medications. Apply STOPPFrail or Beers Criteria for deprescribing candidates. Prioritize deprescribing PPIs, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, and medications without ongoing indication. Taper gradually; monitor for withdrawal or return of symptoms.

Non-pharmacologic multicomponent prevention (HELP model): reorientation, early mobilization, sleep promotion, hydration, vision/hearing optimization. Treat underlying cause. Avoid antipsychotics unless severe agitation threatening safety. Low-dose haloperidol if absolutely necessary; avoid in QTc prolongation or Lewy body dementia.

Annual falls screening for community-dwelling adults ≥65. Multifactorial risk assessment: gait/balance testing, medication review, orthostatic vitals, visual acuity, home safety. Exercise programs (balance + strength) reduce falls 23%. Vitamin D supplementation if deficient. Review/reduce fall-risk medications.

Screen all persons aged 15-65 and all pregnant persons. Younger adolescents and older adults at increased risk should also be screened. Fourth-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination assays are recommended. Repeat screening based on ongoing risk factors.

Screen all adults aged 18-79 for hepatitis C at least once. One-time HCV antibody test; if reactive, confirm with HCV RNA. All persons with active HCV should be linked to treatment with direct-acting antivirals regardless of fibrosis stage.

Screen adults aged 35-70 who are overweight or obese. Fasting plasma glucose, HbA1c, or oral glucose tolerance test. Refer patients with prediabetes to intensive behavioral counseling (Diabetes Prevention Program). Rescreen every 3 years if initial results are normal.

Biennial screening mammography for women aged 40-74. Decision to start at 40 should be individualized considering risk factors. Insufficient evidence for screening after age 74. Supplemental MRI screening for women with extremely dense breasts or high lifetime risk (>20%).

guidelines.guideline: USPSTF Statin Use for CVD Prevention (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Prescribe statins for adults aged 40-75 with one or more CVD risk factors (dyslipidemia, diabetes, hypertension, smoking) and 10-year ASCVD risk ≥10%. Moderate-intensity statin is appropriate for primary prevention. Shared decision-making for 7.5-10% risk range.

150-300 minutes/week moderate-intensity or 75-150 minutes/week vigorous-intensity aerobic activity. Resistance training ≥2 days/week targeting major muscle groups. Additional benefits with flexibility and balance exercises for older adults. Pre-exercise evaluation for individuals with known CVD or high risk.

Multimodal pain management combining physical therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, non-opioid pharmacotherapy (acetaminophen, NSAIDs, duloxetine, gabapentinoids), and interventional procedures. Opioids reserved for refractory cases with careful risk-benefit assessment and ongoing monitoring. Implement ORT and PDMP checks.

Self-care education and reassurance. NSAIDs as first-line pharmacotherapy. Skeletal muscle relaxants as short-term adjunct. Avoid imaging in absence of red flags (progressive neurological deficit, suspected cancer, cauda equina). Physical therapy for subacute/chronic LBP. Avoid opioids for acute nonspecific LBP.

ESIs provide short-term relief for radicular pain associated with disc herniation or spinal stenosis. Fluoroscopic guidance is standard for accuracy and safety. Limit to 3-4 injections per year. Not recommended for axial back pain without radiculopathy. Consider in context of comprehensive pain management plan.

Triple therapy with calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus preferred), antimetabolite (mycophenolate), and corticosteroids. Steroid withdrawal protocols considered after 3-6 months for low-risk kidney transplant recipients. Drug level monitoring essential for tacrolimus (target varies by transplant type and time post-transplant).

CMV prophylaxis (valganciclovir) for D+/R- or high-risk recipients for 3-6 months. PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) for 6-12 months. Hepatitis B prophylaxis if indicated. Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination. Avoid live vaccines while on immunosuppression.

Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is recommended as first-line treatment over pharmacotherapy. CBT-I components: sleep restriction, stimulus control, cognitive restructuring, sleep hygiene, relaxation techniques. Digital CBT-I platforms are effective alternatives when in-person therapy is unavailable.

Iron supplementation if ferritin <75 ng/mL. Alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin enacarbil, pregabalin) are preferred first-line over dopamine agonists (DA augmentation risk). Low-dose DA (pramipexole, ropinirole) as alternative. Opioids (low-dose) for refractory cases. Avoid antihistamines and SSRIs which may worsen RLS.

Endovenous thermal ablation (laser or radiofrequency) is preferred over surgical stripping for great saphenous vein incompetence. Sclerotherapy for reticular and spider veins. Compression stockings for symptom management but not curative. Duplex ultrasound mapping before intervention.

Type A (ascending aorta): emergent surgical repair. Type B uncomplicated: medical management with IV beta-blockers targeting HR <60 and SBP <120. Complicated Type B (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain/HTN): thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR). CT angiography is diagnostic imaging of choice.

Remove from play immediately upon suspicion. No return to sport same day. Graduated return-to-sport protocol: symptom-limited activity → light aerobic → sport-specific → non-contact drills → full contact → return to competition. Each stage minimum 24 hours. Neuropsychological testing may aid clinical decision-making.

Rectal temperature is gold standard (>40.5°C). Immediate cold water immersion (CWI) before transport; cool first, transport second. Target cooling to <39°C within 30 minutes. Do not administer antipyretics (ineffective for exertional heat stroke). Monitor for rhabdomyolysis, DIC, and organ failure.

Buprenorphine or methadone are first-line MOUD for OUD. Buprenorphine can be initiated in office-based settings (X-waiver eliminated). Extended-release naltrexone is an alternative for patients who have achieved abstinence. MOUD should be offered indefinitely; no mandatory tapering timeline.

No FDA-approved pharmacotherapy for stimulant use disorder. Contingency management (motivational incentives) has strongest evidence. CBT and community reinforcement approach are effective. Mirtazapine and topiramate under investigation. Harm reduction approaches including naloxone distribution for polysubstance use.

Offloading with total contact cast or irremovable knee-high device is gold standard for neuropathic plantar ulcers. Moist wound healing environment. Debridement of devitalized tissue. Assess for PAD (ABI/toe pressures). Revascularization for ischemic ulcers. Cellular and tissue-based products for stalled wounds.

Compression therapy (multicomponent bandaging or stockings providing 30-40 mmHg) is the foundation of treatment. Pentoxifylline as adjunct pharmacotherapy. Treat venous insufficiency with ablation when appropriate. Skin grafting for ulcers failing to heal after 4-6 weeks of standard care.

Assess for modifiable risk factors (cardiovascular, metabolic, psychogenic). PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) are first-line pharmacotherapy. Vacuum erection devices as non-invasive alternative. Intracavernosal injection (alprostadil) for PDE5i non-responders. Penile prosthesis for refractory cases. Screen for cardiovascular risk.

TURP remains gold standard for moderate-severe BPH. HoLEP/ThuLEP for large prostates (>80 mL). Minimally invasive options (UroLift, Rezum, Aquablation) for select patients wanting to preserve ejaculatory function. Simple prostatectomy (open/robotic) for very large prostates (>150 mL).

Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12 mg IM x2, 24h apart) for threatened preterm birth 24-34 weeks. Tocolysis (nifedipine or indomethacin <32 weeks) to allow steroid course completion. Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection at <32 weeks. GBS prophylaxis if GBS status unknown.

Call for help immediately. Avoid fundal pressure. McRoberts maneuver + suprapubic pressure as initial steps. If unresolved: delivery of posterior arm, rotational maneuvers (Rubin II, Woods screw). Zavanelli maneuver as last resort. Document timing, maneuvers used, and neonatal status.

Methotrexate single-dose (50 mg/m2 IM) for unruptured ectopic with hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity, and ectopic <3.5 cm. Surgical management (salpingostomy or salpingectomy) for ruptured ectopic, hemodynamic instability, or methotrexate contraindication/failure. Serial hCG monitoring until undetectable.

Nutritional counseling and exercise first-line. Glucose targets: fasting <95, 1-hour postprandial <140, 2-hour <120 mg/dL. Insulin is preferred pharmacotherapy if lifestyle measures insufficient. Metformin and glyburide are alternatives if patient declines insulin. Antenatal testing from 32 weeks.

Exposure and response prevention (ERP) is first-line psychotherapy. SSRIs at higher doses than typical depression doses (e.g., fluoxetine 40-80 mg). Clomipramine as second-line or augmentation. SRI trial should last 8-12 weeks at adequate dose before declaring failure. Deep brain stimulation for severe refractory cases.

Stimulant medications (methylphenidate or amphetamine formulations) are first-line. Long-acting formulations preferred for consistent coverage. Non-stimulant alternatives: atomoxetine, viloxazine, guanfacine XR. Assess for coexisting conditions (anxiety, substance use) before stimulant initiation. Cardiovascular screening before starting stimulants.

CBT-Enhanced (CBT-E) is first-line psychotherapy. Fluoxetine 60 mg is the only FDA-approved medication for bulimia nervosa. Guided self-help CBT as alternative for mild cases. Medical monitoring for electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia), dental erosion. Nutritional rehabilitation with structured meal plans.

Dialectical behavior therapy (DBT) is evidence-based psychotherapy for BPD. No medications are FDA-approved for BPD; pharmacotherapy targets specific symptoms (mood stabilizers for affective instability, low-dose antipsychotics for transient psychosis). Avoid long-term benzodiazepines. Crisis safety planning. Structured treatment framework.

Early mobilization and physical therapy within 24-48 hours of ICU admission (when safe) reduces ICU-acquired weakness. Minimize sedation (target light sedation). Avoid neuromuscular blocking agents when possible. Optimize nutrition. Electrical muscle stimulation is under investigation.

Initiate early enteral nutrition within 24-48 hours of ICU admission for hemodynamically stable patients. Trophic feeding initially, advance to goal over 48-72 hours. Protein target 1.2-2.0 g/kg/day. Parenteral nutrition if EN contraindicated or not meeting goals by day 7. Avoid overfeeding in early critical illness.

Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor for septic and distributive shock. Vasopressin (0.03-0.04 U/min) as adjunct to reduce norepinephrine dose. Epinephrine for refractory septic shock or anaphylaxis. Dobutamine for cardiogenic shock with low cardiac output. Phenylephrine avoided in septic shock except for pure vasoplegia.

CYP2C19 testing recommended before clopidogrel initiation (poor metabolizers need alternative). CYP2D6 testing guides codeine, tramadol dosing (ultra-rapid metabolizers at risk). HLA-B*57:01 testing required before abacavir. DPYD testing recommended before fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy. TPMT/NUDT15 before thiopurines.

TP53 carriers: annual whole-body MRI starting age 20 (or younger if childhood cancer history). Annual breast MRI for women starting age 20. Annual dermatologic exam. CBC and urinalysis every 3-4 months. Colonoscopy every 2-5 years starting age 25. Adrenal ultrasound for children.

WHO pain ladder: Step 1 non-opioid (acetaminophen, NSAIDs). Step 2 weak opioid (tramadol, codeine). Step 3 strong opioid (morphine, hydromorphone, fentanyl). Adjuvant agents (gabapentin, duloxetine) at each step. Breakthrough dosing: 10-15% of 24-hour opioid dose q1h PRN. Bowel regimen with all opioids.

SPIKES protocol for serious illness communication. Assess patient understanding of illness. Explore values, goals, and fears. Align treatment plan with expressed goals. Document discussions in medical record. Update advance directives. Palliative care consultation for complex goals discussions.

Anti-obesity medications (AOMs) recommended as adjunct to lifestyle for BMI ≥30 or ≥27 with comorbidities. Semaglutide 2.4 mg and tirzepatide show greatest weight loss efficacy (15-21%). Phentermine-topiramate, naltrexone-bupropion as alternatives. Long-term treatment typically required as weight regain common after discontinuation.

PN indicated when EN is contraindicated or insufficient for >7 days (or >3 days in malnourished patients). Start with moderate caloric goals (20-25 kcal/kg/day). Protein 1.5-2.0 g/kg/day for critically ill. Monitor electrolytes daily initially, then weekly. Refeeding syndrome prevention with thiamine and phosphate monitoring.

Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta) is recommended for patients ≥2 years with at least one F508del mutation (~90% of CF patients). Significant improvements in FEV1, sweat chloride, and quality of life. Continue airway clearance and other CF therapies. Monitor liver function and cataracts.

Initial management with physical therapy, NSAIDs, and activity modification. Epidural steroid injections for short-term symptom relief. Surgical decompression (laminectomy) for patients with severe neurogenic claudication failing conservative management. Fusion reserved for concurrent instability.

Physical therapy and stretching are foundation of management. Oral baclofen, tizanidine, or dantrolene for generalized spasticity. Botulinum toxin injection for focal/regional spasticity. Intrathecal baclofen pump for severe generalized spasticity refractory to oral medications.

NSAIDs for pain. Combined OCPs or progestins as first-line hormonal therapy. GnRH agonists with add-back therapy for refractory cases. Dienogest or elagolix as newer options. Laparoscopic excision/ablation for surgical management. Fertility preservation counseling. Endometriomas >4 cm: consider surgical excision before ART.

Emergent pericardiocentesis for hemodynamically significant tamponade. Echo-guided approach preferred. IV fluid bolus as temporizing measure. Avoid diuretics and vasodilators (reduce preload). Surgical pericardial window for recurrent effusions. Drain management and fluid analysis (cytology, culture, protein, LDH).

NSAIDs or triptans as first-line for moderate-severe migraine attacks. Treat early in the attack for best efficacy. Gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant) for patients who cannot use triptans. Ditans (lasmiditan) as alternative. Antiemetics (metoclopramide) as adjunct. Limit acute medication use to <10-15 days/month to prevent MOH.

guidelines.section_title: CGRP Monoclonal Antibodies for Migraine Prevention
guidelines.recommendation_text: CGRP mAbs (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) are effective for episodic and chronic migraine prevention. Generally well-tolerated; constipation and injection site reactions most common. Consider for patients who have failed or cannot tolerate 2+ oral preventives. Eptinezumab is IV; others are SC self-injection.

Average-risk adults: begin screening at age 45. Colonoscopy every 10 years (preferred), or stool-based tests (FIT annually, mt-sDNA every 3 years). CT colonography every 5 years as alternative. High-risk (family history, prior polyps): colonoscopy at earlier age with shorter intervals per guidelines.

Hemodynamically unstable VT: immediate synchronized cardioversion. Stable monomorphic VT: IV amiodarone or procainamide. Catheter ablation for recurrent VT. ICD for secondary prevention after sustained VT/VF not due to reversible cause. Substrate-based ablation for VT storm. Avoid Class IC antiarrhythmics in structural heart disease.

Wide local excision with adequate margins is standard surgical approach. Radiation therapy recommended for high-grade or margin-close tumors. Doxorubicin-based chemotherapy for advanced/metastatic disease. Trabectedin for liposarcoma/leiomyosarcoma after anthracycline failure. Multidisciplinary tumor board review essential.

Maximal safe resection followed by concurrent temozolomide + radiation (Stupp protocol). MGMT methylation status guides treatment decisions (predictive for temozolomide benefit). Bevacizumab for recurrent GBM. Tumor treating fields (TTFields) as adjunct. Dexamethasone for cerebral edema. Seizure prophylaxis only if seizures occur.

Radical inguinal orchiectomy for diagnosis and initial treatment. Stage I seminoma: surveillance vs. single-agent carboplatin vs. radiation. Stage I non-seminoma: surveillance vs. RPLND vs. BEP x1 cycle. Sperm banking before treatment. Tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) for staging and monitoring.

Early stage (IA1-IB1): cone biopsy or simple hysterectomy. Stage IB2-IVA: concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin. Pembrolizumab added for PD-L1+ advanced/recurrent disease. Bevacizumab added to chemotherapy for persistent/recurrent disease. HPV vaccination for primary prevention.

Endoscopic resection for superficial tumors (T1a). Neoadjuvant chemoradiation (CROSS regimen) followed by esophagectomy for locally advanced disease. Nivolumab adjuvant for residual disease after neoadjuvant chemoRT. Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy for advanced/metastatic (PD-L1 CPS ≥5).

Maintain phosphate within normal range using dietary restriction and phosphate binders. Avoid calcium-based binders in dialysis patients with hypercalcemia or adynamic bone. Active vitamin D for CKD G3-G5D with secondary hyperparathyroidism. Cinacalcet for persistent hyperparathyroidism on dialysis. PTH target 2-9x upper normal for dialysis patients.

Target systolic BP <120 mmHg (standardized office measurement) for most CKD patients. ACE inhibitors or ARBs first-line, especially with albuminuria. Finerenone (non-steroidal MRA) for CKD with T2DM and albuminuria to reduce kidney and cardiovascular events. Thiazide-like diuretics effective in CKD G1-G3; loop diuretics for G4-G5.

guidelines.guideline: ACC/AHA Heart Failure with Preserved EF (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce HF hospitalizations in HFpEF. Diuretics for volume management. Treat comorbidities (AF, hypertension, obesity, coronary disease). GLP-1 RAs for HFpEF with obesity. MRAs (spironolactone) may reduce hospitalizations. Exercise rehabilitation improves functional capacity.
applies_to_conditions: heart failure with preserved ejection fraction

guidelines.recommendation_text: SGLT2 inhibitors for T2DM with established CKD (eGFR ≥20), heart failure, or high cardiovascular risk regardless of HbA1c. GLP-1 RAs for T2DM with established ASCVD or high cardiovascular risk. Finerenone for T2DM with CKD and albuminuria. Metformin remains first-line for glycemic management. Dual GLP-1/GIP agonist (tirzepatide) for additional glycemic and weight benefit.

SCAI stages A-E for cardiogenic shock classification. Stage C (classic shock): IV inotropes/vasopressors + consideration of mechanical circulatory support (Impella, IABP, ECMO). Stage D (deteriorating): escalate MCS. Early cardiac catheterization for acute MI with shock. Multidisciplinary shock team activation.

TAVR is standard for inoperable and high-surgical-risk patients with severe aortic stenosis. For intermediate risk, both TAVR and SAVR are acceptable (shared decision-making). For low-risk patients, TAVR is an option with demonstrated non-inferior outcomes at 5 years. Heart team evaluation is essential. Age and durability considerations for younger patients.

TIPS is indicated for refractory ascites, recurrent variceal bleeding unresponsive to endoscopic therapy, and Budd-Chiari syndrome. MELD score guides patient selection; generally avoided if MELD >18. Covered stents (PTFE-covered) preferred for improved patency. Monitor for hepatic encephalopathy post-procedure.

Uterine artery embolization is effective for symptomatic fibroids (menorrhagia, bulk symptoms) in patients who want to avoid hysterectomy. Not recommended for patients actively seeking pregnancy. MRI before procedure to characterize fibroids. Post-embolization syndrome (pain, fever, nausea) is common and self-limited.

RAI (I-131) is first-line definitive therapy for Graves disease in the US. Calculate dose based on gland size and uptake. Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Hypothyroidism is expected outcome; start levothyroxine when TSH elevates. Pre-treatment with methimazole for severe hyperthyroidism; discontinue 3-5 days before RAI.

Stabilize ABCs. Activated charcoal if ingestion within 1-2 hours and intact airway. Whole bowel irrigation for sustained-release or enteric-coated preparations. Specific antidotes when indicated (naloxone, acetylcysteine, fomepizole, flumazenil with caution). Consult poison control center. Enhanced elimination (hemodialysis) for specific toxins.

Fomepizole (preferred) or ethanol to inhibit alcohol dehydrogenase. Hemodialysis for severe poisoning (high osmol gap, metabolic acidosis, renal failure, visual symptoms for methanol). Folic acid/leucovorin adjunct for methanol. Thiamine/pyridoxine adjunct for ethylene glycol. Do not wait for confirmatory levels if high clinical suspicion.

Atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline, or mefloquine based on destination resistance patterns. Begin before travel (varies by drug). Atovaquone-proguanil: start 1-2 days before, continue 7 days after return. Doxycycline: start 1-2 days before, continue 28 days after. Mefloquine: start 2 weeks before, continue 4 weeks after.

Prevention: hand hygiene, food/water precautions. Self-treatment for moderate-severe: azithromycin (preferred for Southeast Asia due to fluoroquinolone resistance) or fluoroquinolone. Loperamide as adjunct for adults without dysentery. Rifaximin for non-invasive disease. Oral rehydration for all cases.

Begin rehabilitation as early as clinically appropriate (within 24-48 hours for mobilization). Intensive task-specific training improves motor recovery. Constraint-induced movement therapy for upper extremity. Speech-language pathology for aphasia and dysphagia. Screen and treat post-stroke depression. Spasticity management with botulinum toxin.

Mild pulmonary: observation with serial serology and imaging (many self-resolve). Moderate-severe or immunocompromised: fluconazole or itraconazole. Disseminated disease: amphotericin B induction followed by azole maintenance. CNS involvement: fluconazole (lifelong) ± intrathecal amphotericin B for refractory cases.

Mild-moderate pulmonary: observation or itraconazole for >4 weeks of symptoms. Moderate-severe: liposomal amphotericin B induction (1-2 weeks) then itraconazole (12 months). Disseminated disease: same approach as moderate-severe. Monitor itraconazole levels and Histoplasma antigen for treatment response.

Initial steps: warm, dry, stimulate, clear airway if needed. PPV if HR <100 or apneic/gasping at 30 seconds. CPAP for spontaneous breathing with labored respirations. Intubation if PPV ineffective. Epinephrine if HR <60 despite effective ventilation and chest compressions. Delayed cord clamping (30-60 seconds) for vigorous term and preterm infants.

Human milk feeding is the most effective NEC prevention strategy. Probiotics may reduce NEC risk in VLBW infants (institutional protocols vary). Medical management: NPO, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole). Surgical consultation for pneumoperitoneum, clinical deterioration, or fixed loops.

Surfactant replacement therapy for intubated preterm infants with RDS. Less invasive surfactant administration (LISA) via thin catheter during CPAP. Early CPAP as initial respiratory support for spontaneously breathing preterm infants. Target SpO2 90-95% in preterm infants. Caffeine for apnea of prematurity.

Ultrasound and MRI (without gadolinium) are safe at any gestational age. Ionizing radiation: dose-dependent risk; most diagnostic studies are below harmful threshold (<50 mGy). CT with informed consent when clinically indicated. Gadolinium crosses placenta — avoid unless essential. Iodinated contrast: use if indicated; check neonatal thyroid function.

Weight loss (5-10% body weight) is the most effective treatment. Acetazolamide is first-line pharmacotherapy. Topiramate as alternative (also promotes weight loss). Therapeutic LP for acute vision-threatening papilledema. CSF shunting or optic nerve sheath fenestration for refractory cases with progressive vision loss.

All four pillars should be initiated for HFrEF (EF ≤40%): ACEi/ARNi (sacubitril-valsartan preferred), beta-blocker (carvedilol, bisoprolol, metoprolol succinate), MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone), and SGLT2i (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin). Rapid sequencing: start all four within weeks, uptitrate to target doses. Each class independently reduces mortality.
applies_to_conditions: heart failure with reduced ejection fraction

guidelines.recommendation_text: ISTH DIC scoring system: platelets, fibrin markers, PT, fibrinogen. Treat underlying cause. Supportive: platelets if <10K (or <50K with bleeding), FFP/cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL with bleeding. Heparin for DIC with thrombotic predominance (e.g., malignancy-associated). Antifibrinolytics generally avoided unless hyperfibrinolysis confirmed.

Budesonide is the preferred first-line treatment for active microscopic colitis. Maintenance budesonide for relapse prevention. Cholestyramine as adjunct for associated bile acid malabsorption. Immunomodulators (azathioprine) or biologics for budesonide-refractory cases. Discontinue potential offending medications (NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs).

guidelines.section_title: Bile Acid Diarrhea Diagnosis and Treatment
guidelines.recommendation_text: Consider BAD in patients with chronic watery diarrhea, especially post-cholecystectomy or ileal disease. SeHCAT scan (where available) or empiric trial of bile acid sequestrant (cholestyramine, colesevelam) is diagnostic/therapeutic. Response to sequestrant supports diagnosis. Colesevelam may be better tolerated than cholestyramine.

Carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine are first-line pharmacotherapy. Lamotrigine, baclofen, or gabapentin as second-line. MRI to exclude secondary causes (tumor, MS). Microvascular decompression for medically refractory TN with neurovascular compression on MRI. Stereotactic radiosurgery or percutaneous procedures as alternatives.

Recommended for symptomatic COPD patients (MRC dyspnea grade ≥2). Minimum 6-8 weeks, ≥2 sessions/week. Components: endurance training, resistance training, education, psychosocial support, nutritional counseling. Improves exercise capacity, dyspnea, and quality of life. Reduces hospitalizations. Maintenance exercise program after completion.

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with HFrEF, the use of an ACE inhibitor (or ARB if ACE inhibitor not tolerated), a beta-blocker (bisoprolol, carvedilol, or sustained-release metoprolol succinate), and an MRA is recommended to reduce morbidity and mortality (Class I, Level A). In patients with symptomatic HFrEF, an ARNI is recommended in place of an ACE inhibitor or ARB to further reduce morbidity and mortality (Class I, Level A). In patients with HFrEF and NYHA class II-IV symptoms, an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended to reduce hospitalizations and cardiovascular mortality (Class I, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with HFpEF, SGLT2 inhibitors can be beneficial in decreasing heart failure hospitalizations and cardiovascular mortality (Class IIa, Level B-R). Diuretics are recommended for symptom relief in patients with HFpEF who have evidence of volume overload (Class I, Level B). Management of hypertension, atrial fibrillation, coronary artery disease, and other comorbidities is recommended according to published guidelines (Class I, Level C).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with HFmrEF (LVEF 41-49 percent), GDMT used for HFrEF may be considered to reduce morbidity and mortality, particularly in patients with LVEF on the lower end of this range (Class IIb, Level B-NR). SGLT2 inhibitors can be beneficial in patients with HFmrEF to reduce hospitalization and cardiovascular mortality (Class IIa, Level B-R).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: Diuretics are recommended to relieve congestion, improve symptoms, and prevent worsening HF in patients with HF and fluid retention (Class I, Level B). Loop diuretics are preferred for most patients with HF. Thiazide diuretics may be added for diuretic resistance. Diuretic dose should be adjusted to achieve and maintain euvolemia.

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: ICD therapy is recommended for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death in patients with symptomatic HF (NYHA class II-III) and LVEF 35 percent or less despite 3 or more months of GDMT, with life expectancy greater than 1 year (Class I, Level A). CRT is indicated for patients with LVEF 35 percent or less, sinus rhythm, LBBB with QRS 150 ms or greater, and NYHA class II-IV symptoms on GDMT (Class I, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
