guidelines.recommendation_text: Patients with advanced HF refractory to GDMT and device therapy should be referred to an advanced HF center for evaluation of MCS or heart transplantation (Class I, Level C). Durable LVAD therapy is indicated in select patients with advanced HFrEF as bridge to transplant or destination therapy (Class I, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: IV loop diuretics are recommended for patients hospitalized with HF and volume overload to improve symptoms (Class I, Level B). In patients with acute decompensated HF, continuation of GDMT is recommended unless contraindicated (Class I, Level B). In patients with HFrEF not previously on GDMT, initiation before discharge is recommended (Class I, Level B).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with HF and AF, anticoagulation and rate/rhythm control should be managed per AF guidelines. In HF with diabetes, SGLT2 inhibitors are recommended to reduce HF progression. In HF with CAD, optimal medical therapy and consideration of revascularization is recommended. Iron deficiency should be treated with IV iron in symptomatic HFrEF patients.

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.recommendation_text: Palliative care consultation is recommended to improve quality of life in patients with advanced HF (Class I, Level B). Discussion of prognosis, goals of care, and advance directives is recommended for all patients with HF (Class I, Level C). Opioids may be considered for symptom relief of dyspnea refractory to other therapies (Class IIb, Level B).

guidelines.guideline: 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure
guidelines.section_title: Stage A and B Heart Failure Prevention
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients at risk for HF (Stage A), optimal control of blood pressure, lipids, and glucose is recommended (Class I, Level A). SGLT2 inhibitors are recommended in patients with T2DM and high CV risk or CKD to prevent HF (Class I, Level A). In patients with structural heart disease but no HF symptoms (Stage B), ACE inhibitors/ARBs and beta-blockers are recommended if LVEF is reduced (Class I, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: For adults with confirmed hypertension and known CVD or 10-year ASCVD event risk of 10 percent or higher, a BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg is recommended (Class I, Level B-R). For adults with confirmed hypertension without additional markers of increased CVD risk, a BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg may be reasonable (Class IIb, Level C-EO).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Nonpharmacological interventions that are effective include weight loss in overweight/obese patients, heart-healthy dietary pattern (DASH diet), reduced sodium intake (less than 1500 mg/day optimal, at least 1000 mg/day reduction), enhanced potassium intake, increased physical activity (90-150 min/week aerobic), and moderation of alcohol (2 or fewer drinks/day for men, 1 or fewer for women) (Class I, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use of first-line agents (thiazide diuretics, CCBs, and ACE inhibitors or ARBs) is recommended for stage 1 hypertension (Class I, Level A). For stage 2 hypertension (BP 140/90 or higher), initiation of 2 first-line agents of different classes is recommended (Class I, Level C-EO). ACE inhibitors and ARBs should not be used in combination (Class III, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Resistant hypertension is defined as BP above goal despite use of 3 or more antihypertensive medications of different classes, including a diuretic at optimal doses, or controlled BP requiring 4 or more medications. Evaluate for secondary causes (primary aldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, pheochromocytoma, OSA). Adding a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone) is effective as fourth-line therapy (Class I, Level B-R).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Screen for secondary causes of hypertension in patients with resistant hypertension, early onset hypertension (before age 30), abrupt onset, or disproportionate target organ damage. Common causes include primary aldosteronism, renal parenchymal disease, renovascular disease, obstructive sleep apnea, drug-induced, and pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma. Primary aldosteronism is detected by aldosterone-to-renin ratio.

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Hypertensive emergency is SBP greater than 180 and/or DBP greater than 120 mm Hg with target-organ damage. Requires IV antihypertensive therapy and ICU admission. Initial goal is to reduce BP by no more than 25 percent within first hour, then to 160/100-110 mm Hg over next 2-6 hours. Hypertensive urgency (elevated BP without acute target organ damage) can be managed with oral medications and close follow-up.

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: In adults with hypertension and CKD, treatment with an ACE inhibitor (Class IIa, Level B-R) or ARB (Class IIa, Level C-EO) is reasonable to slow kidney disease progression, particularly in patients with albuminuria (greater than 300 mg/day or greater than 300 mg/g Cr). BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg is recommended for patients with CKD (Class I, Level B-R).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: In adults with diabetes mellitus and hypertension, BP target of less than 130/80 mm Hg is recommended (Class I, Level B-R). In adults with diabetes and hypertension, all first-line classes of antihypertensives are useful and effective. An ACE inhibitor or ARB is reasonable in the presence of albuminuria (Class IIa, Level B-NR).

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Accurate BP measurement requires patient to sit quietly for at least 5 minutes with back supported, feet flat on floor, arm supported at heart level. Use appropriate cuff size (bladder encircling 80 percent of arm). Obtain 2-3 readings 1-2 minutes apart and average. Confirm elevated readings on separate occasions before diagnosis. Measure BP in both arms and use arm with higher reading for subsequent measurements.

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: Out-of-office BP measurements are recommended to confirm diagnosis of hypertension and for titration of BP-lowering medication, in conjunction with telehealth counseling or clinical interventions (Class I, Level A). Home BP monitoring with validated devices is recommended. Average home BP of 130/80 mm Hg or higher is considered elevated. Ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) is the most accurate method and captures nocturnal dipping patterns.

guidelines.guideline: 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults
guidelines.recommendation_text: White coat hypertension (elevated office BP with normal out-of-office BP) occurs in 10-20 percent of adults with office hypertension. Masked hypertension (normal office BP with elevated out-of-office BP) occurs in 10-15 percent and carries cardiovascular risk similar to sustained hypertension. ABPM or home BP monitoring is recommended to detect these conditions. White coat hypertension requires lifestyle modification and annual monitoring; masked hypertension requires treatment.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients with AF, the CHA2DS2-VASc score is recommended to assess stroke risk and guide antithrombotic therapy decisions (Class I, Level B-NR). CHA2DS2-VASc includes: CHF (1), Hypertension (1), Age 75 or older (2), Diabetes (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex category female (1). Male score of 2 or greater or female score of 3 or greater warrants anticoagulation.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients with AF eligible for oral anticoagulation, DOACs (apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban, rivaroxaban) are recommended over warfarin, except in patients with moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves (Class I, Level A). Warfarin is recommended for patients with mechanical heart valves (target INR based on valve type/position) and moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (Class I, Level B-NR).

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: Bleeding risk assessment should be performed when considering anticoagulation, but elevated bleeding risk alone should not preclude anticoagulation when indicated (Class I, Level B-NR). The HAS-BLED score helps identify modifiable bleeding risk factors: Hypertension, Abnormal renal/liver function, Stroke, Bleeding history, Labile INR, Elderly (age over 65), Drugs/alcohol. Score of 3 or higher indicates high bleeding risk requiring close monitoring.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: Rate control is recommended for AF patients with persistent rapid ventricular rates (Class I, Level B-R). Beta-blockers or nondihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) are recommended as first-line for rate control (Class I, Level B-R). In patients with HFrEF, beta-blockers and/or digoxin are preferred (Class I, Level B-R). Lenient rate control (resting HR less than 110 bpm) may be reasonable initially if symptoms are acceptable (Class IIb, Level B-R).

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: Rhythm control is recommended for AF patients with symptoms despite adequate rate control (Class I, Level B-R). Early rhythm control within 1 year of AF diagnosis may reduce cardiovascular outcomes in patients with CV risk factors (Class IIa, Level B-R). Antiarrhythmic drugs for rhythm control include flecainide, propafenone (no structural heart disease), dofetilide, dronedarone, sotalol, and amiodarone. Drug selection depends on comorbidities and side effect profile.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: Catheter ablation for pulmonary vein isolation is recommended for symptomatic paroxysmal AF refractory to or intolerant of at least one antiarrhythmic medication (Class I, Level A). Catheter ablation may be considered as first-line therapy in select patients (Class IIa, Level B-R). In patients with AF and HFrEF, catheter ablation is reasonable to improve symptoms and LV function (Class IIa, Level B-R). Ablation for persistent AF may require more extensive substrate modification.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with AF and HFrEF, anticoagulation and rate control with beta-blockers are recommended (Class I, Level B-R). Digoxin may be added if rate control is inadequate. Rhythm control with ablation is reasonable for patients with HF to improve symptoms and ventricular function (Class IIa, Level B-R). Non-dihydropyridine CCBs should be avoided in HFrEF due to negative inotropic effects. Dofetilide or amiodarone are preferred antiarrhythmics in HF.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients on anticoagulation requiring surgery, bridging with LMWH/UFH is generally not recommended for DOACs and may be harmful in most patients on warfarin, except for those at highest thromboembolic risk (mechanical valve, recent stroke/TIA) (Class III, Level B-R). DOAC interruption timing depends on bleeding risk and renal function. Resume anticoagulation when adequate hemostasis achieved, typically 24-72 hours postoperatively.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: Opportunistic AF screening by pulse palpation or ECG is reasonable for patients age 65 and older during clinic visits (Class IIa, Level B-NR). Systematic ECG screening may be considered in patients age 75 and older or those at high stroke risk (Class IIb, Level B-R). Wearable devices and smartphone apps can detect AF but require ECG confirmation. Subclinical AF (device-detected) warrants evaluation for stroke risk and consideration of anticoagulation based on clinical context.

guidelines.guideline: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
guidelines.recommendation_text: Comprehensive risk factor management is recommended for all AF patients to reduce AF burden and improve outcomes (Class I, Level B-R). This includes weight loss (target BMI less than 27), blood pressure control (target less than 130/80), glycemic control, treatment of OSA with CPAP, moderate physical activity, and limiting alcohol consumption (fewer than 3 drinks/week or abstinence). Abstinence from alcohol reduces AF recurrence.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: For patients presenting with acute chest pain, initial evaluation should include focused history, physical examination, and 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes (Class I, Level B-NR). Key history elements include pain character, location, radiation, duration, precipitating/relieving factors, and associated symptoms. Assess for high-risk features: ongoing chest pain, hemodynamic instability, signs of heart failure, or new murmur.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin (hs-cTn) is recommended as the preferred biomarker for evaluation of patients with suspected ACS (Class I, Level A). Serial measurements at 0 and 1-3 hours are recommended. Very low hs-cTn (below limit of detection or less than 6 ng/L) with low clinical probability can identify patients for early discharge. Rising hs-cTn pattern (delta greater than 3 ng/L at 1 hour or greater than 6 ng/L at 3 hours) suggests acute myocardial injury.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: For low-risk patients with acute chest pain, rapid diagnostic protocols (0/1-hour or 0/3-hour) using hs-cTn and clinical assessment are recommended (Class I, Level A). Validated risk scores (HEART, TIMI, EDACS) can assist in identifying low-risk patients suitable for early discharge. Patients with HEART score 0-3, negative hs-cTn, and nonischemic ECG have 30-day MACE rate less than 1 percent and may be discharged with outpatient follow-up.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: For intermediate-risk patients with acute chest pain and negative serial troponins, functional testing (exercise ECG, stress imaging) is reasonable to evaluate for obstructive CAD (Class IIa, Level B-NR). Exercise treadmill testing without imaging is reasonable for patients who can exercise and have interpretable baseline ECG. Stress imaging (echocardiography, nuclear, CMR) is preferred for patients who cannot exercise or have uninterpretable ECG.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: CCTA is recommended as an alternative to functional testing for low-to-intermediate risk patients with acute chest pain to exclude obstructive CAD (Class I, Level A). CCTA provides anatomic assessment of coronary stenosis and plaque burden. CCTA is preferred for patients with lower pretest probability (less than 50 percent) and is useful when functional testing would be nondiagnostic. CAC scoring adds prognostic information but does not exclude obstructive CAD.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: Chest pain observation units with accelerated diagnostic protocols are effective for intermediate-risk patients (Class IIa, Level B-NR). Protocol includes serial hs-cTn at 0 and 3-6 hours, continuous monitoring, and provocative testing before discharge. Observation unit stay typically 6-24 hours. Goal is to safely identify patients who can be discharged versus those requiring admission for further evaluation or intervention.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: Validated clinical decision pathways using risk scores are recommended to improve diagnostic accuracy (Class I, Level B-NR). HEART score (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin) is widely validated for 30-day MACE prediction. TIMI risk score validated for ACS patients. HEART score less than or equal to 3 with negative troponin identifies very low-risk patients (less than 2 percent MACE). Integration of clinical gestalt with scores improves accuracy.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain
guidelines.recommendation_text: Disposition decisions should integrate clinical assessment, troponin results, ECG findings, and risk scores. Admit to coronary care/ICU: STEMI, hemodynamic instability, ongoing ischemia. Admit to telemetry: NSTEMI, high-risk features, positive troponin. Observation unit: intermediate risk with negative initial workup. Discharge: low risk (HEART 0-3), negative serial troponin, nonischemic ECG, with outpatient follow-up within 72 hours.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: Heart Team approach is recommended for patients with complex CAD to determine optimal revascularization strategy (Class I, Level C-EO). CABG is preferred for patients with diabetes and multivessel CAD, left main disease with high SYNTAX score, or complex 3-vessel disease. PCI is reasonable for patients with low SYNTAX scores, single vessel disease not involving proximal LAD, or high surgical risk. Patient preferences and comorbidities should guide decisions.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: The SYNTAX score is recommended to assess coronary anatomic complexity and guide revascularization strategy (Class I, Level B-NR). Low SYNTAX (0-22): PCI and CABG have similar outcomes, PCI reasonable. Intermediate SYNTAX (23-32): CABG generally preferred but PCI acceptable. High SYNTAX (33 or greater): CABG strongly preferred for improved long-term outcomes. SYNTAX II incorporates clinical variables for more individualized risk prediction.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: CABG is recommended to improve survival in patients with significant left main stenosis (greater than 50 percent) (Class I, Level B-R). PCI is reasonable for left main disease in patients with low-intermediate anatomic complexity (SYNTAX less than or equal to 32) who are appropriate candidates and when performed by experienced operators (Class IIa, Level B-R). CABG preferred for left main with high SYNTAX, bifurcation involvement, or concurrent multivessel disease.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: CABG is recommended for patients with multivessel CAD involving the proximal LAD, especially those with diabetes, LV dysfunction, or complex disease (Class I, Level A). For patients with multivessel CAD and diabetes, CABG improves survival compared to PCI (Class I, Level A). Complete revascularization should be targeted when feasible. In patients with STEMI and multivessel disease, staged PCI of non-culprit lesions is recommended.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with stable ischemic heart disease, optimal medical therapy (OMT) is the foundation of treatment (Class I, Level A). Revascularization in addition to OMT is reasonable for patients with persistent angina despite OMT, significant stenosis (greater than 70 percent epicardial or greater than 50 percent left main), and positive ischemia testing or FFR less than or equal to 0.80 (Class IIa, Level B-R). Revascularization does not reduce mortality compared to OMT alone in most stable CAD patients.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: DAPT with aspirin and a P2Y12 inhibitor is recommended after PCI. For ACS patients receiving DES, DAPT for at least 12 months is recommended (Class I, Level A). For stable CAD with DES, DAPT for at least 6 months is recommended (Class I, Level B-R). Shorter DAPT (1-3 months) followed by P2Y12 monotherapy may be reasonable in patients at high bleeding risk (Class IIb, Level B-R). Extended DAPT beyond 12 months may be considered for high-risk patients without excessive bleeding risk.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: Aspirin (81-325 mg loading, 81 mg maintenance) is recommended for all patients undergoing PCI (Class I, Level A). For ACS patients, a P2Y12 inhibitor should be initiated: ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel due to superior efficacy (Class I, Level B-R). Prasugrel contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA, age 75 or older, or weight less than 60 kg. Clopidogrel reasonable if bleeding risk is high or ticagrelor/prasugrel unavailable. Pretreatment with P2Y12 inhibitor is reasonable for STEMI but not routinely recommended for NSTEMI before angiography.

guidelines.guideline: 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization
guidelines.recommendation_text: Complete revascularization is recommended when feasible and is associated with improved outcomes compared to incomplete revascularization (Class I, Level B-R). For STEMI with multivessel disease, complete revascularization (staged or during index procedure) is recommended (Class I, Level A). Physiologic guidance (FFR/iFR) is recommended for non-culprit lesions in STEMI before revascularization. Staged procedures are generally preferred over multivessel PCI during primary PCI for STEMI.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: In adults aged 40-75 without clinical ASCVD or diabetes with LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL, estimate 10-year ASCVD risk using Pooled Cohort Equations (Class I, Level B-NR). Risk categories: Low risk (less than 5 percent), Borderline risk (5 to less than 7.5 percent), Intermediate risk (7.5 to less than 20 percent), High risk (20 percent or higher). Risk discussion should inform statin therapy decisions.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: High-intensity statin therapy lowers LDL-C by 50 percent or more (atorvastatin 40-80 mg, rosuvastatin 20-40 mg). Moderate-intensity statin therapy lowers LDL-C by 30-49 percent (atorvastatin 10-20 mg, rosuvastatin 5-10 mg, simvastatin 20-40 mg, pravastatin 40-80 mg). Low-intensity statin therapy lowers LDL-C by less than 30 percent. Statin intensity selection should be based on ASCVD risk and LDL-C level.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: In adults 40-75 with LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL and 10-year ASCVD risk 7.5-19.9 percent (intermediate risk), moderate-intensity statin is recommended (Class I, Level A). In adults with 10-year risk 20 percent or higher (high risk), high-intensity statin is recommended (Class I, Level A). In borderline risk (5 to less than 7.5 percent), statin therapy may be considered with risk enhancers. Risk-enhancing factors include family history of premature ASCVD, LDL-C 160 or higher, metabolic syndrome, CKD, inflammatory conditions, South Asian ancestry.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: In patients with clinical ASCVD, high-intensity statin therapy is recommended to reduce LDL-C by 50 percent or more (Class I, Level A). If LDL-C remains 70 mg/dL or higher on maximally tolerated statin, adding ezetimibe is reasonable (Class IIa, Level B-R). If LDL-C remains 70 mg/dL or higher on statin plus ezetimibe, adding a PCSK9 inhibitor is reasonable, especially if the patient has very high-risk features (Class IIa, Level A).

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: For very high-risk ASCVD (history of multiple major ASCVD events or 1 major event plus multiple high-risk conditions), LDL-C threshold of 70 mg/dL is used to consider adding non-statin therapy (Class IIa, Level B-R). Very high-risk features include: recent ACS (within 12 months), history of MI, history of ischemic stroke, symptomatic PAD. High-risk conditions include: age 65 or older, heterozygous FH, prior CABG or PCI, diabetes, hypertension, CKD, current smoking, LDL-C 100 or higher despite maximally tolerated statin.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: Ezetimibe lowers LDL-C by approximately 15-20 percent and is recommended as first add-on therapy when statin alone is insufficient (Class IIa, Level B-R). PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab) lower LDL-C by 50-60 percent and are recommended in very high-risk ASCVD patients with LDL-C 70 or higher on maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe (Class IIa, Level A). Bempedoic acid is an option for statin-intolerant patients.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: Statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS) occur in 5-10 percent of patients but true intolerance to all statins is rare (less than 2 percent). Management strategies include: rechallenge with same or different statin, lower dose or alternate-day dosing, hydrophilic statins (pravastatin, rosuvastatin) may be better tolerated, address vitamin D deficiency, exclude hypothyroidism. For true statin-intolerant patients, use non-statin therapies (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, bempedoic acid).

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: Fasting lipid panel should be obtained before initiating statin therapy (Class I, Level B-NR). Repeat lipid panel 4-12 weeks after starting statin to assess adherence and response, then every 3-12 months (Class I, Level B-NR). LDL-C is primarily used to monitor therapy. If anticipated response not achieved, assess adherence, reinforce lifestyle modifications, and consider dose adjustment or addition of non-statin therapy. Annual monitoring generally sufficient for stable patients.

guidelines.guideline: 2018 ACC/AHA Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol
guidelines.recommendation_text: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) should be suspected in patients with LDL-C 190 mg/dL or higher, especially with family history of premature ASCVD or elevated LDL (Class I, Level B-NR). Dutch Lipid Clinic Network criteria aid diagnosis. High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for heterozygous FH, with addition of ezetimibe and/or PCSK9 inhibitor to achieve LDL-C reduction of 50 percent or more (Class I, Level B-R). Cascade screening of first-degree relatives is recommended. Homozygous FH requires aggressive treatment including LDL apheresis.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Criteria for diabetes diagnosis: A1C 6.5 percent or higher, OR fasting plasma glucose (FPG) 126 mg/dL or higher, OR 2-hour plasma glucose 200 mg/dL or higher during 75g OGTT, OR random plasma glucose 200 mg/dL or higher with classic hyperglycemic symptoms. In asymptomatic patients, diagnosis requires two abnormal test results from the same sample or two separate samples. A1C should be measured using NGSP-certified method.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Prediabetes is defined as: A1C 5.7-6.4 percent, OR FPG 100-125 mg/dL (impaired fasting glucose), OR 2-hour glucose 140-199 mg/dL during OGTT (impaired glucose tolerance). Patients with prediabetes should be referred to intensive lifestyle intervention program targeting 7 percent weight loss and 150 min/week of moderate physical activity. Metformin may be considered for prediabetes prevention, especially in those with BMI 35 or higher, age less than 60, or prior GDM.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Type 1 diabetes features: younger onset (but can occur at any age), rapid onset of symptoms, lean body habitus, presence of autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, insulin autoantibodies), low or absent C-peptide. Type 2 diabetes features: older onset, gradual symptoms, obesity/overweight, insulin resistance, preserved C-peptide initially. Autoantibody testing should be considered when classification is uncertain. Approximately 5-10 percent of adults with initial T2DM diagnosis have autoimmune diabetes.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA) presents as adult-onset diabetes with positive islet autoantibodies, initially manageable without insulin but progressing to insulin requirement. Features suggesting LADA: age less than 50, BMI less than 25, personal/family history of autoimmune disease, rapid progression to insulin need, positive GAD antibodies. Initial management may include oral agents but close monitoring for insulin requirement is essential. LADA represents approximately 10 percent of adult diabetes.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Screen for prediabetes/T2DM in adults age 35 and older, regardless of weight (Class B). Screen at any age in overweight/obese adults with risk factors: first-degree relative with diabetes, high-risk race/ethnicity, CVD history, hypertension, HDL less than 35 mg/dL or TG greater than 250 mg/dL, PCOS, physical inactivity, acanthosis nigricans. Screen women with history of GDM every 3 years lifelong. If normal results, repeat testing at minimum 3-year intervals.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: A1C target less than 7 percent is appropriate for most nonpregnant adults (Class A). More stringent target (less than 6.5 percent) may be considered for patients with short duration of diabetes, T2DM treated with lifestyle or metformin only, long life expectancy, or no significant CVD, if achievable without significant hypoglycemia. Less stringent target (less than 8 percent) may be appropriate for patients with history of severe hypoglycemia, limited life expectancy, advanced complications, extensive comorbidities, or longstanding diabetes with difficulty achieving goal.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Real-time CGM or intermittently scanned CGM should be offered to adults with T1DM (Class A) and adults with T2DM on multiple daily injections or insulin pump therapy (Class B). CGM improves A1C and reduces hypoglycemia. CGM should be considered for adults with T2DM on basal insulin who meet specific criteria. Proper education on CGM use and interpretation is essential. CGM data should be reviewed at each visit using standardized reports (AGP).

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For most adults with T1DM or T2DM, CGM targets are: Time in Range (TIR) 70-180 mg/dL greater than 70 percent, Time below range (TBR) less than 70 mg/dL less than 4 percent, Time below range less than 54 mg/dL less than 1 percent, Time above range greater than 180 mg/dL less than 25 percent. For older/high-risk adults: TIR greater than 50 percent, TBR less than 1 percent. Each 5 percent increase in TIR is clinically significant. TIR correlates with A1C and risk of complications.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Level 1 hypoglycemia: glucose 54-70 mg/dL. Level 2 (clinically significant): glucose less than 54 mg/dL. Level 3 (severe): requires assistance for recovery. Hypoglycemia risk assessment should occur at every visit. For patients at increased risk, consider: relaxing A1C target, CGM use, structured education, review of medications (reduce or eliminate sulfonylureas/insulin secretagogues). Glucagon should be prescribed for all patients at risk of level 2/3 hypoglycemia.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Sick day rules should be taught to all patients with diabetes. Key principles: never omit insulin completely (may need adjustment), monitor blood glucose more frequently, check ketones if glucose greater than 250 mg/dL (T1DM), maintain hydration, seek medical attention for persistent vomiting, ketones, or inability to maintain hydration. SGLT2 inhibitors should be held during acute illness due to DKA risk. Metformin should be held during dehydration or AKI risk.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic agent for T2DM when not contraindicated (Class A). Benefits include: efficacy, safety, low cost, possible CV benefit, weight neutrality. Start 500 mg once or twice daily with meals, titrate to 2000 mg/day as tolerated. GI side effects are common initially and often resolve. Extended-release formulation may improve GI tolerability. Check B12 levels periodically with long-term use.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: After metformin, choice of additional agent should be individualized based on: established ASCVD or high CV risk (GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i with proven benefit), heart failure (SGLT2i), CKD with albuminuria (SGLT2i or GLP-1 RA), need to minimize hypoglycemia (GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i, DPP-4i, TZD), need for weight loss (GLP-1 RA, SGLT2i), cost considerations. Combination therapy early in disease may be more effective than sequential monotherapy.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: GLP-1 receptor agonists with proven CV benefit (liraglutide, semaglutide, dulaglutide) are recommended for T2DM patients with established ASCVD, multiple CV risk factors, or CKD (Class A). GLP-1 RAs lower A1C by 1.0-1.5 percent and promote weight loss (2-6 kg). Semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy) has demonstrated superior CV and weight outcomes. Tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist) provides additional A1C and weight reduction.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: SGLT2 inhibitors with proven CV and renal benefit (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin) are recommended for T2DM with established ASCVD, HF, or CKD (Class A). SGLT2i reduce HF hospitalizations and CKD progression independent of A1C. Use is now recommended down to eGFR 20 mL/min for cardiorenal protection (glycemic efficacy diminishes below eGFR 45). DKA risk requires sick-day rules and holding during surgery/illness.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Consider insulin early if: significant hyperglycemia (A1C greater than 10 percent, glucose greater than 300 mg/dL), symptoms of hyperglycemia, evidence of catabolism, or when diabetes type is uncertain. Basal insulin (glargine, detemir, degludec) is preferred initial insulin. Starting dose typically 10 units/day or 0.1-0.2 units/kg/day. Titrate by 2-4 units every 3-7 days to fasting glucose target (80-130 mg/dL). If A1C remains above target, add prandial insulin or GLP-1 RA.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: If fasting glucose at target but A1C remains above goal, assess postprandial glucose and consider adding prandial coverage. Options for intensification: basal-plus (add single prandial insulin at largest meal), basal-bolus (prandial insulin at all meals), or addition of GLP-1 RA to basal insulin. GLP-1 RA plus basal insulin is preferred over basal-bolus for many patients due to lower hypoglycemia risk and weight benefit. Concentrated insulins (U-200, U-300, U-500) are available for patients with high insulin requirements.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Fixed-ratio combinations of basal insulin plus GLP-1 RA (iDegLira: degludec/liraglutide, iGlarLixi: glargine/lixisenatide) offer convenience and may improve adherence. These combinations provide complementary mechanisms: basal insulin controls fasting glucose, GLP-1 RA controls postprandial glucose and promotes weight loss. Consider when A1C remains elevated on optimized basal insulin or GLP-1 RA alone. Start at low dose and titrate weekly to fasting glucose target.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Blood pressure should be measured at every routine visit (Class B). For patients with diabetes and hypertension at higher CV risk (existing ASCVD or 10-year ASCVD risk greater than or equal to 15 percent), BP target less than 130/80 mmHg is recommended (Class B). For patients at lower CV risk, target less than 140/90 mmHg may be appropriate (Class C). Treatment should include lifestyle modification plus ACEi/ARB as first-line if albuminuria present. Combination therapy often needed.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For adults 40-75 years with diabetes without ASCVD, moderate-intensity statin is recommended (Class A). For diabetes with ASCVD or multiple ASCVD risk factors, high-intensity statin is recommended (Class A). For very high-risk patients (ASCVD plus diabetes), if LDL remains greater than or equal to 70 mg/dL on maximum tolerated statin, adding ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitor is reasonable. Lifestyle modification including weight loss and dietary changes should accompany pharmacotherapy.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Aspirin 75-162 mg/day is recommended as secondary prevention in patients with diabetes and ASCVD (Class A). For primary prevention, aspirin may be considered in diabetes with increased CV risk if bleeding risk is not prohibitive (Class C). Clopidogrel may be used as alternative if aspirin allergy. DAPT per ACS/PCI guidelines if applicable. Aspirin for primary prevention requires shared decision-making given bleeding risks.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Adults with T1DM should have initial dilated eye exam within 5 years of diagnosis (Class B). Adults with T2DM should have exam at time of diagnosis (Class B). Repeat exam annually if no retinopathy; every 1-2 years if mild NPDR; more frequently if more severe disease. Glycemic and BP control reduce retinopathy risk and progression. Refer promptly to ophthalmology for DME, severe NPDR, or PDR. Anti-VEGF agents are first-line for center-involving DME.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Annually assess UACR and eGFR in T1DM duration greater than 5 years and all T2DM patients (Class B). DKD defined as persistently elevated UACR (greater than or equal to 30 mg/g) and/or reduced eGFR (less than 60 mL/min). ACEi or ARB at maximum tolerated dose recommended for DKD with UACR greater than or equal to 30 (Class A). SGLT2i recommended for eGFR greater than or equal to 20 to slow CKD progression (Class A). Finerenone recommended for DKD at risk of progression (Class A).

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Screen all patients with T2DM at diagnosis and T1DM after 5 years for distal symmetric polyneuropathy (DSPN) using: 10-g monofilament, vibration sensation (128 Hz tuning fork), ankle reflexes. Screen annually thereafter (Class B). Optimize glycemic control to prevent/slow neuropathy progression (Class B). For painful DPN: first-line agents include duloxetine, pregabalin, or gabapentin. TCAs may be used but require cardiac monitoring. Tapentadol for refractory cases.

guidelines.guideline: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes (2024)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Perform comprehensive foot evaluation at least annually: inspection, pedal pulses, sensation testing (10-g monofilament plus 1 of: vibration, pinprick, ankle reflexes, temperature) (Class B). More frequent exam in high-risk patients (prior ulcer/amputation, neuropathy, PAD, foot deformity). Provide foot self-care education. Refer high-risk patients to foot care specialist. Multidisciplinary teams recommended for diabetic foot ulcers. Offloading and debridement are cornerstone of ulcer treatment.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: CKD is defined as abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for greater than 3 months. Classified by GFR category (G1-G5) and albuminuria category (A1-A3). G1: eGFR greater than or equal to 90 (normal or high), G2: 60-89 (mildly decreased), G3a: 45-59 (mildly-moderately decreased), G3b: 30-44 (moderately-severely decreased), G4: 15-29 (severely decreased), G5: less than 15 (kidney failure). Albuminuria: A1 less than 30 mg/g (normal), A2 30-300 mg/g (moderately increased), A3 greater than 300 mg/g (severely increased).

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use the CKD-EPI 2021 creatinine equation for GFR estimation (race-free equation) (Grade 1B). The equation uses age, sex, and serum creatinine. Cystatin C-based or combined creatinine-cystatin C equations recommended for confirmatory testing when eGFR is near decision thresholds or when creatinine-based estimates are less accurate (extremes of body composition, muscle wasting). Report eGFR with creatinine as primary measurement; consider cystatin C for confirmation.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use UACR from spot urine sample for initial detection of increased urinary albumin (Grade 1B). First morning void preferred but random sample acceptable. Confirm elevated UACR (greater than or equal to 30 mg/g) with repeat testing (at least 2 of 3 samples elevated) before diagnosing CKD. UACR provides better prognostic information than total protein or dipstick. Albuminuria category is an independent predictor of CKD progression, CVD, and mortality.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Assess CKD progression risk using the Kidney Failure Risk Equation (KFRE), which predicts 2-year and 5-year risk of kidney failure requiring dialysis/transplant (Grade 1B). Variables: age, sex, eGFR, UACR. Higher risk warrants more intensive monitoring and intervention. Risk factors for progression include: lower eGFR, higher albuminuria, hypertension, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, AKI episodes, African ancestry, male sex, younger age at onset, and smoking.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Target systolic BP less than 120 mmHg for adults with CKD (Grade 2B), measured using standardized office BP measurement. More conservative target (less than 130/80 or less than 140/90) may be appropriate in elderly, frail patients, or those with orthostatic hypotension or significant comorbidities. Lifestyle modifications recommended for all. Intensive BP control reduces cardiovascular events and slows CKD progression. Monitor for hypotension, AKI, and electrolyte abnormalities.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: ACE inhibitors or ARBs are recommended for adults with CKD and severely increased albuminuria (UACR greater than 300 mg/g) with or without diabetes (Grade 1B). ACEi/ARBs are suggested for adults with CKD and moderately increased albuminuria (UACR 30-300 mg/g) with diabetes (Grade 2C). Titrate to maximum tolerated dose for optimal renal protection. Acceptable to have up to 30 percent increase in creatinine after initiation; if greater, evaluate for volume depletion, renovascular disease.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: SGLT2 inhibitors with proven kidney benefit (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin) are recommended for adults with CKD and eGFR greater than or equal to 20 mL/min, with or without diabetes, to reduce CKD progression and cardiovascular events (Grade 1A). May be initiated down to eGFR 20 and continued until dialysis or transplant. Expected initial decline in eGFR (hemodynamic effect) is acceptable and does not warrant discontinuation unless greater than 30 percent. Hold during acute illness.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Evaluate anemia when Hb less than 13 g/dL (males) or less than 12 g/dL (females). Workup includes CBC, reticulocyte count, ferritin, TSAT, B12, folate. Iron deficiency defined as TSAT less than 20 percent and/or ferritin less than 100 ng/mL (ND-CKD) or less than 200 ng/mL (dialysis). Correct iron deficiency before or concurrent with ESA therapy (Grade 1B). ESA target Hb 10-11.5 g/dL; do not intentionally exceed 13 g/dL due to CV risk. Oral iron may be inadequate; IV iron often needed.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Monitor serum calcium, phosphorus, PTH, and alkaline phosphatase starting in CKD G3a (Grade 1C). Frequency based on CKD stage: G3a-b every 6-12 months, G4 every 3-6 months, G5 every 1-3 months for phosphorus/calcium, every 3-6 months for PTH. Treat hyperphosphatemia with dietary restriction and phosphate binders. PTH target not specified; avoid levels extremely above or below normal. Vitamin D supplementation if deficient; active vitamin D (calcitriol, paricalcitol) for severe/progressive hyperparathyroidism.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Consider oral bicarbonate supplementation to maintain serum bicarbonate in the normal range (22-26 mEq/L) for patients with CKD and serum bicarbonate less than 22 mEq/L (Grade 2B). Metabolic acidosis accelerates CKD progression, promotes muscle wasting, and worsens bone disease. Sodium bicarbonate or sodium citrate can be used. Monitor for volume overload due to sodium content. Dietary interventions (increased fruit/vegetable intake) may also improve acidosis.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (2012)
guidelines.recommendation_text: AKI is defined as: Increase in serum creatinine by greater than or equal to 0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours, OR increase in serum creatinine to greater than or equal to 1.5 times baseline within 7 days, OR urine volume less than 0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours. Diagnosis requires only one criterion. This unified definition replaces RIFLE and AKIN criteria. Early recognition allows for timely intervention and prevention of further injury.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (2012)
guidelines.recommendation_text: AKI Stage 1: Creatinine 1.5-1.9 times baseline OR increase greater than or equal to 0.3 mg/dL, OR urine output less than 0.5 mL/kg/h for 6-12 hours. Stage 2: Creatinine 2.0-2.9 times baseline, OR urine output less than 0.5 mL/kg/h for greater than or equal to 12 hours. Stage 3: Creatinine greater than or equal to 3.0 times baseline OR increase to greater than or equal to 4.0 mg/dL OR initiation of RRT, OR urine output less than 0.3 mL/kg/h for greater than or equal to 24 hours OR anuria for greater than or equal to 12 hours. Stage determines prognosis and guides management intensity.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (2012)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Maintain adequate volume status and renal perfusion pressure. Avoid nephrotoxic medications when possible (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, amphotericin B). Review medication dosing based on renal function. Consider alternatives to iodinated contrast when possible. In high-risk patients, use lowest contrast dose, iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast, and ensure adequate hydration. Monitor high-risk patients (CKD, diabetes, heart failure, sepsis, major surgery) closely for early AKI detection.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (2012)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Identify patients at risk for CI-AKI: pre-existing CKD (eGFR less than 60), diabetes with CKD, heart failure, hypotension, high contrast volume, intra-arterial contrast. Use lowest possible contrast dose. Volume expansion with isotonic saline (1.0-1.5 mL/kg/h for 3-12 hours before and 6-12 hours after) is recommended for at-risk patients (Grade 1A). N-acetylcysteine is not recommended (no proven benefit). Discontinue metformin in high-risk patients; reassess eGFR at 48-72 hours.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (2012)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Adjust medication doses for reduced kidney function in AKI. Estimated GFR in AKI is unreliable; use clinical judgment and consider creatinine trajectory. Renally cleared drugs require dose reduction and/or extended intervals. Therapeutic drug monitoring recommended for aminoglycosides, vancomycin, and other nephrotoxic antibiotics. Consider protein binding changes and volume of distribution alterations. Avoid nephrotoxic drugs; if unavoidable, use shortest duration with closest monitoring.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury (2012)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Initiate RRT emergently when life-threatening changes exist: refractory hyperkalemia, severe metabolic acidosis, pulmonary edema/volume overload unresponsive to diuretics, uremic complications (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding) (Grade 1A). Consider broader clinical context when deciding to start RRT, including trajectory of kidney function and overall patient status. CRRT and IHD have similar outcomes in most patients; CRRT preferred for hemodynamically unstable patients. Delivered dose for CRRT should be 20-25 mL/kg/h.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Individualized A1C target range of 6.5-8.0 percent is recommended for patients with diabetes and CKD not treated with dialysis (Grade 1C). A target around 7 percent is reasonable for most patients. More relaxed target (7.5-8 percent) for patients with limited life expectancy, advanced CKD, extensive comorbidities, or hypoglycemia unawareness. A1C may underestimate glycemic control in CKD due to shortened RBC lifespan, anemia, and EPO use; consider CGM for more accurate assessment.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Metformin can be continued or initiated in patients with T2DM and CKD with eGFR greater than or equal to 30 mL/min/1.73m2 (Grade 1B). Dose reduction recommended for eGFR 30-45 mL/min (maximum 1000 mg/day). Should not be initiated if eGFR less than 30; may continue with close monitoring in stable patients already on therapy. Discontinue if eGFR falls below 30 or during acute illness with risk of lactic acidosis (sepsis, hypoxia, dehydration, contrast procedures). Monitor B12 levels.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: GLP-1 receptor agonists are recommended in patients with T2DM and CKD who have not achieved glycemic targets despite use of metformin and SGLT2i, or who are unable to use those medications (Grade 1B). GLP-1 RAs reduce cardiovascular events and have demonstrated kidney benefits. Most GLP-1 RAs can be used without dose adjustment in CKD (semaglutide, dulaglutide). Exenatide should be avoided in eGFR less than 30. Consider for weight management benefits in CKD patients with obesity.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: SGLT2 inhibitors with proven kidney or cardiovascular benefit (empagliflozin, canagliflozin, dapagliflozin) are recommended for patients with T2DM and CKD with eGFR greater than or equal to 20 mL/min/1.73m2 (Grade 1A). First-line therapy for diabetic kidney disease. Reduce risk of kidney failure progression by 30-40 percent and cardiovascular events. Once started, may be continued even if eGFR falls below 20 until dialysis or transplant. Initial eGFR dip (hemodynamic effect) is expected and not a reason to discontinue.

guidelines.guideline: KDIGO 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in CKD
guidelines.recommendation_text: Finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) is recommended for adults with T2DM, eGFR greater than or equal to 25 mL/min, normal serum potassium, and albuminuria (UACR greater than or equal to 30 mg/g) despite maximum tolerated dose of ACEi or ARB (Grade 1A). Finerenone reduces risk of CKD progression and cardiovascular events (FIDELIO-DKD, FIGARO-DKD trials). Start 10 mg daily if eGFR greater than or equal to 60, or 10 mg daily if eGFR 25-59. Monitor potassium within 4 weeks of initiation and after dose changes.

guidelines.section_title: Outpatient Treatment - Healthy Adults Without Comorbidities
guidelines.recommendation_text: For outpatient treatment of CAP in adults without comorbidities or risk factors for resistant pathogens, monotherapy with amoxicillin (1g TID) OR doxycycline (100mg BID) OR a macrolide (azithromycin 500mg day 1 then 250mg days 2-5, or clarithromycin 500mg BID) is recommended. Macrolide monotherapy should only be used in areas with pneumococcal macrolide resistance <25%. Duration is typically 5 days, extending only if clinical stability not achieved.

For outpatient CAP in adults with comorbidities (chronic heart, lung, liver, or renal disease; diabetes; alcoholism; malignancy; asplenia), combination therapy is recommended: amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125mg BID or 2g BID) OR cephalosporin (cefpodoxime 200mg BID or cefuroxime 500mg BID) PLUS macrolide (azithromycin or clarithromycin) OR doxycycline. Alternative: respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy (moxifloxacin 400mg daily or levofloxacin 750mg daily).

Empiric coverage for MRSA (vancomycin 15mg/kg IV q12h or linezolid 600mg IV/PO q12h) should be added when: prior respiratory isolation of MRSA, recent hospitalization with parenteral antibiotics in past 90 days, cavitary infiltrate, empyema, or severe CAP with risk factors for MRSA. Discontinue MRSA coverage if cultures negative at 48-72 hours and patient improving. Nasal MRSA PCR negative has high NPV for MRSA pneumonia.

Empiric antipseudomonal coverage should be added for severe CAP when: prior respiratory isolation of P. aeruginosa, structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis), frequent COPD exacerbations requiring steroids/antibiotics, or recent hospitalization with IV antibiotics. Use antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem, or imipenem) plus respiratory fluoroquinolone or aminoglycoside. De-escalate based on culture results.

Procalcitonin (PCT) should NOT be used to determine initial antibiotic therapy in CAP - antibiotics should be initiated regardless of PCT level in clinically diagnosed CAP. PCT can guide duration of therapy: consider discontinuation when PCT <0.25 ng/mL or >80% decline from peak, along with clinical improvement. PCT levels help distinguish bacterial from viral infection but should not override clinical judgment.

guidelines.section_title: CURB-65 and PSI for Severity Assessment
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use validated severity scores to guide site of care. CURB-65 (Confusion, Uremia BUN>19, Respiratory rate>=30, Blood pressure SBP<90 or DBP<=60, age>=65): 0-1=outpatient, 2=consider admission, 3-5=inpatient/ICU. PSI (Pneumonia Severity Index) class I-II=outpatient, III=observation, IV-V=inpatient. Severe CAP criteria for ICU: major criteria (septic shock requiring vasopressors, respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation) or >=3 minor criteria.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA/ATS Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Guideline (2016)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For HAP not at high risk for mortality and no risk factors for MRSA, use single agent: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h, cefepime 2g IV q8h, levofloxacin 750mg IV daily, imipenem 500mg IV q6h, or meropenem 1g IV q8h. For HAP with high mortality risk or risk factors for MRSA (prior MRSA, IV antibiotics in past 90 days, MRSA prevalence >20%), add vancomycin or linezolid. Duration 7 days for most HAP.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA/ATS Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Guideline (2016)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For VAP, use antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, ceftazidime, meropenem, imipenem, or aztreonam) PLUS second antipseudomonal agent (fluoroquinolone or aminoglycoside) if MDR risk factors present. Add MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid) if MRSA risk factors. MDR risk factors: IV antibiotics in past 90 days, septic shock, ARDS, >5 days hospitalization before VAP, acute RRT. De-escalate to narrowest spectrum based on cultures.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA/ATS Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Guideline (2016)
guidelines.recommendation_text: De-escalation of empiric therapy is recommended once culture results and clinical response are available, typically at 48-72 hours. Narrow spectrum based on identified pathogen and susceptibilities. Discontinue MRSA and antipseudomonal coverage if not isolated. If cultures negative and patient improving, de-escalate to narrowest spectrum active against likely pathogens. De-escalation is safe and reduces antibiotic exposure without increasing mortality.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA/ATS Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Guideline (2016)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Inhaled antibiotics (tobramycin, amikacin, colistin/polymyxin) may be considered as adjunctive therapy for VAP caused by MDR gram-negative pathogens susceptible only to aminoglycosides or polymyxins. Use in addition to (not instead of) systemic antibiotics. Typical dosing: tobramycin 300mg nebulized BID or colistin 75-150mg nebulized BID. Monitor for bronchospasm. Evidence is limited but may improve clinical cure in difficult-to-treat infections.

For acute uncomplicated cystitis in women, first-line options: nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals 100mg BID x 5 days, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS BID x 3 days (if local resistance <20%), or fosfomycin 3g single dose. Second-line: fluoroquinolones (ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin) x 3 days. Beta-lactams (amoxicillin-clavulanate, cefdinir, cefpodoxime) are less effective but alternatives if others contraindicated.

For uncomplicated pyelonephritis, outpatient treatment: ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x 7 days (or 1000mg XR daily x 7 days) OR ciprofloxacin 500mg PO + single IV dose ceftriaxone 1g if fluoroquinolone resistance suspected. Alternative: TMP-SMX DS BID x 14 days (with initial IV ceftriaxone if TMP-SMX resistance suspected). Inpatient treatment for severe cases: ceftriaxone 1g IV daily, fluoroquinolone IV, or aminoglycoside + ampicillin.

DO NOT treat asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB) in most patients including: elderly, diabetics, spinal cord injury, or indwelling catheters. TREAT ASB only in: pregnancy (screen at 12-16 weeks, treat to prevent pyelonephritis) and before urologic procedures with mucosal bleeding. Pyuria alone without symptoms is NOT an indication for treatment. Treatment of ASB increases antibiotic resistance without clinical benefit.

For recurrent UTI (>=3 infections/year), prevention strategies: behavioral (post-coital voiding, increased hydration), vaginal estrogen for postmenopausal women, D-mannose supplementation. Antibiotic prophylaxis options: continuous low-dose (nitrofurantoin 50-100mg daily, TMP-SMX half-tablet daily) or post-coital single dose. Self-start therapy with patient-initiated antibiotics at symptom onset. Cranberry products may reduce recurrence but evidence mixed.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Guideline (2014)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For impetigo, topical therapy with mupirocin or retapamulin ointment BID x 5 days is recommended for limited lesions. Oral therapy for extensive disease: dicloxacillin 250mg QID or cephalexin 250mg QID x 7 days for non-MRSA. For MRSA impetigo: doxycycline 100mg BID, TMP-SMX DS BID, or clindamycin 300-400mg TID x 7 days. Good wound hygiene and handwashing to prevent spread.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Guideline (2014)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For purulent SSTI (abscess, furuncle, carbuncle), incision and drainage (I&D) is the primary treatment. Antibiotics in addition to I&D recommended for: abscess >=2cm, multiple lesions, extensive surrounding cellulitis, systemic signs (fever), immunocompromised, or inadequate I&D. Cover MRSA: TMP-SMX DS BID, doxycycline 100mg BID, or clindamycin 300-450mg TID x 5-10 days. Send wound culture to guide therapy.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Guideline (2014)
guidelines.recommendation_text: For non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess or drainage), target beta-hemolytic streptococci. Mild: cephalexin 500mg QID or dicloxacillin 500mg QID x 5-7 days. Moderate: cefazolin 1g IV q8h or nafcillin 1-2g IV q4h. Severe: vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam to cover MRSA and gram-negatives until culture data available. Mark borders to assess progression. Elevate affected extremity.

guidelines.guideline: IDSA Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Guideline (2014)
guidelines.recommendation_text: Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Immediate surgical debridement is essential - do not delay for imaging. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics: vancomycin or daptomycin (MRSA) PLUS piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (gram-negatives and anaerobes) PLUS clindamycin (toxin suppression). Type I (polymicrobial): diabetics, surgical site, perineal. Type II (monomicrobial): Group A Strep, often in healthy adults. LRINEC score >6 suggests necrotizing infection.

guidelines.section_title: Diabetic Foot Infection Classification and Treatment
guidelines.recommendation_text: Classify diabetic foot infections: mild (superficial, <2cm cellulitis), moderate (deeper infection or >2cm cellulitis), severe (systemic toxicity or metabolic instability). Mild: oral therapy targeting Staph/Strep (cephalexin, dicloxacillin, or amoxicillin-clavulanate) x 1-2 weeks. Moderate: broader coverage including gram-negatives. Severe: IV broad-spectrum (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem). Obtain deep tissue culture, not swab. Assess for osteomyelitis with probe-to-bone test and MRI.

Within 1 hour of sepsis recognition: (1) Measure lactate, re-measure if initial >2 mmol/L; (2) Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics (do not delay antibiotics if cultures cannot be obtained quickly); (3) Administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; (4) Begin rapid crystalloid infusion 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L; (5) Apply vasopressors if hypotensive during or after fluid resuscitation to maintain MAP >=65 mmHg. Time zero is triage or earliest recognition of sepsis elements.

Administer at least 30 mL/kg of IV crystalloid within the first 3 hours for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion (hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L). Balanced crystalloids (lactated Ringer's, Plasma-Lyte) preferred over normal saline. After initial resuscitation, use dynamic measures to guide additional fluid (pulse pressure variation, passive leg raise, fluid challenge). Avoid excessive fluid administration. Albumin may be used when patients require substantial amounts of crystalloid.

Norepinephrine is the first-line vasopressor for septic shock (target MAP >=65 mmHg). Add vasopressin (up to 0.03 units/min) as second-line agent to reduce norepinephrine dose or if MAP target not achieved. Epinephrine as third-line. Avoid dopamine except in highly selected patients (low risk of tachyarrhythmia with bradycardia). Add dobutamine for cardiac dysfunction with persistent hypoperfusion despite adequate volume and MAP.

Administer IV antimicrobials as soon as possible, ideally within 1 hour of sepsis recognition for sepsis and septic shock. Each hour delay in antibiotic administration is associated with increased mortality. Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics if possible, but do NOT delay antibiotics for culture collection. Use broad-spectrum empiric coverage initially, then de-escalate based on culture results and clinical response.

Identify and control the source of infection as rapidly as possible. Source control interventions include: drainage of abscess, debridement of necrotic tissue, removal of infected device, definitive surgery for ongoing contamination. Achieve source control within 6-12 hours of diagnosis when feasible. Use least invasive intervention that achieves source control. Balance urgency of intervention against risks of procedure and physiologic stability.

Measure lactate at presentation. If initial lactate >2 mmol/L, re-measure within 2-4 hours. Guide resuscitation to normalize lactate (target lactate clearance >=10-20% every 2 hours). Lactate >4 mmol/L indicates severe tissue hypoperfusion. Serial lactate measurements help assess response to therapy. Lactate clearance is associated with improved outcomes. Consider alternative sources of elevated lactate (medications, liver dysfunction, seizures).

For adults with septic shock and ongoing vasopressor requirement, suggest IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50mg q6h or 200mg continuous infusion) rather than no corticosteroids. Most benefit in patients requiring escalating vasopressor doses despite adequate resuscitation. Duration typically 3-7 days, tapered based on clinical response. Do NOT use corticosteroids in sepsis without shock. Consider fludrocortisone 50 mcg daily in addition to hydrocortisone.

For initial non-severe CDI: fidaxomicin 200mg BID x 10 days (preferred) OR vancomycin 125mg PO QID x 10 days. Metronidazole 500mg TID x 10-14 days only if fidaxomicin/vancomycin unavailable. For severe CDI (WBC >=15,000 or Cr >=1.5 mg/dL): vancomycin 125mg PO QID or fidaxomicin 200mg BID. For fulminant CDI (hypotension, ileus, megacolon): vancomycin 500mg PO/NG QID PLUS metronidazole 500mg IV q8h, consider rectal vancomycin if ileus. Discontinue inciting antibiotic if possible.

For first recurrence: fidaxomicin 200mg BID x 10 days or extended-pulsed fidaxomicin regimen (preferred), OR vancomycin taper and pulse (125mg QID x 10-14 days, then BID x 7 days, then daily x 7 days, then every 2-3 days x 2-8 weeks). For second or subsequent recurrence: fidaxomicin if not used previously, vancomycin taper, OR fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT). Bezlotoxumab (anti-toxin B antibody) reduces recurrence risk. Consider FMT after 2+ recurrences despite appropriate antibiotic therapy.

Antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures is recommended ONLY for highest-risk patients: (1) prosthetic cardiac valve or prosthetic material for valve repair, (2) previous infective endocarditis, (3) unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease or repaired CHD with residual shunt/valvular regurgitation, (4) cardiac transplant recipients with valvulopathy. Amoxicillin 2g PO 30-60 minutes before procedure. Ampicillin 2g IV/IM if unable to take PO. Penicillin allergy: cephalexin 2g, azithromycin 500mg, or clindamycin 600mg.

Empiric therapy for native valve endocarditis pending cultures: vancomycin + ceftriaxone (or ampicillin-sulbactam for HACEK coverage). MSSA: nafcillin or oxacillin 2g IV q4h x 6 weeks (add gentamicin first 3-5 days optional). MRSA: vancomycin (trough 15-20) or daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg IV daily x 6 weeks. Viridans streptococci (penicillin-susceptible): penicillin G or ceftriaxone x 4 weeks. Enterococcus: ampicillin + gentamicin (or ceftriaxone for E. faecalis) x 4-6 weeks.

guidelines.section_title: Vertebral and Long Bone Osteomyelitis Treatment
guidelines.recommendation_text: Obtain bone biopsy for culture before antibiotics when feasible; blood cultures positive in 50% of vertebral osteomyelitis. Empiric therapy: vancomycin (MRSA) + ceftriaxone or fluoroquinolone (gram-negatives). MSSA: nafcillin/oxacillin or cefazolin. MRSA: vancomycin or daptomycin. Duration: 6 weeks IV antibiotics for vertebral osteomyelitis; consider transition to oral after 2-3 weeks if bioavailable agent available and clinical improvement. Long bone: 4-6 weeks, may need surgery. Monitor CRP/ESR for response.

For community-acquired intra-abdominal infection, cover gram-negatives, gram-positives, and anaerobes. Mild-moderate severity: ertapenem, moxifloxacin, or cefazolin/cefuroxime/ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or ticarcillin-clavulanate. High severity or healthcare-associated: piperacillin-tazobactam, imipenem, meropenem, or cefepime + metronidazole. Add vancomycin if MRSA risk. Duration: 4-7 days following adequate source control; 5-7 days if no source control possible. Longer duration does not improve outcomes.

After confirming COPD diagnosis with spirometry (post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70), assess symptoms and exacerbation risk using GOLD ABCD tool. Group A: low symptoms (mMRC 0-1 or CAT <10), 0-1 moderate exacerbations. Group B: high symptoms (mMRC >=2 or CAT >=10), 0-1 moderate exacerbations. Group E: >=2 moderate exacerbations or >=1 hospitalization regardless of symptoms. Guide initial therapy based on group assignment.

Group A: bronchodilator (SABA, SAMA, LABA, or LAMA). Group B: LAMA + LABA combination preferred. Group E: LAMA + LABA; consider LAMA + LABA + ICS (triple therapy) if blood eosinophils >=300 cells/uL. ICS-containing therapy appropriate when: eosinophils >=300, history of or concomitant asthma, or >=2 moderate exacerbations despite dual bronchodilator. Avoid ICS if repeated pneumonia, inappropriate prior ICS use without indication, or mycobacterial infection.

Triple therapy (ICS + LAMA + LABA) is indicated for: COPD patients with blood eosinophils >=300 cells/uL who remain symptomatic or have exacerbations on LAMA + LABA; or eosinophils 100-300 with >=2 moderate exacerbations or >=1 hospitalization. IMPACT and ETHOS trials showed mortality benefit with triple therapy vs dual bronchodilators in appropriate patients. Use single-inhaler triple therapy (SITT) when possible for adherence. Examples: fluticasone furoate/umeclidinium/vilanterol, budesonide/glycopyrrolate/formoterol.

Systemic corticosteroids improve FEV1, oxygenation, and shorten recovery time in COPD exacerbations. Recommended: prednisone 40mg daily (or equivalent) for 5 days. Longer courses do not provide additional benefit. IV methylprednisolone may be used for severe exacerbations or inability to take PO. Consider withholding or reducing steroids in milder exacerbations managed outpatient. Blood eosinophils >=300 during exacerbation predict better response to steroids.

Antibiotics recommended for COPD exacerbations with: (1) all three cardinal symptoms (increased dyspnea, sputum volume, and sputum purulence), (2) increased sputum purulence plus one other cardinal symptom, or (3) need for mechanical ventilation. Duration: 5-7 days. Common agents: amoxicillin-clavulanate, azithromycin, doxycycline, or respiratory fluoroquinolone. Procalcitonin <0.25 ng/mL may help guide decision to withhold antibiotics.

Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) is indicated for: PaO2 <=55 mmHg or SpO2 <=88% at rest (confirmed on 2 occasions 3 weeks apart while stable), OR PaO2 55-60 mmHg with evidence of pulmonary hypertension, peripheral edema, or polycythemia (Hct >55%). Goal: SpO2 >=90% or PaO2 >=60 mmHg. Prescribe for >=15 hours/day for mortality benefit. LOTT trial showed no benefit for moderate resting or exercise-induced hypoxemia.

Noninvasive ventilation (NIV) is first-line for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure in COPD exacerbation. Indications: respiratory acidosis (pH <=7.35 and PaCO2 >=45 mmHg), severe dyspnea with accessory muscle use, persistent hypoxemia despite supplemental O2. NIV reduces need for intubation, ICU stay, and mortality. Contraindications: respiratory arrest, cardiovascular instability, impaired mental status, inability to protect airway, facial trauma. Use full-face or oronasal mask; typical settings IPAP 10-20, EPAP 4-6.

Pulmonary rehabilitation is the most effective therapeutic strategy to improve dyspnea, health status, and exercise tolerance in COPD. Recommend for all symptomatic patients with FEV1 <80% predicted. Minimum 6-8 weeks program duration, 2-3 sessions/week. Components: endurance training, strength training, education, psychosocial support, nutritional counseling. Particularly important after hospitalization for exacerbation (reduces readmissions). Benefits persist 1-2 years with maintenance exercise.

All COPD patients should receive: (1) Annual influenza vaccine, (2) Pneumococcal vaccination (PCV20 alone, or PCV15 followed by PPSV23), (3) Tdap once then Td/Tdap boosters, (4) COVID-19 vaccine per current recommendations, (5) Zoster vaccine (Shingrix 2 doses) for age >=50, (6) RSV vaccine for age >=60. Vaccination reduces exacerbations, hospitalizations, and mortality in COPD. Administer during stable disease if possible.

Asthma diagnosis requires: (1) History of variable respiratory symptoms (wheeze, shortness of breath, chest tightness, cough) that vary over time and in intensity, AND (2) Confirmed variable expiratory airflow limitation on spirometry (FEV1 increase >12% and >200mL after bronchodilator, OR positive bronchial challenge test, OR excessive variation in peak flow). If spirometry normal when symptomatic, consider alternative diagnosis. Document diagnosis before starting controller therapy when possible.

Assess asthma control using 4 questions over past 4 weeks: (1) daytime symptoms >2/week, (2) any night waking due to asthma, (3) reliever needed >2/week, (4) any activity limitation. Well-controlled: none of these. Partly controlled: 1-2 of these. Uncontrolled: 3-4 of these. Also assess risk factors for exacerbations: poor control, high SABA use (>=3 canisters/year), low FEV1, smoking, eosinophilia, major psychosocial problems, prior intubation.

GINA recommends ICS-formoterol as preferred reliever across all steps of asthma treatment (MART: Maintenance And Reliever Therapy). As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol reduces severe exacerbations compared to SABA reliever. For mild asthma (Steps 1-2): as-needed ICS-formoterol alone is preferred over daily ICS + SABA reliever. For moderate-severe asthma: maintenance ICS-formoterol plus as-needed ICS-formoterol for relief. Avoid SABA-only treatment without ICS.

Step 1-2 (mild): As-needed low-dose ICS-formoterol (preferred) OR daily low-dose ICS + as-needed SABA. Step 3: Low-dose ICS-formoterol maintenance and reliever (MART). Step 4: Medium-dose ICS-formoterol MART, or add LAMA. Step 5: High-dose ICS-LABA, add-on LAMA, consider biologic (anti-IgE, anti-IL5/5R, anti-IL4R). Step up if uncontrolled after checking adherence/technique. Step down after 3 months good control; reduce ICS dose by 25-50%. Never stop ICS completely.

guidelines.section_title: Biologic Therapy Indications for Severe Asthma
guidelines.recommendation_text: Consider biologic add-on therapy for severe asthma uncontrolled on Step 4-5 treatment with type 2 inflammation biomarkers. Anti-IgE (omalizumab): allergic asthma, elevated IgE. Anti-IL5/IL5R (mepolizumab, benralizumab, reslizumab): eosinophilic asthma, blood eos >=150 (mepolizumab) or >=300 (benralizumab). Anti-IL4R (dupilumab): type 2 inflammation, eos >=150 or FeNO >=25, also for comorbid atopic dermatitis/CRSwNP. Anti-TSLP (tezepelumab): broad severe asthma regardless of phenotype.

Mild-moderate exacerbation: increase ICS-formoterol (up to maximum 12 actuations/day) or add prednisone 40-50mg/day x 5-7 days. Severe exacerbation (PEF <50%, life-threatening features): oxygen to maintain SpO2 94-98%, SABA via nebulizer or MDI+spacer frequently, systemic corticosteroids immediately (prednisone 40-50mg or IV methylprednisolone), ipratropium if severe, consider IV magnesium sulfate 2g over 20 min. Assess response q1h. ICU for impending respiratory failure.

Actively manage asthma during pregnancy - uncontrolled asthma poses greater risk to fetus than asthma medications. Continue controller medications (ICS, LABA, LTRA safe in pregnancy). Budesonide is the most studied ICS. Do not stop ICS due to pregnancy. Treat exacerbations aggressively with SABA, systemic corticosteroids, and oxygen. Monitor asthma more frequently (monthly). Avoid known triggers. Maintain FEV1 >80% predicted. Breastfeeding: continue asthma medications.

Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) is common in asthma and may be the only symptom. For patients on regular ICS: optimize controller therapy as first step. Pre-exercise: SABA or ICS-formoterol 15 min before exercise. Non-pharmacologic: adequate warm-up, breathing through nose, avoiding cold dry air.
