For EIB without chronic asthma: as-needed ICS-formoterol or SABA before exercise. Daily low-dose ICS if frequent symptoms. Consider LTRA as alternative.

Severe asthma is defined as asthma requiring Step 4-5 treatment (medium/high-dose ICS-LABA) to prevent becoming uncontrolled, or remaining uncontrolled despite this treatment. Before labeling severe asthma, confirm diagnosis, assess adherence and inhaler technique, treat comorbidities (rhinosinusitis, GERD, obesity, OSA), eliminate triggers. Phenotype severe asthma: check blood eosinophils, total IgE, FeNO, skin prick tests, and sputum eosinophils if available. Refer to specialist for biologic consideration.

guidelines.section_title: Risk Stratification in Upper GI Bleeding
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use validated risk scores for risk stratification. Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) for identifying low-risk patients suitable for outpatient management: GBS 0-1 can be considered for outpatient EGD. AIMS65 and Rockall scores predict mortality. Components of GBS: BUN, hemoglobin, systolic BP, heart rate, melena, syncope, hepatic disease, heart failure. High-risk features: hemodynamic instability, active hematemesis, high GBS, coagulopathy.

Initial resuscitation with crystalloid fluids to restore hemodynamic stability. Restrictive transfusion strategy: transfuse pRBCs for hemoglobin <7 g/dL in most patients; threshold of 8 g/dL for patients with cardiovascular disease. Over-transfusion associated with increased rebleeding and mortality in variceal bleeding. Correct coagulopathy if INR >2.5. Platelet transfusion if <50,000 with active bleeding. Reverse anticoagulation as appropriate.

Start IV PPI (esomeprazole or pantoprazole 80mg bolus, then 8mg/hr infusion, or 40mg IV BID) before endoscopy in patients with suspected peptic ulcer bleeding. Post-endoscopy: continue high-dose IV PPI for 72 hours after endoscopic therapy for high-risk ulcer stigmata (active bleeding, visible vessel, adherent clot). Then transition to oral PPI BID x 2 weeks, then daily. Intermittent IV PPI dosing (80mg q12h) may be as effective as continuous infusion.

Perform upper endoscopy within 24 hours of presentation for most patients with UGIB after resuscitation. Urgent EGD (<12 hours) may be considered for high-risk patients (hemodynamic instability, bloody NG aspirate, high GBS). Very early EGD (<6 hours) has not been shown to improve outcomes. Delay EGD in hemodynamically unstable patients until adequately resuscitated. For low-risk patients (GBS 0-1), outpatient EGD within 24-48 hours is appropriate.

Test all patients with peptic ulcer bleeding for H. pylori. Rapid urease test at time of EGD has lower sensitivity in acute bleeding setting - if negative, confirm with other modalities (stool antigen, urea breath test) or re-test after acute episode resolves. Treat H. pylori if positive. Re-test to confirm eradication 4 weeks after completing treatment (off PPI for 2 weeks). H. pylori eradication reduces ulcer recurrence and rebleeding.

guidelines.section_title: Risk Stratification in Lower GI Bleeding
guidelines.recommendation_text: Use Oakland Score for risk stratification in acute lower GI bleeding. Oakland Score <=8 identifies very low-risk patients suitable for outpatient management. Components: age, sex, prior LGIB hospitalization, DRE findings, heart rate, systolic BP, hemoglobin. High-risk features: hemodynamic instability, ongoing bleeding, anticoagulation, significant comorbidities. Most LGIB is self-limited (80-85%), but identify high-risk patients for inpatient management.

Perform colonoscopy within 24 hours for hemodynamically stable patients with acute LGIB after adequate bowel preparation. Urgent colonoscopy (<12 hours) has not been shown to improve outcomes compared to elective colonoscopy and may have lower diagnostic yield due to poor preparation. For patients with hemodynamic instability or high-risk features, consider CT angiography to localize bleeding before colonoscopy or angiographic intervention.

CT angiography (CTA) is recommended as first-line imaging for patients with severe or ongoing LGIB to localize bleeding source before intervention. CTA sensitivity highest with active bleeding rate >0.3-0.5 mL/min. Positive CTA guides angiographic embolization or surgical planning. Nuclear medicine tagged RBC scan detects slower bleeding (0.1-0.5 mL/min) but with lower spatial resolution. If CTA negative and bleeding stops, proceed with colonoscopy after preparation.

First-line ascites management: sodium restriction (2g/day = 88 mEq/day) plus diuretics. Start spironolactone 100mg daily, may add furosemide 40mg daily; maintain 100:40 ratio, titrate to max spironolactone 400mg + furosemide 160mg. Target weight loss 0.5 kg/day (no edema) or 1 kg/day (with edema). Large-volume paracentesis (LVP) for tense ascites; give albumin 6-8 g per liter removed if >5L removed. Discontinue diuretics if Na <120, AKI, HE, or muscle cramps.

Primary SBP prophylaxis with daily norfloxacin 400mg or TMP-SMX DS daily for: (1) ascitic fluid protein <1.5 g/dL with either Child-Pugh >=9 or serum creatinine >=1.2 or BUN >=25 or Na <=130, (2) GI bleeding in cirrhosis (short-term ceftriaxone 1g IV daily x 7 days). Secondary prophylaxis (after SBP episode): lifelong daily fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX. Rifaximin 550mg BID is alternative. SBP diagnosis: ascitic fluid PMN >=250 cells/mm3.

First-line treatment for overt hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose 25mL (15-45mL) q1-2h until at least 2 soft stools/day, then titrate to 2-3 soft BMs daily. For lactulose-refractory HE or secondary prophylaxis: add rifaximin 550mg BID. Identify and treat precipitants: GI bleeding, infection, constipation, hypokalemia, dehydration, medications (sedatives, opioids), dietary protein excess. Zinc supplementation if deficient. L-ornithine-L-aspartate (LOLA) as alternative. Evaluate for liver transplant.

Screen for varices with EGD at cirrhosis diagnosis; repeat every 2-3 years if no varices, annually if small varices or decompensation. Primary prophylaxis: NSBB (carvedilol 6.25-12.5mg daily preferred over propranolol/nadolol) OR endoscopic variceal ligation (EVL) for medium/large varices. Carvedilol may reduce varices progression. Combination NSBB + EVL not superior to either alone for primary prophylaxis. HVPG-guided therapy: target >20% reduction or <12 mmHg.

Perform HCC surveillance every 6 months with ultrasound (with or without AFP) for all patients with cirrhosis regardless of etiology. Patients with chronic HBV should undergo surveillance even without cirrhosis if: Asian male >40, Asian female >50, African/Black >20, or family history of HCC. MRI may be used if US quality suboptimal. CT or MRI with contrast for any suspicious lesion. LI-RADS classification for nodules. AFP alone is insufficient for surveillance.

TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) is indicated for: (1) refractory ascites (diuretic-resistant or intolerant), (2) recurrent variceal bleeding despite optimal endoscopic and pharmacologic therapy, (3) Budd-Chiari syndrome, (4) hepatic hydrothorax refractory to diuretics. Consider early TIPS (<72h) for high-risk variceal bleed (Child-Pugh C or B with active bleeding). Contraindications: severe HE, MELD >18-20 (relative), right heart failure, uncontrolled sepsis, biliary obstruction.

Induction therapy based on disease severity and location. Mild ileocecal: budesonide 9mg daily x 8-16 weeks. Moderate-severe: systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40-60mg/day, taper over 2-3 months) as bridge to steroid-sparing therapy. Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab, certolizumab) for moderate-severe disease, especially with prior steroid failure. Combination anti-TNF + immunomodulator (azathioprine/6-MP) superior to either alone for moderate-severe disease.

Steroid-sparing maintenance therapy required for all patients who required steroids for induction. Options: thiopurines (azathioprine 2-2.5 mg/kg or 6-MP 1-1.5 mg/kg), methotrexate 25mg SQ weekly, anti-TNF agents, vedolizumab, ustekinumab. Anti-TNF therapy preferred for moderate-severe disease or high-risk features (young age, deep ulcers, perianal disease, prior surgery). Therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) to optimize anti-TNF dosing. Corticosteroids are NOT appropriate for maintenance.

Perianal Crohn's requires multidisciplinary approach (gastroenterology + colorectal surgery). MRI pelvis for fistula assessment. Simple perianal fistula: antibiotics (ciprofloxacin + metronidazole) + surgical drainage if abscess. Complex fistula: anti-TNF therapy (infliximab has best evidence) + seton placement for drainage. Maintenance anti-TNF to prevent fistula recurrence. Azathioprine/6-MP may help maintain fistula closure. Avoid definitive fistulotomy in Crohn's (risk of non-healing wound).

For mild-moderate UC, use 5-aminosalicylates (5-ASA). Proctitis: mesalamine suppository 1g daily (topical preferred). Left-sided colitis: mesalamine enema + oral mesalamine >=2.4g/day. Pancolitis: oral mesalamine >=2.4g/day; once-daily dosing equivalent to divided doses. Combination oral + rectal 5-ASA superior to either alone. For 5-ASA refractory mild-moderate UC: budesonide MMX 9mg daily or oral corticosteroids.

Moderate-severe UC requires corticosteroids for induction (prednisone 40-60mg/day) with early initiation of steroid-sparing maintenance. Anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab, golimumab): infliximab + azathioprine combination superior to either alone (UC SUCCESS trial). Vedolizumab: gut-selective, preferred if infection/malignancy concern. Tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor): rapid onset, oral. Ustekinumab: effective for anti-TNF experienced patients. Ozanimod (S1P modulator): newer oral option.

Acute severe UC (Truelove-Witts criteria: >=6 bloody stools/day + systemic toxicity) is a medical emergency. Admit to hospital, NPO if toxic, IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 60mg/day or hydrocortisone 100mg TID). Assess response by day 3-5 using Oxford criteria. If no response to IV steroids: rescue therapy with infliximab 5-10mg/kg or cyclosporine 2-4mg/kg/day. Surgical consultation early. Colectomy if failing medical therapy, perforation, toxic megacolon, or massive hemorrhage.

Test for H. pylori in: active peptic ulcer disease, history of PUD (if not previously treated), gastric MALT lymphoma, after endoscopic resection of early gastric cancer, uninvestigated dyspepsia in patients <60 without alarm features (test-and-treat strategy), before long-term low-dose aspirin in high ulcer risk patients, unexplained iron deficiency anemia, idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura. Preferred noninvasive tests: urea breath test (UBT) or stool antigen. Hold PPI x 2 weeks before testing.

First-line regimens for 14 days (avoid 7-day regimens in North America due to high resistance): (1) Bismuth quadruple therapy: PPI BID + bismuth subsalicylate 524mg QID + metronidazole 250mg QID + tetracycline 500mg QID. (2) Concomitant therapy: PPI BID + amoxicillin 1g BID + clarithromycin 500mg BID + metronidazole 500mg BID. Clarithromycin triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin) only if local clarithromycin resistance <15% and patient has no prior macrolide exposure.

After treatment failure, avoid previously used antibiotics (especially clarithromycin and levofloxacin due to resistance). Options: (1) Bismuth quadruple therapy if not used first-line, (2) Levofloxacin triple (PPI + amoxicillin + levofloxacin) x 14 days, (3) Rifabutin-based therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + rifabutin) for multiply-failed patients. Consider culture with susceptibility testing after 2+ treatment failures. Vonoprazan-based regimens (potassium-competitive acid blocker) show improved eradication in studies.

Confirm H. pylori eradication in ALL treated patients using urea breath test (UBT) or stool antigen at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics. Hold PPI for at least 2 weeks before testing to avoid false negatives. H2 blockers may be used during PPI washout if needed. Re-treatment indicated if eradication not confirmed. Serology is NOT appropriate for confirmation of eradication (antibodies persist for years after successful treatment).

Screen for HBV with HBsAg, anti-HBs, and anti-HBc in: all persons born in regions with HBsAg prevalence >=2%, household/sexual contacts of HBV-infected persons, persons who have ever injected drugs, HIV-positive individuals, MSM, pregnant women, patients requiring immunosuppressive therapy (screen before starting), hemodialysis patients, patients with elevated ALT/AST of unknown etiology. Test all before starting hepatotoxic medications or immunosuppression.

Treat chronic HBV if: (1) Immune-active CHB: HBeAg-positive with ALT >2x ULN and HBV DNA >20,000 IU/mL, or HBeAg-negative with ALT >2x ULN and HBV DNA >2,000 IU/mL; (2) Cirrhosis with any detectable HBV DNA; (3) Immunosuppression-related reactivation risk; (4) HBV-related extrahepatic manifestations. Consider treatment if: ALT elevated but <2x ULN with high viral load, family history of HCC, liver biopsy showing significant fibrosis (>=F2).

First-line antivirals: entecavir 0.5mg daily (1mg if lamivudine-experienced), tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF) 300mg daily, or tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) 25mg daily. TAF preferred over TDF for patients with bone or renal disease risk. All have high barrier to resistance. Pegylated interferon alfa (48 weeks) is alternative for motivated patients seeking finite therapy (HBeAg+ with chance of seroconversion), but avoid in decompensated cirrhosis. Duration: indefinite for most; consider stopping after HBsAg loss.

On antiviral therapy: check HBV DNA every 3-6 months until undetectable, then every 6-12 months. Monitor ALT every 3-6 months. For TDF: monitor creatinine and phosphorus every 6-12 months. For entecavir: monitor renal function annually if risk factors. Check HBeAg and anti-HBe every 6-12 months if HBeAg+. Assess HBsAg annually; stop treatment if HBsAg loss confirmed (anti-HBs seroconversion ideal). Monitor for HCC per surveillance guidelines.

One-time universal HCV screening recommended for all adults aged 18+. Screen with anti-HCV antibody; if reactive, confirm with HCV RNA. Screen during each pregnancy. Rescreen annually for: ongoing risk (active injection drug use, HIV+ MSM), incarcerated individuals, hemodialysis patients. Test HCV RNA directly (skip antibody) in immunocompromised or acute HCV suspected. All HCV+ patients should be treated regardless of fibrosis stage.

Pan-genotypic regimens for treatment-naive patients: (1) Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir (Epclusa) 400/100mg daily x 12 weeks, (2) Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir (Mavyret) 300/120mg daily x 8 weeks (12 weeks if cirrhosis). Simplified treatment: no genotype, fibrosis staging, or resistance testing needed for most. Avoid NS3/4A protease inhibitors (glecaprevir) in decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C). Cure rate (SVR12) >95% across genotypes. Drug interactions: check HCV-drug interaction checkers.

Confirm SVR (sustained virologic response = cure) by checking HCV RNA at least 12 weeks after completing treatment (SVR12). Undetectable HCV RNA at SVR12 indicates >99% chance of permanent cure. Post-SVR: continue HCC surveillance indefinitely if advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis (every 6 months with US +/- AFP). No need for repeat HCV screening unless ongoing risk behaviors. Patients can be reinfected; counsel on prevention if ongoing risk.

For DAA treatment failure (rare, <5%): test for NS5A resistance-associated substitutions (RASs). Retreatment options: sofosbuvir/velpatasvir/voxilaprevir (Vosevi) x 12 weeks for NS5A-experienced patients. Glecaprevir/pibrentasvir x 16 weeks plus weight-based ribavirin for select patients. Sofosbuvir + glecaprevir/pibrentasvir x 12-16 weeks with ribavirin for complex cases. Refer to hepatology for multidrug failures.

BCLC (Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer) staging guides HCC treatment. Stage 0 (very early): single <2cm, preserved liver function, PS 0 - treat with ablation or resection. Stage A (early): single or up to 3 nodules <=3cm, preserved function, PS 0 - resection, ablation, or transplant. Stage B (intermediate): multinodular, preserved function, PS 0 - TACE. Stage C (advanced): portal invasion or extrahepatic spread or PS 1-2 - systemic therapy. Stage D (terminal): PS 3-4 or Child-Pugh C - best supportive care.

Locoregional therapies for HCC: (1) Ablation (RFA, microwave): first-line for tumors <3cm, can be curative; consider for up to 3 nodules <=3cm each. (2) TACE (transarterial chemoembolization): intermediate stage (BCLC B), multinodular disease; contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis or main portal vein thrombosis. (3) TARE (Y-90 radioembolization): alternative to TACE, may be better for portal vein involvement. (4) SBRT: emerging role, especially if ablation/TACE not feasible.

First-line systemic therapy for advanced HCC (BCLC C): atezolizumab + bevacizumab (IMbrave150 trial) is preferred if no contraindications to bevacizumab (treat varices first, no recent bleeding). Alternative first-line: durvalumab + tremelimumab (HIMALAYA), or single-agent TKI (lenvatinib or sorafenib). Second-line after TKI: regorafenib, cabozantinib, or ramucirumab (if AFP >=400). CheckMate 040: nivolumab (accelerated approval). Requires Child-Pugh A liver function.

Liver transplant is curative for HCC within Milan criteria: single tumor <=5cm, OR up to 3 tumors each <=3cm, with no macrovascular invasion or extrahepatic spread. 5-year survival >70% within Milan. UCSF criteria (single <=6.5cm, or <=3 tumors each <=4.5cm, total diameter <=8cm) may expand eligibility with similar outcomes. Locoregional therapy used as bridge to transplant or to downstage beyond Milan. AFP >1000 associated with poor post-transplant outcomes.

CGM is recommended for all patients with type 1 diabetes. For type 2 diabetes, CGM recommended if: on intensive insulin therapy (MDI or pump), history of severe hypoglycemia, hypoglycemia unawareness, or not achieving glycemic goals. CGM improves time in range, reduces hypoglycemia, and lowers A1C. Real-time CGM with alerts preferred for hypoglycemia risk. Intermittently scanned CGM (isCGM/flash) reasonable alternative. Professional CGM useful for therapy adjustments.

Insulin pump (CSII) therapy is recommended for adults with type 1 diabetes not meeting glycemic goals on MDI, or those with hypoglycemia unawareness, extreme insulin sensitivity, or highly variable schedules. May consider in type 2 diabetes on intensive insulin therapy. Automated insulin delivery (AID) systems that integrate CGM with pump to adjust basal rates show improved outcomes. Patient must be able and willing to use technology and perform carb counting.

Initial evaluation: TSH (if low, obtain radionuclide scan - hot nodules rarely malignant), ultrasound with standardized reporting (ACR TI-RADS). FNA based on sonographic pattern and size: highly suspicious >=1cm, moderately suspicious >=1.5cm, mildly suspicious >=2.5cm. Bethesda cytology system guides management. Molecular testing (Afirma, ThyroSeq) for indeterminate cytology to avoid diagnostic surgery. Most nodules are benign; observe with repeat US in 6-12 months if benign cytology.

Treatment options for Graves disease: (1) Antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred; PTU only in first trimester pregnancy or thyroid storm): 12-18 months course, remission in 30-50%; (2) Radioactive iodine (RAI): definitive, leads to hypothyroidism requiring lifelong levothyroxine; (3) Thyroidectomy: for large goiter, suspicious nodule, severe ophthalmopathy, or patient preference. Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) for symptom control. Avoid RAI in pregnancy and active Graves ophthalmopathy.

Screen TSH in early pregnancy for high-risk women (prior thyroid disease, T1DM, family history). Overt hypothyroidism: levothyroxine to maintain TSH <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester, <3.0 in second/third. Women on levothyroxine preconception should increase dose by 30% when pregnancy confirmed. Subclinical hypothyroidism: treat if TPOAb positive. Hyperthyroidism: PTU in first trimester (teratogenicity of methimazole), then may switch to methimazole. Avoid RAI in pregnancy. Postpartum thyroiditis common.

Screen with DXA: all women >=65, all men >=70, younger adults with risk factors (prior fragility fracture, glucocorticoid use >=3 months, parental hip fracture, smoking, excess alcohol, rheumatoid arthritis, low BMI). FRAX tool calculates 10-year fracture probability. Treat if: hip or spine DXA T-score <=-2.5, hip or vertebral fracture, or T-score -1.0 to -2.5 with FRAX 10-year hip fracture >=3% or major osteoporotic fracture >=20%.

Oral bisphosphonates (alendronate 70mg weekly, risedronate 35mg weekly or 150mg monthly, ibandronate 150mg monthly) are first-line for most patients. Take on empty stomach with full glass of water, remain upright 30-60 minutes. IV zoledronic acid 5mg yearly if unable to tolerate oral, GI issues, or adherence concerns. Ensure adequate calcium (1000-1200mg/day) and vitamin D (800-2000 IU/day). Denosumab for bisphosphonate intolerance or renal impairment (eGFR <30-35).

guidelines.section_title: Osteoporosis Treatment Duration and Drug Holiday
guidelines.recommendation_text: Reassess fracture risk after 3-5 years of bisphosphonate therapy. Drug holiday (temporary discontinuation) may be considered after 5 years oral bisphosphonate or 3 years IV zoledronic acid if: no incident fracture on therapy, T-score >-2.5, and low FRAX risk. During holiday, monitor with DXA every 2-3 years; resume if significant bone loss or fracture. No drug holiday for denosumab (rapid bone loss after discontinuation - transition to bisphosphonate). Anabolic agents (teriparatide, romosozumab) for very high-risk patients.

Provoked VTE (surgery, trauma, immobility, estrogen): 3 months anticoagulation, then stop. Unprovoked VTE: at least 3 months, then consider extended anticoagulation indefinitely if bleeding risk low-moderate. High recurrence risk favors extended treatment: unprovoked proximal DVT or PE, male sex, positive D-dimer after stopping anticoagulation, residual DVT on ultrasound. Use validated bleeding risk scores (HAS-BLED, VTE-BLEED). Reduced-dose DOACs (rivaroxaban 10mg, apixaban 2.5mg BID) for extended therapy.

DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) are preferred over warfarin for most VTE: no INR monitoring, fewer drug interactions, lower intracranial bleeding. Apixaban and rivaroxaban do not require lead-in LMWH. Warfarin preferred if: severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 for most DOACs, <15 for apixaban), antiphospholipid syndrome (especially triple-positive), mechanical heart valve, or drug interactions/cost concerns. DOACs contraindicated in pregnancy (use LMWH).

For cancer-associated VTE, LMWH or DOAC preferred over warfarin. DOACs (edoxaban, rivaroxaban, apixaban) show similar efficacy to LMWH with increased bleeding in GI/GU cancers. LMWH preferred for: GI luminal cancers with intact lesion, GU cancers, high-risk GI bleeding, thrombocytopenia, drug interactions. Treat for at least 6 months; continue while cancer active or on chemotherapy. For incidental PE: treat same as symptomatic if subsegmental or larger.

For patients on anticoagulation requiring surgery: (1) Assess bleeding risk of procedure and thrombotic risk of interruption. (2) Hold warfarin 5 days before surgery (INR typically <1.5 by day 5). Bridging with LMWH only for highest thrombotic risk (mechanical mitral valve, recent VTE <3 months, severe thrombophilia). (3) DOACs: hold based on CrCl and bleeding risk - typically 24-48h for low-bleed procedures, 48-72h for high-bleed. No DOAC bridging needed. Resume 24-72h post-op based on hemostasis.

Warfarin reversal: 4-factor PCC (Kcentra) 25-50 units/kg for life-threatening bleeding or urgent surgery (faster than FFP). Vitamin K 10mg IV for sustained reversal. DOAC reversal: Idarucizumab (Praxbind) 5g IV for dabigatran. Andexanet alfa (Andexxa) for rivaroxaban/apixaban (expensive, limited). 4-factor PCC 50 units/kg reasonable alternative for factor Xa inhibitors if andexanet unavailable. Activated charcoal if DOAC ingested within 2-4 hours. Hemodialysis removes dabigatran.

Anticoagulation recommended for AF with CHA2DS2-VASc >=2 (men) or >=3 (women). DOACs preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF. No anticoagulation needed if score 0. Consider anticoagulation for score 1 (men) or 2 (women). CHA2DS2-VASc: CHF (1), HTN (1), Age>=75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Female (1). LAA occlusion (Watchman) for patients with contraindication to long-term anticoagulation.
